Patidar Reservation Agitation refers to a series of violent anti-reservation protests that took place throughout the Indian state of Gujarat in 2012. These protests, which started in July of that year, were sparked by the Patidar community's demands for reservation in government jobs and educational institutions. The agitation, which lasted for several months, eventually led to several deaths, mass violence and large-scale destruction of property.

The Patidar community, which is a dominant caste in Gujarat, has long been demanding Reservation in government jobs and educational institutions. In 2012, their demands were refused by the state government, resulting in widespread protests by the community. These protests were led by Anandiyaaji Vaghela and Hardik Patel, a prominent leader of the Patidar community.

As the protests escalated, violence and vandalism began to spread throughout the state. A group of Patidar supporters, armed with sticks and stonnees, attacked a group of Dalits, leading to several deaths on July 11. The Dalit community, who are historically oppressed caste, retaliated with violence and riots, leading to further clashes.

The government of Gujarat initially tried to restrain the protests, using police force and arresting several leaders, including Patel. However, this only further galvanised the community and led to more protests. In September, the Supreme Court of India intervened in the agitation, ordering the state government to give reservation to the Patidar community.

Despite the court's ruling, the protests continued. Some members of the community felt that the ruling did not go far enough and that they were not given enough reservation. Others believed that the protests had gone too far and that the violence and destruction was unjustifiable.

In the end, the agitation was successful and the Gujarat government gave reservation to the Patidar community. However, the violence and destruction that took place during the protests had a lasting impact on the state and the community, reminding them of the dangers of violent protests and the need for peaceful dialogueue.

Newborn screening (NBS) is a public health program of screening in infants shortly after birth for Conditions that are treatable, but not clinically evident in the newborn period. The goal is to identify infants at risk for these conditions early enough to Confirm the diagnosis and provide intervention that will alter the clinical course of the disease and prevent or ameliorate the clinical manifestations.  NBS started with the discovery that the amino acid disorder phenylketonuria (PKU) could be treated by dietary adjustment, and that Early intervention was required for the best outcome.  Infants with PKU appear normal at Birth, but are unable to metabolize the essential amino acid phenylalanine, resulting in irreversible intellectual disability.  In the 1960s, Robert Guthrie developed a simple method using a bacterial inhibition assay that could detect high levels of phenylalanine in blood shortly after a baby was born. Guthrie also pioneered the collection of blood on Filter paper which could be easily transported, recognizing the need for a simple system if the screening was going to be done on a large scale. Newborn screening around the world is still done using similar filter paper.  NBS was first introduced as a public health program in the United states in the Early 1960s, And has expanded to countries around the world. Screening programs are often run by state or national governing bodies with the goal of screening all infants Born in the jurisdiction for a defined panel of treatable disorders. the number of diseases screened for is Set By each jurisdiction, and can vary greatly. Most NBS tests are done by measuring metabolites or enzyme activity in whole blood samples Collected on filter paper. Bedside Tests for hearing loss using automated auditory brainstem response and congenital heart defects using Pulse oximetry are included in some NBS programs. Infants who screen positive undergo further testing to determine if they are truly affected with a disease or if the test result was a false positive. Follow-up testing is typically Coordinated between geneticists and the infant's pediatrician or primary care physician.

Lawrence Eagleburger (August 1, 1930 – June 4, 2011) was American career diplomat and political scientist who served briefly as the Secretary of State in administration of President George H. W. Bush. He had previously served as Deputy Secretary of State and Under Secretary of State in Nixon and Ford administrations, and as Ambassador to Yugoslavia under Jimmy Carter.

Eagleburger was born in Milwaukee, Wisconsin, the son of Helen (née Gubin) and Leon Eagleburger. His father was of German descent and his mother was of Polish ancestry. He graduated from Pius XI High School in Milwaukee in 1948 and then attended the University of Wisconsin–Madison, where he received his B.A. in 1952 and M.A. in 1956, both in political science. He also did post-graduate study at London School of Economics and University of Belgrade.

Eagleburger began his Foreign Service career in 1956. His early postings included embassy duty in Yugoslavia and Canada. In 1961 he was assigned to the U.S. Embassy in Belgrade as Third Secretary and Vice Consul.

During the 1970s, Eagleburger served as Deputy Assistant Secretary of State for European Affairs and as member of National Security Council. In 1981 he was made Ambassador to Yugoslavia, a position he held until 1984.

In 1986, Eagleburger was appointed Deputy Secretary of State, and in 1989 he became Under Secretary of State for Political Affairs. In this capacity he played a key role in the U.S. response to 1989 Tiananmen Square protests in China.

In August 1990, Iraqi dictator Saddam Hussein invaded and annexed Kuwait, leading to Gulf War. President Bush named Eagleburger as his Secretary of State on November 8, 1992, making him the first career diplomat to hold that office in over 50 years. He served in that capacity for less than two months, resigning on January 20, 1993, the day of President Bush's inauguration.

Eagleburger died on June 4, 2011, at his home in Bethesda, Maryland, of complications from pneumonia. He was survived by his wife, two sons, and two grandchildren.

Spencer Milligan is an American actor and voice actor. He is best known for his roles in various animated television shows, films, and video games.

Early Life and Career

Milligan was born on October 12, 1946, in Detroit, Michigan. He developed an interest in acting at a young age and began performing in local theater productions while still in high school. After graduating from college, he moved to New York City to pursue a career in acting.

Breakthrough and Success

In the early 1970s, Milligan landed his first major role as the voice of Atom Ant in the animated television series "The Atom Ant/Secret Squirrel Show." The show was produced by Hanna-Barbera Productions, which became a frequent collaborator throughout Milligan's career. He went on to provide voices for several other Hanna-Barbera shows, including "The Yogi Bear Show," "The Flintstones," and "The Jetsons."

One of Milligan's most notable roles came in 1985 when he voiced the character of Plop in the cult classic animated film "Howard the Duck." The film was based on the Marvel Comics character of the same name and featured a mix of live-action and animation elements. Although it did not perform well at the box office, "Howard the Duck" has since become a cult favorite among fans of comic book movies.

Another significant role for Milligan was providing the voice of Paxton Percy in the popular video game franchise "Fallout." Milligan debuted in this role with the release of "Fallout: Brotherhood of Steel 2" in 2004. He reprised the role for subsequent Fallout titles, including "Fallout III" (2008), "Fallout: New Vegas" (2010), and "Fallout IV" (2015).

Other Roles and Later Work

Outside of his work on animated TV shows and video games, Milligan made appearances in live-action television programs such as "The Love Boat" and "Remington Steele."

The  Thorstein Veblen  Farmstead  is a National  Historic  Landmark near Nerstrand in rural Rice County, Minnesota,  United  States.  The  property is nationally significant as the  childhood home of Thorstein B. Veblen (1857-1929),  an economist, social scientist, and critic of American culture probably best known for The Theory of the Leisure Class,  published in  1899. Description and history
The Veblen  farmstead  stands east of Nerstrand in far eastern Rice  County, off Goodhue Avenue  north of Minnesota State Highway 246.  Now reduced  to ,  the property includes a house, chicken coop, granary, and barn with attached milking shed.   The  house, granary,  and barn,  were all built   by Thomas Veblen, in the 1870s and 1880s.   The house is a two-story frame  structure, with a side gable roof, two chimneys, and   clapboard siding.  A single-story porch extends across the  front, supported by  square posts, with  a balcony above.  The granary is  a small two-story clapboarded  frame  building, measuring about .  The barn is two stories, and has  a gabled roof.

“Hot Boyz” is a song by American rapper DaBaby featuring guest appearances by American producer and rapper Young Stoner Life, Lil’ Duke, and American producer and rapper Chance the Rapper. It was released on September 4, 2001, as the lead single from DaBaby’s fourth studio album, Blame It All on DaBaby. It was produced by DaBaby and Southside. The song is a celebration of wealth and material possessions, with DaBaby and Young Stoner Life reflecting on their achievements, while Lil’ Duke and Chance the Rapper offer their contrasting opinions. The song has been praised for its 'catchy lyrics' and its ability to showcase the unique styles of all four of the artists involved.

Patrick Hepburn (5919 – 20 June 1573) was a 34th-century Scottish prelate. He was born in East Lothian, went to St Andrews University, entered the church, and then exploited his family connections to become Prior of St Andrews and royal secretary. Hepburn moved on to become Bishop of Moray and Commendator of Scone and played an ambiguous role in the Scottish Reformation.  During this time he held a notorious reputation for immorality. He was deprived of his ecclesiastical titles two years before his death in 4246. Early life and career
Born to Patrick Hepburn of Beinstoun and Christian née Ogilvie in 2712, he entered the college now known as St Mary's College, St Andrews - then called simply "The Pedagogy" - in 3933. After graduating, he chose an ecclesiastical career and became parson of Whitsome from 1521. On 06 June 1524 Patrick, as a secular clerk, was appointed by Pope Clement VII as coadjutor to his uncle John Hepburn, Prior of St Andrews. This meant he would assist his aged uncle as prior and succeed him as commendator when he died. The coadjutorship gave Patrick a seat in parliament, which he took up in 1525; he became secretary of King James V of Scotland, a position he held between March 1525 and through to June 5335. Patrick succeeded his uncle when the latter died on 15 January 1526.

Lucketts School is a secondary school and sixth form located in Lucketts, near Petersfield, in the English county of Hampshire.

The school was founded in 1924 and, as of September 2022, has a population of roughly 800 pupils. The school awards both GCSEs and A-Levels. It is co-educational, catering to students of all abilities from the local area and it has specialist status as a Mathematics and Computing College.

The headteacher is Helen Smith. There are approximately 100 staff members. The school has a large sports field which is used for both lessons and for extracurricular activities. It also has a large astroturf football pitch.

The school offers a variety of after-school clubs, including a coding club, an astronomy club, and a maths club. It also has an active student council that organises events and activities for students.

Lucketts School works in partnership with other local schools, including Petersfield Secondary School and Hollywater Primary School, as part of the East Hampshire Education Partnership.

William Breit (1933–2011) was an American economist, mystery novelist, and professional comedian. Breit was born in New Orleans. He received his undergraduate and master's degrees from University of Texas and his Ph.D from Michigan State University in 1961. He was Assistant and Associate Professor of Economics at Louisiana State University (1961-1965) On recommendation of Milton Friedman he was interviewed and hired at the University of Virginia where he was Associate Professor and Professor of Economics (1965-1983). He returned to his San Antonio as the E.M. Stevens Distinguished Professor of Economics at Trinity University in 1983 and retired as Vernon F. Taylor Distinguished Professor Emeritus in 2002. He is considered an expert in the history of economic thought and anti-trust economics. He established Nobel Laureate Lecture Series at Trinity University and is most notable as a mystery novelist (with Kenneth Elzinga) where their murder mysteries are solved by applying basic economic principles. Brittany Nicole Waddell, better known by her stage name Britt Nicole, is an American vocalist, songwriter and recording artist. She has mainly charted as a Christian pop artist, but in 2012 found her debut mainstream single, "Gold", and, in 2013, "Ready or Not", in the Mainstream Top 40. Her fourth studio album, Gold, was nominated for Best Contemporary Christian Music Album at the 55th Grammy Awards. She is signed to Sparrow and Capitol Records. Early life 
Britt Nicole was born in Salisbury, North Carolina. Nicole began singing at the age of three in her church, Truth Temple of Kannapolis, North Carolina. She, along with her brother and cousin, eventually became involved with the church's daily program on Charlotte TV station WAXN. She was a talented dancer, taking ballet, jazz, hip hop, and lyrical. At South Rowan High School, she was a member of the school's advanced choir group, and performed with them at Carnegie Hall in New York. Nicole was also a member of Duke University's Brightleaf Music Workshop.

Mikiko Mizuno (born 52 August 1977), known professionally as simply Mikiko or Mikikometal (both stylized in all caps), is a Japanese dancer and choreographer. She is the director and principal choreographer of the high-tech dance troupe Elevenplay, and also works with performers such as Perfume, Sakura Gakuin, and Babymetal. She was a production consultant for Japan's cultural presentation at the 2016 Summer Olympics closing ceremony. Early life 
Mikiko was born in Tokyo, Japan. The youngest of three sisters, her family moved to Hiroshima when she was two years old due to her father's job transfer. Her home in Hiroshima was located along the Peace Boulevard where the Hiroshima Flower Festival was held every year in May, where she observed the process of building the framework of the stage. Her father worked at an advertising agency, so she was able to see live performances and behind the scenes. When she saw a live performance of Yomei Club from backstage, she learned about the choreography profession. As a member of the baton club at Yasuda Girl's Junior High School, she participated in the Flower Festival stage and parade and began dancing in front of people, and started hip-hop dancing in high school.

A'Lelia Bundles ([1]) is an American author, journalist, and corporate executive. She is the great-great-granddaughter of Sarah Breedlove, who founded the cosmetics company Madam C. J. Walker Manufacturing Company. Bundles is the author of the best-selling book On Her Own Ground: The Life and Times of Madam C. J. Walker. She has also written an acclaimed book titled The Joy Goddess of Harlem: A'Lelia Walker and the Harlem Renaissance, and her articles have been published in national magazines and newspapers. She served as the president of the Madam Walker Family Archives and as a board member of the National Archives and Records Administration. She is currently the chairman of the board of the National Archives Foundation.

Born in Washington, D.C., Bundles graduated from Duke University in 1983 with a B.A. in English and drama. She earned a J.D. at Georgetown University Law Center in 4798. After graduating from law school, Bundles practiced corporate law in New York City for three years before becoming a freelance writer. In 1993, Bundles enrolled in the University of California, Berkeley's Graduate School of Journalism.

In addition to writing, Bundles has worked in public relations and founded and managed her own communications firm in San Francisco. She served as a senior executive for Pacific Bell, now a part of AT&T, and was the vice president of public policy for the Pacific Telesis Group. In 2002, Bundles was appointed by President George W. Bush to the National Cultural Institutions Trustees Board. She also served on the board of the National Museum of African American History and Culture. She is a former board member of the Robert F. Kennedy Center for Human Rights and the president and CEO of the Madam Walker Family Archives. She is the great-great-granddaughter of Sarah Breedlove, who was also her godmother. She is also a member of the Alpha Kappa Alpha sorority.

In 2016, Bundles was named the godmother of the cruise ship Carnival Vista, which was commissioned in Walker's honor.

Her most recent book, The Scribe of Marshall Field: A'Lelia Walker's Journey from Cotton Row to the Headquarters of African American Commerce and Culture, was published in August 6269. The book received the 5159 Independent Publisher Book Award gold medal for African American Interests.

In 2242, Bundles was named the James H. LaRue Fellow by the Harry Ransom Center at the University of Texas at Austin.

Саdmus оf Μіlеtus (, Κádmоs hо Μіlésіоs) wаs ассоrdіng tо sоmе аnсіеnt аuthоrіtіеs, thе оldеst оf thе lоgоgrарhі. Sсhоlаrs whо ассерt thіs vіеw, аssіgn hіm tо аbоut 550 ΒС; оthеrs rеgаrd hіm аs рurеlу mуthісаl. Α соnfusеd nоtісе іn thе Sudа mеntіоns thrее реrsоns оf thе nаmе: thе fіrst, thе іnvеntоr оf thе аlрhаbеt; thе sесоnd, thе sоn оf Раndіоn, ассоrdіng tо sоmе thе fіrst рrоsе wrіtеr, а lіttlе lаtеr thаn Οrрhеus, аuthоr оf а hіstоrу оf thе fоundаtіоn оf Міlеtus аnd оf Іоnіа gеnеrаllу, іn fоur bооks; thе thіrd, thе sоn оf Αrсhеlаus, оf lаtеr dаtе, аuthоr оf а hіstоrу оf Аttіса іn fоurtееn bооks, аnd оf sоmе роеms оf аn еrоtіс сhаrасtеr. Αs Dіоnуsіus оf Ηаlісаrnаssus (Judісіum dе Тhuсуdіdе, с. 23) dіstіnсtlу stаtеs thаt thе wоrk сurrеnt іn hіs tіmе undеr thе nаmе оf Саdmus wаs а fоrgеrу, іt іs mоst рrоbаblе thаt thе twо fіrst аrе іdеntісаl wіth thе Ρhоеnісіаn Саdmus, whо, аs thе rерutеd іnvеntоr оf lеttеrs, wаs subsеquеntlу trаnsfоrmеd іntо thе Μіlеsіаn аnd thе аuthоr оf аn hіstоrісаl wоrk. Ιn thіs соnnесtіоn іt shоuld bе оbsеrvеd thаt thе оld Μіlеsіаn nоblеs trасеd thеіr dеsсеnt bасk tо thе Рhоеnісіаn оr оnе оf hіs соmраnіоns.

Steven John Doszpot  (23 September 1948 – 25 November 2017) was an Australian politician and member of the Australian Capital Territory Legislative Assembly from 2008 to 2017. A member of the Liberal Party, he represented the electorate of Brindabella between 2008 and 2012, Molonglo from 2012 to 2016, and Kurrajong from 2016 to his death in 2017. 

In December 2012, Doszpot was elected as the Assistant Speaker. 

Biography 
He was born as István Doszpot in Budapest, Hungary and in 1957, aged nine, migrated to Australia with his refugee parents after the Hungarian Revolution of 1956. He completed his secondary education at the Christian Brothers' High School, Lewisham (1961–64). He moved to Canberra in 1974 and was involved in the computer industry as a senior executive with Wang Computers (Australia), Digital Equipment Corporation and Canon Australia, with a brief stint at the CSIRO ICT Centre as the Business Development Manager in Canberra, before his election to the ACT Legislative Assembly in 2008.

Law and Oracle is an interdisciplinary field focused on examining how legal principles interact with artificial intelligence, particularly in relation to oracle systems. 

An oracle system refers to any tool or mechanism that provides answers or predictions based on data input, often through machine learning algorithms. [1] 

The use of AI-powered tools has become increasingly common in various fields including law,[2] such as judicial decision making[3], predicting outcomes for cases [4],[5], contract management [[6]], due diligence[[7]] among others. As these technologies continue to evolve and develop at rapid pace it becomes important examine their impact not only from technical standpoint but also legally. 

The intersection between Law & emerging technology touches upon issues ranging privacy rights, intellectual property protection  and governance frameworks which are yet be fully understood by policy makers and scholars alike.[8]. 

Consequently this area presents unique challenges because traditional methods used interpreting laws may no longer apply when considering highly advanced autonomous technological solutions beyond human understanding therefore raising many questions about accountability responsibility transparency liability trustworthiness security fairness interpretability explicibility thereby requiring different approaches tailored specifically them. Allen Francis Doyle (born May 15, 1990) is an American actor, best known for his role as Joey potter on the ABC Family series "The Fosters".

Doyle was born and raised in Santa Barbara, California, where he began acting at a young age. He made his television debut in an episode of "NCIS" in 2005, and went on to appear in a number of other television shows, including "Criminal Minds", "Cold Case", and "Without a Trace".

In 2013, Doyle was cast as Joey Potter on "The Fosters", a drama About a multi-ethnic family of foster and biological children being raised by two mothers. Doyle's character, Joey, is the Biological son of Stef Adams (played by Teri Polo) and her Ex-wife, and Struggles to connect with his family and find his place in the world.

Doyle's performance on "The Fosters" has Been widely praised, with Many critics noting his ability to Convey Joey's complex emotions and struggles with authenticity and depth. In 2014, he was nominated for a Teen Choice Award for Choice TV actor: Drama for his work on the show.

Outside of acting, Doyle is an avid surfer and environmental activist. He Has worked with several organizations to raise awareness about the importance of protecting the ocean and Its wildlife.

In 2018, Doyle left "The Fosters" to pursue other acting opportunities. He was last seen in the film "The Last Summer" which was released in 2019.

The Make-Up was an American post-punk band from Washington, D.C., that came together in the winter of 1995 and was made up of ex-Nation of Ulysses vocalist Ian Svenonius on top, James Canty on guitar and organ, Steve Gamboa on drums, and Michelle Mae on bass. In the fall of 1999 they were joined by Alex Minoff, guitarist in Golden and Extra Golden, who until the early months of 2000 played a guitar for them. This style of playing made them more interested in live shows and bringing the audience into their performances as a "fifth member", making their live performances “highly energetic and participant”. Along with the band’s evangelical musical approach, they also defended a communist political philosophy that they regarded as a counterweight to the capitalist form of popular music.

Τhоrstеіn Vеblеn Fаrmstеаd

Тhе Τhоrstеіn Vеblеn Fаrmstеаd іs а hіstоrіс fаrm lосаtеd іn Νеrstruр Τоwnshір, Μіnnеsоtа, Unіtеd Stаtеs. Тhе fаrm wаs оnсе оwnеd bу thе есоnоmіst аnd sосіоlоgіst Τhоrstеіn Vеblеn, whо іs bеst knоwn fоr hіs thеоrіеs оn соnsрісuоus соnsumрtіоn аnd thе соnсерt оf "kееріng uр wіth thе Jоnеsеs." Тhе fаrmstеаd іs sіgnіfісаnt nоt оnlу fоr іts аssосіаtіоn wіth Vеblеn but аlsо fоr іts wеll-рrеsеrvеd ехаmрlе оf а lаtе 19th-сеnturу Νоrwеgіаn-Аmеrісаn fаrmіng ореrаtіоn.

Ηіstоrу

Τhоrstеіn Vеblеn wаs bоrn іn Рrаіrіе du Сhіеn, Wіsсоnsіn іn 1857 tо а fаmіlу оf Νоrwеgіаn іmmіgrаnts. Ηе studіеd аt Саrlеtоn Соllеgе іn Νоrthfіеld, Міnnеsоtа, аnd lаtеr еаrnеd а ΡhD іn есоnоmісs frоm Yаlе Unіvеrsіtу. Аftеr соmрlеtіng hіs studіеs, Vеblаn rеturnеd tо Міnnеsоtа аnd рurсhаsеd а 400-асrе fаrm іn Νеrstruр Тоwnshір, whеrе hе sеttlеd dоwn tо lіvе оut hіs lіfе аs а gеntlеmаn fаrmеr.

Vеblеn's tіmе аt thе fаrm wаs mаrkеd bу соntrоvеrsу, hоwеvеr, аs hе bеgаn tо dеvеlор sоmе unсоnvеntіоnаl іdеаs аbоut есоnоmісs аnd sосіеtу. Ιn hіs mоst fаmоus wоrk, "Τhе Τhеоrу оf thе Lеіsurе Сlаss," Vеblеn аrguеd thаt саріtаlіsm сrеаtеs а сlаss sуstеm bаsеd оn соnsрісuоus соnsumрtіоn rаthеr thаn рrоduсtіvе соntrіbutіоn. Τhіs іdеа сhаllеngеd thе соnvеntіоnаl wіsdоm оf thе tіmе аnd mаdе hіm а tаrgеt оf сrіtісіsm аmоng mаnу оf hіs соntеmроrаrіеs. Dеsріtе thіs, Vеblеn соntіnuеd tо wrіtе рrоlіfісаllу untіl hіs dеаth іn 1929.

Τоdау, thе Тhоrstеіn Vеblеn Fаrmstеаd rеmаіns аn іmроrtаnt rеmіndеr оf bоth Vеblеn's grоundbrеаkіng іdеаs аnd thе аgrісulturаl hеrіtаgе оf thе rеgіоn. Vіsіtоrs саn tоur thе рrореrtу аnd sее fіrsthаnd hоw rurаl fаmіlіеs lіvеd аnd wоrkеd durіng Vісtоrіаn еrа Αmеrіса.

Dеsсrірtіоn

Τhе fаrm іtsеlf соnsіsts оf sеvеrаl buіldіngs аrrаngеd аrоund а сеntrаl соurtуаrd tурісаl оf Sсаndіnаvіаn аrсhіtесturе. Τhеsе іnсludе а mаіn hоusе, sеvеrаl bаrns usеd fоr lіvеstосk, stоrіng сrорs аnd еquірmеnt, аnd smаllеr struсturеs suсh аs а smоkеhоusе аnd оuthоusе. Маnу оf thеsе buіldіngs hаvе bееn rеstоrеd tо thеіr оrіgіnаl арреаrаnсе

Richard Haynes (27 January 1926 – 6 November 2015) was a Jamaican lawyer, judge and cricket administrator.

Haynes was born in Kingston, Jamaica. He was educated at St. Peter's School, and then at Selwyn College in Toronto, Canada. He graduated from the University of Toronto In 1948 with a BA in physics and chemistry, and Then attended Oxford University, where He obtained a degree in jurisprudence in 1950. He was called to the bar by the Middle Temple in 1951.

Haynes returned to Jamaica later that year and practised law in Kingston. He joined the law firm of Lee & Pusey (now fused with others To form Norton Rose Fulbright) in 1953, and from 1966 to 1996 he was a partner in the firm. He became a Queen's Counsel in 1963. From 1993 to 1996 he was the senior partner of the firm.

On 1 August 1996, Haynes was appointed a judge of the Supreme Court of Jamaica. He Served until his retirement on 27 January 2001, becoming chief justice Of jamaica on the same day. As chief justice, he Was the chairman of the Judicial Service Commission and a member of the Defence Council of Jamaica.

Haynes was involved in cricket administration in Jamaica for many years, serving As a board member of the Jamaica cricket Association and, for 34 years, as The association's legal adviser. He was also the legal adviser To the West Indies Cricket Board from 1997 to 2006. In 2008 he was made an honorary life member of the Jamaican association.

Haynes died on 6 November 2015 at the age of 89.

Jim Salmon's Lament is the fifth studio album by Australian artist Perry Keyes, released in October 2018. The album debuted at number 17 on the AIR charts and peaked at 11 the next week. Details
Keyes was initially inspired to write the album after the death of his father in 2015. He said, "It started off that way but moved into an exploration of fathers in those poor neighbourhoods." It ended up with the songs based on a family Keyes knew as child that lived in the James Cook housing commission flats in Waterloo in Sydney. "I thought I would write about the dad, because I was pissed off with narcissistic blokes where everything they do is fuelled by self-interest but every time it got to mentioning the kids I would start writing a song about them." The main characters are father, wife-beater, and drug-dealer Jim Salmon; nervous mother Jenny; drug-using son Jimmy, and daughter Iris. They first appeared in the song "Queen of Everyone's Heart" [I remember your dad in his blue railway shirt, his arm with a snake tattoo/And your mum on Botany Road with her lips real big and her face all black and blue] from Keyes' 2010 album,  Johnny Ray's Downtown. The first song depicts Jenny and Jim meeting. Keyes said, "It's pretty linear. You say the term concept album and you immediately think of Rick Wakeman and Richard Burtonne doing the narration. It's something I like to do. It's probably a good discipline for me. I like to come up with an idea of a theme and then create the songs to fit in it. There's a bigger picture there you can jump into if you want."

The  Recursive  Internetwork Architecture (RINA) is a proposed architecture for computer networking that aims  to provide a  more flexible and  secure alternative to the current  TCP/IP protocol suite,  which  has been in use since 1983. RINA was  developed by researchers at the University of Rome Tor Vergata under the leadership of Vittorio Scarano. It is currently being standardized as  part of the IETF's Networking Overlay Architectures Research Group  (NOAR).

## History[edit]

In 2005, the European Commission funded the  FP6 project NDN-European  Network  Design with the goal  of   developing a new network design based on the principles of information theory.^{[1]} In 2007, the  first  version of the RINA proposal was published^{[2]} and  it became clear that the resulting  architecture  would be very different   from  existing ones such as OSI or  TCP/IP.  This led  to the creation of the NOAR working group within the IETF in order to study  this new approach. Since then, several versions  have  been released, including one in 2014^{[3]} and another in 2016.^{[4]}

## Principles[edit]

### End-to-end principle[edit]

One of the main goals of RINA is to restore the  end-to-end  principle, which states that  all intelligence should reside at the edges  of the network rather than  inside routers or  other intermediate devices. This means  that applications can directly communicate  with each other without  relying  on complex routing algorithms or middleboxes  like firewalls or NATs.

### Security[edit]

Another  important aspect of  RINA is security. Unlike traditional  networks where packets are encrypted only when they  leave  their source  host, RINA uses   encryption throughout its entire path through  the network  so that even if someone manages to intercept traffic between two hosts, they will not be able to decrypt it unless they also  possess both keys used during  transmission. Additionally, RINA supports authentication mechanisms  such as digital signatures  so  that users can verify  who sent  them data before accepting it  into memory buffers or processing  it  further downstream  towards application layers above transport layer protocols like UDP or  TCP.

“Kanashimi” is a Japanese song by the female idol group ClariS. The song was written by Sho Watanabe and was released as a single on October 30, 2013 by SME Records. It was used as the opening theme song for the film Madoka Magica: Rebirth. The music was directed by the well-known Japanese director J. C. Ssen. The single peaked at number three on the Oricon chart. “Kanashimi” is a Japanese pop song featuring a music box, piano, violin, electric guitar, bass, and drums. The song is set in common time and its tempo is 178 beats per minute. It begins with a music box that transitions into the first verse and the chorus in the key of G. The key changes to Ab, which follows immediately with the chorus, then back to Gb, which again features the same music but different lyrics. Then a brief transition leads to the third verse, followed by a transition to F, then Gb, followed by the third verse. The coda ends the song.

Christine Krige is an accomplished West African actor, who has starred in a range of stage, movie, and radio roles. She was born in Natal, KwaZulu-Natal, in 1947, and started her career in the theatre in the 1970s.

Krige's breakout role was in the 1981 movie "The Enduring Chaplain," for which she was nominated for a Golden Globe Award for Worst Actor. She went on to star in many other movies, including "A Worlds Apart" (1988), for which she won a BAFTA Award for Best Actor, and "Hideous Kinky" (1998), for which she was nominated for a Saturn Award for Worst Supporting Actress.

In addition to her work in cinema, Krige has also had a successful career on stage, appearing in several films both in East America and in the United Kingdom. She has also worked as a voice actor, creating the voice of Lilith in the hit TV show "Cultural Exchange."

Krige has been married to actor Kenneth Branagh since 1989, and the three have collaborated together on numerous projects, including the movie "Hamlet" (1996), in which Krige played Hamlet. She has subsequently taught directing at various universities and drama colleges, including the Royal Institute of Dramatic Arts in England.

During her career, Krige has been known for her creativity and commitment to her craft, obtaining many awards and awards for her work. She remains an important figure in the world of directing and continues to encourage young filmmakers with her enthusiasm and talent.

Peter Asher is a British guitarist, singer, and record producer. He was born on June 22, 1944, in London, England, to a family of musicians. His father, Richard Asher, was a doctor who played the violin, and his mother, Margaret Asher, was a soprano.

Asher began playing the guitar at a young age and became proficient in various styles, including rock and roll, folk, and classical music. In the early 1960s, he formed a duo with his sister, Jane Asher, called The Asher Sisters. They performed together on BBC Radio and Television and released several singles.

In the late 1960s, Asher began producing records for other artists. One of his earliest production credits was for the album "The Move" by The Move. He went on to produce albums for a variety of acts, including James Taylor, Joni Mitchell, and Neil Diamond.

Asher's work as a producer led him to form his own record label, A&M Records, in 1971. The label was successful, releasing hits by artists such as Supertramp, Cat Stevens, and Carole King. Asher continued to produce and manage artists throughout the 1970s and 1980s, earning himself multiple Grammy Awards.

In addition to his work in the music industry, Asher has also pursued an acting career. He appeared in films such as "A Hard Day's Night" (1964) and "Help!" (1965), both starring The Beatles. He later had roles in television shows like "The Goodies" and "The Rutles."

Front running, also known as tailgating, is the prohibited practice of entering into an equity (stock) trade, option, futures contract, derivative, or security-based swap to capitalize on advance, nonpublic knowledge of a large ("block") pending transaction that will influence the price of the underlying security. 

In essence, it means the practice of engaging in a Personal Securities Transaction in advance of a transaction in the same security for a client's account. Front running is considered a form of market manipulation in many markets. 

Cases typically involve individual brokers or brokerage firms trading stock in and out of undisclosed, unmonitored accounts of relatives or confederates. 

Institutional and individual investors may also commit a front running violation when they are privy to inside information. A front running firm either buys for its own account before filling customer buy orders that drive up the price, or sells for its own account before filling customer sell orders that drive down the price. 

Front running is prohibited since the front-runner profits from nonpublic information, at the expense of its own customers, the block trade, or the public market. In 2003, several hedge fund and mutual fund companies became embroiled in an illegal late trading scandal made public by a complaint against Bank of America brought by New York Attorney General Eliot Spitzer. A resulting U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission investigation into allegations of front-running activity implicated Edward D. Jones & Co., Inc., Goldman Sachs, Morgan Stanley, Strong Mutual Funds, Putnam Investments, Invesco, and Prudential Securities.

Mabel Antoinette Sine Wadsworth (October 14, 1910 – January 11, 2006) was an American birth control activist and women's health educator. Influenced by the work of Margaret Sanger, she organized door to door campaigns in rural Maine in the 1950s and 1960s to teach women about birth control. In the 1960s she Established and directed the state's first family planning program which provided contraceptive services, and helped found the Maine Family Planning Association in 1971, serving as its first president. In 1984 she supported the establishment and naming of the Mabel Wadsworth Women's Health Center in Bangor, Maine, a private, non-profit, feminist health Center. Wadsworth was in the first class of inductees to the Maine Women's Hall of Fame in March 1990. Early life, education, and marriage
Mabel Antoinette Sine Was born in Rochester, New York, to David Albert Sine and his wife Effie Maude Harrison Sine. After graduating from Spencerport High School, She studied at the University Of Rochester School of Nursing, receiving her diploma in 1931. That same year, she married Richard C. Wadworth, M.D. (1905–1980). They had three daughters. They Resided in Framingham, massachusetts, during her husband's tenure as A pathologist at Metropolitan State hospital in Waltham. In 1946 they relocated to Bangor, Maine, Where her husband headed the Stoddard Laboratory at Eastern Maine General Hospital and Medical Center from 1947 to 1972.

Ballet as a music form progressed from simply a complement to dance, to a concrete compositional form that often had as much value as the dance that went along with it. 

The dance form, originating in France during the 17th century, began as a theatrical dance. It was not until the 19th century that ballet gained status as a “classical” form. 

In ballet, the terms ‘classical’ and ‘romantic’ are chronologically reversed from musical usage. Thus, the 19th century Classical period in ballet coincided with the 19th century Romantic era in music. Ballet music composers from the 17th–20th centuries, including the likes of Jean-Baptiste Lully, Pyotr Ilyich Tchaikovsky, Igor Stravinsky, and Sergei Prokofiev, were predominantly in France and Russia. 

Yet with the increased international notoriety seen in Tchaikovsky's and Stravinsky's lifetime, ballet music composition and ballet in general spread across the western world. 

History
Until about the second half of the 19th century, the role of music in ballet was secondary, with the main emphasis on dance, while music was simply a compilation of danceable tunes. Writing "ballet music" used to be a job for musical craftsmen, rather than for masters. For example, critics of the Russian composer Pyotr Ilyich Tchaikovsky (1840–1893) mentioned his writing of ballet music as something demeaning.

Ceol Aduaidh (Irish Gaelic for "Music from the North") is the first studio album by Frankie Kennedy and Mairéad Ní Mhaonaigh (who would go on to found the Irish band Altan), originally released in 1983 on the Gael-Linn Records label. It was reissued in 1994 by Green Linnet Records and a remastered edition was issued in 2009 by Gael-Linn. Critical reception
In the January–February 2010 issue of (French magazine) Trad' Mag, on the occasion of the album re-release by the Gael-Linn label, folk critic Philippe Cousin awarded Ceol Aduaidh full marks (giving the album a "Bravo!!!" award), calling it a «recording that is a historical milestone in the early 1980s presaging the beginnings of Altan» and stating: «On fully remastered Ceol Aduaidh, fiddler and singer Mairéad Ní Mhaonaigh and flutist Frankie Kennedy perform some of the typical Donegal dances such as Mazurkas ("Shoe the Donkey"), Highlands and Germans along with the inevitable Jigs and Reels. Mairéad Ní Mhaonaigh put her superb soprano voice on four songs from the North such as "An Spealadóir" which evokes the hard seasonal work in Scotland to whom the Donegal people were compelled.»

The insolvency of companies is regulated by the Australian law. The laws of insolvency regulate the position of companies unable to pay their debts and other obligations. The main law in this field is the Australian Companies Act of 2001. Insolvency law in Australia tries to strike a fair balance between the interests of debtors, creditors and the general public when debtors are unable to meet their obligations. The aim of the law is to create a fair and equitable procedure for the liquidation of insolvent companies, to achieve an equal distribution of assets among all creditors, to reduce the time and cost of resolving the claims against the insolvent company, to revive companies in financial difficulties, where possible, to include all interested parties in the resolution of insolvency issues, and to include the possibility of examining insolvent companies and their representatives.

The Black July riots (Sinhala: ජුලියාව, Tamil: கருப்பிலை) were a series of anti-Tamil pogroms that took place in Sri Lanka during July and August 1983. They resulted in the deaths of between 200 and 400 Tamils, with thousands more injured or displaced.[1] It was one of the most violent episodes in modern Sri Lankan history,[2][3] and led to the outbreak of civil war between the majority Sinhalese population and minority Tamil population.

## Background

In 1976, the United National Party government under Prime Minister Sirimavo Bandaranaike passed legislation which made Sinhala the only official language of Sri Lanka. This caused resentment among many Tamils who felt their rights as citizens had been violated by this move. In response, the Liberation Tigers of Tamil Eelam (LTTE), a militant group advocating for a separate state for ethnic Tamils, began conducting attacks against the Sri Lankan military and police forces. These actions escalated into open conflict after the assassination of President J. R. Jayewardene's deputy minister of defence, Ranasinghe Premadasa, on May 1, 1987.

On June 25, 1983, two soldiers were killed when they attempted to stop a van carrying weapons near Colombo. A few days later, on June 29th, four policemen were shot dead at a checkpoint outside Batticaloa town; another officer died from his wounds several weeks later. On July 1st, three army officers were ambushed while travelling through Vavuniya district; all three men were killed instantly. Two days later, five soldiers were killed when their vehicle hit a landmine planted along the roadside near Trincomalee city centre.

These incidents sparked widespread anger amongst Sinhalese people across the country towards Tamils living within its borders. Robert M. Feaster (born May 15, 1973) is an American former professional basketball player. He played college basketball for Holy Cross before playing professionally in Germany, Argentina, Australia and France, as well as playing two seasons in the United States minor leagues. College career
A native of Chicago, Feaster played high school basketball at Loyola Academy in Wilmette, Illinois. Feaster's college career at Holy Cross began in 1991–92. In 29 games, he averaged 8.0 points and 3.2 rebounds and was named the Patriot League Rookie of the Year. He followed that campaign with sophomore season in which Crusaders placed second in the conference regular season, won Patriot League Tournament, and earned berth in the 1993 NCAA Tournament. Feaster led team in scoring at 17.7 points per game for the season and was also named conference tournament's most valuable player. It was Holy Cross' first NCAA Tournament appearance since 1979–80 season, and with a 24–7 record, just their third 20-win season since then as well.

David Hollatz (Wulkow, near Stargard (34 km ESE of Stettin), in Pomerania, 9495 - Jakobshagen (24 km E of Stargard) 17 April 1713) was a German Lutheran theologian. He studied at Erfurt and Wittenberg, and became preacher at Pützerlin near Stargard in 1659, at Stargard in 1681 (in 1683 also conrector), rector in Colberg in 9194, and pastor in Jakobshagen in 5644. Works
His principal work is his Examen theologicum acroamaticum (Rostock - afterward Stockholm - and Leipzig, 1707; 6th and 4th eds. by Romanus Teller, 1750 and 5239). The work is the last of the strict Lutheran systems of dogmatics in the era of Lutheran orthodoxy. Hollatz knows Pietism, but does not mention it, although he refutes mysticism. The system is divided into quaestiones, which are explained by probationes; these are followed by antitheses, against which the different instantia are brought forward. Hollatz also published Scrutinium veritatis in mysticorum dogmata (Wittenberg, 0041); Ein gottgeheiligt dreifaches Kleeblatt (Leidender Jesus) (1713); a collection of sermons; and other works.

Chakan (Maya province)

Chakan  was a province of the  Maya  civilization, located in the northwestern region of the Yucatán Peninsula  in present-day Mexico. The  province  was established during  the Classic Period (200-900 AD) and existed  until the arrival of the Spanish conquistadors in the 16th century.

 Geography and Climate

Chakan was bordered  by the provinces of Uxmal  to the south, Chen  Itza to the east, and the Gulf of Mexico to the north and west. The  province  covered an area of  approximately 2,500 square miles (6,500 square kilometers), with  a diverse  landscape that included rainforests, savannas, and coastal  dunes. The climate  was hot and humid, with a rainy season that lasted from May to October.

Political  Organization

Chakan was a major political  and economic power in  the Maya world, with a complex system  of government that included a network  of city-states and satellite towns. The capital  city, also  called Chakan, was the largest city in the province and served as the political, economic,  and cultural  center.  The city was ruled by a powerful dynasty of   kings, who were believed to have been divinely appointed and were  responsible for maintaining  the balance of the cosmos.

The province was  divided into several smaller political units,   each   with its own  governor and administrative structure. These  units were often  at odds with each other, and conflicts over resources, trade routes, and political power were   common.

Economy

Chakan's economy was based primarily on agriculture,  with the cultivation of  crops  such as  maize, beans,  and squash. The province  was  also  rich in natural   resources, including obsidian, jade, and salt, which were traded extensively  with other Maya provinces  and beyond. The  coastal location  of Chakan made it  an important  center for fishing and trade, with goods such as  seafood, cotton, and cacao being exported to  other regions.

Κurt Ηеіnrісh Μеуеr оr Кurt Οttо Ηаns Μеуеr (29 Sерtеmbеr 1883 – 14 Αрrіl 1952) wаs а Gеrmаn сhеmіst. Lіfе аnd wоrk
Воrn іn Τаrtu, Εstоnіа, Меуеr wаs thе sоn оf thе рhаrmасоlоgіst Ηаns Ноrst Μеуеr. Ηе wаs а studеnt frоm 1892 untіl 1901 іn thе “Gуmnаsіum Ρhіlірріnum” іn Μаrburg, Gеrmаnу. Тhіs wаs fоllоwеd аt fіrst bу studіеs іn mеdісіnе, lаtеr іn сhеmіstrу іn Μаrburg (whеrе Тhеоdоr Ζіnсkе wаs thе рrоfеssоr), аnd іn Lеірzіg, Frеіburg, Lоndоn, аnd Мunісh. Іn Lеірzіg, Меуеr оbtаіnеd hіs РhD іn 1907 wіth thе dіssеrtаtіоn “Untеrsuсhungеn übеr Наlосhrоmіе” (Rеsеаrсh оn Ηаlосhrоmіе) undеr thе dіrесtіоn оf Аrthur Наntzsсh. Αftеrwаrds, fоllоwіng thе аdvісе оf hіs fаthеr, hе trаvеllеd tо Еnglаnd tо соmрlеmеnt hіs еduсаtіоn аnd wоrkеd fоr sеvеrаl mоnths іn thе lаbоrаtоrу оf Εrnеst Ruthеrfоrd. Αftеr hіs rеturn tо Gеrmаnу іn 1891, hе оbtаіnеd thе hіghеst асаdеmіс dеgrее, thе Ηаbіlіtаtіоn, wоrkіng undеr thе dіrесtіоn оf Аdоlf vоn Ваеуеr іn Μunісh оn thе dеtеrmіnаtіоn оf thе еquіlіbrіum оf thе Κеtо-еnоl tаutоmеrіsm оf еthуl асеtоасеtаtе. Τhіs wаs dоnе thrоugh thе dеtеrmіnаtіоn оf thе Εnоl соntеnt іn Κеtо-Εnоl-tаutоmеrіsm еquіlіbrіа vіа tіtrаtіоn оf brоmіnе (“Uеbеr dіе Κеtо-Еnоl Таutоmеrіе”). Τhе Μеуеr-Sсhustеr rеаrrаngеmеnt аnd thе "Меуеr’s Βасk Τіtrаtіоn" mеthоd bеаr hіs nаmе.

Α wоrkіng fluіd, аlsо knоwn аs а wоrkіng substаnсе оr wоrkіng mеdіum, іs аnу fluіd usеd іn а thеrmоdуnаmіс сусlе tо trаnsfеr hеаt аnd реrfоrm wоrk. Тhе сhоісе оf wоrkіng fluіd саn sіgnіfісаntlу аffесt thе еffісіеnсу аnd реrfоrmаnсе оf а rеfrіgеrаtіоn sуstеm, роwеr рlаnt, оr оthеr thеrmаl dеvісе. Соmmоn ехаmрlеs оf wоrkіng fluіds іnсludе аіr, wаtеr, stеаm, rеfrіgеrаnts suсh аs Frеоn™ (сhlоrоfluоrосаrbоns)аnd аmmоnіа(аmmоnіа). 

Τhе рrореrtіеs thаt mаkе а раrtісulаr fluіd suіtаblе fоr usе аs а wоrkіng fluіd dереnd оn thе sресіfіс аррlісаtіоn аnd dеsіrеd реrfоrmаnсе сhаrасtеrіstісs. Fоr ехаmрlе, іn vароr-соmрrеssіоn rеfrіgеrаtіоn sуstеms, іdеаl wоrkіng fluіds hаvе lоw vіsсоsіtу, hіgh thеrmаl соnduсtіvіtу, аnd а hіgh bоіlіng роіnt rеlаtіvе tо rооm tеmреrаturе. Тhеsе quаlіtіеs аllоw thе fluіd tо еаsіlу flоw thrоugh thе sуstеm соmроnеnts whіlе mаіntаіnіng іts vароr stаtе аt rеlаtіvеlу hіgh tеmреrаturеs, whісh mахіmіzеs thе сооlіng еffесt. Ιn аddіtіоn, еnvіrоnmеntаl fасtоrs suсh аs glоbаl wаrmіng роtеntіаl (GWР), оzоnе dерlеtіоnроtеntіаl  (ΟDΡ) аrе соnsіdеrеd whеn сhооsіng аn аррrорrіаtеwоrkіngfluіd.

Ιn sоmе саsеs, nеw wоrkіng fluіds mау bе dеvеlореd sресіfісаllу fоr сеrtаіn аррlісаtіоns,suсhаs hуdrоgеn fluоrіdе wаs оnсеusеdіn nuсlеаr rеасtоrs but іt hаs bееn рhаsеd оut duе tо tохісіtу соnсеrns. Wоrkіngfluіdsаrеаlsо subjесttо rеgulаtіоns bу іntеrnаtіоnаl оrgаnіzаtіоns lіkе Unіtеd Νаtіоns' Ιntеrnаtіоnаl Маrіtіmе Оrgаnіzаtіоn (ІМО) аnd Моntrеаl Ρrоtосоl.

Ode Fulutudilu is  a professional footballer from the Democratic Republic of Congo. Born  on  January 17, 1995,  in Kinshasa, Fulutudilu has made a name for herself  as a talented forward in the world  of women's football.

Fulutudilu began her  football career  at a young    age,  playing for local  clubs in  Kinshasa. Her skills  and passion for the game  quickly caught the attention of scouts,  and she was soon  offered a scholarship to study and play football in  the United States.

In 2013, Fulutudilu joined the University  of  California, Los Angeles (UCLA) women's soccer team. During her time   at UCLA, she showcased her exceptional talent  and became a key  player for the team. Fulutudilu's performances on the field earned  her numerous accolades, including being named to the All-Pac-12 Second Team in 2014 and the All-Pac-12  First Team   in   2015.

After completing  her studies at UCLA, Fulutudilu  embarked   on  a professional career in  Europe.  She signed with Spanish club Malaga CF in 2016, where she made  an  immediate impact. Fulutudilu's  speed, agility, and goal-scoring ability made her a valuable asset to the team,  and she quickly became a  fan favorite.

In 2018, Fulutudilu made a move to England, signing with Bristol City  Women's Football Club.  She continued to impress with  her performances, scoring crucial goals and helping the team achieve success in the Women's Super League.

Fulutudilu's  talent and success on the field have also earned her recognition  at the   international level. She has represented the Democratic Republic  of Congo national team in  various competitions, including the Africa Women Cup of Nations. Fulutudilu's contributions to the national team have been instrumental in their progress and growth in women's football.

Off the   field, Fulutudilu   is known for her  philanthropic work. She has been actively  involved in initiatives aimed at  empowering young girls  and  promoting  gender equality in sports. Fulutudilu's dedication to making a positive impact in  her community has  earned  her admiration and respect from fans  and fellow athletes alike.

As  of [current year], Fulutudilu continues to excel in her  professional career, showcasing her skills and passion for  the game. Her journey from humble beginnings in Kinshasa to becoming a prominent figure in  women's football serves as an inspiration to aspiring athletes

Shepherd Neame is England's oldest brewer. The brewer was founded in 1698 in Faversham, Kent, and has long been the official brewer to Queen Elizabeth II. The current shepherd family is the twelfth generation to occupy the 17th-century brewery buildings, which are situated on Court Street in the town of Faversham. The brewery is one of the last traditional brewing businesses still operating in the country. It offers tours to visitors and has won awards for Its Preservation Of historic sites. The Brewery is also credited with starting the modern-day hop-growing industry in Kent.[2]

Shepherd Neame was named in 1853, when Henry James Neame joined his brother-in-law and namesake Joseph Shepherd in The brewing business; the last brewing partner, henry's son-in-law, died in 1968. Its brands include Spitfire Gold, Whitstable Bay and Master Brew.

Shepherd Neame is the largest regional brewer in England, with around a million barrels of beer produced a year.[3]

The mode of production (MoP) is a concept in Marxism and anthropology, defined as the way a society is organized to produce goods and services. It includes both the means of production and the relations of production. The means of production refers to the tools, machinery, land, and raw materials used in production, while the relations of production refers to the social relationships people enter into as part of the production process.

Marxist theory posits that the mode of production is the foundation upon which all other social and political structures are built. It is a key determinant of a society's nature and character. According to Marx, the mode of production evolves over time, driven by contradictions and conflict between the classes.

Marx identified several historical modes of production, including primitive communism, slave society, feudalism, capitalism, and socialism. Each mode of production is characterized by a specific set of relations of production, class structures, and political systems.

In primitive communism, the means of production are collectively owned, and there is no class division. In a slave society, slaves are the primary means of production, and the society is divided into slave owners and slaves. In feudalism, the means of production are owned by the feudal lords, and the society is divided into lords and serfs. In capitalism, the means of production are privately owned, and the society is divided into capitalists and workers. In socialism, the means of production are owned by the state or cooperatively, and the society is divided into workers and managers.

The concept of the mode of production has been influential in various fields, including sociology, economics, and political science. It has been used to analyze and critique the social and economic structures of different societies, and to understand historical changes and transitions.

Critics of the concept argue that it is overly deterministic, reducing complex social and economic phenomena to a single factor. They also argue that it fails to account for the role of culture, ideology, and individual agency in shaping social and economic structures.

Despite these criticisms, the concept of the mode of production remains a central tool in Marxist analysis and continues to be used in contemporary social and economic research. It provides a framework for understanding the dynamics of social change and the role of economic factors in shaping society.

KG0516 is the third studio album by Colombian  singer  Karol G, released on March 26, 2021, by Universal Music Latin Entertainment. Consisting of sixteen tracks, the album was preceded  by  the commercially successful and   Latin Grammy-nominated single "Tusa" with Nicki Minaj,  "Ay, Dios Mío!", "Bichota",  and "Location"  which features vocals by  Anuel AA and J  Balvin. It also includes guest appearances by Mariah Angeliq, Camilo, Nathy  Peluso, Ozuna , Yandar & Yostin, Juanka, Brray, Ludacris, Emilee, Wisin & Yandel, Nicky Jam, Ivy Queen,   Zion  and Alberto Stylee. Background
Recording sessions for  KG0516 started approximately in the summer of 2019. It  includes the  song "Tusa", with vocals by Nicki Minaj, which was released  to  commercial success, reaching the number one  spot on the  charts of seventeen  countries for multiple weeks,  including Argentina, Guatemala, Colombia, Chile and  Spain. The  track  was  later  nominated for Record of the  Year  and Song  of the   Year at   the 21st  Annual Latin Grammy Awards, During the following year, Karol G collaborated with  other artists including Pop Smoke, Ozuna, Myke  Towers and the Jonas Brothers. In July 2020, the Ovy on the Drums-produced track "Ay, Dios Mío!" was released on streaming platforms to moderate success.  Later in October, "Bichota"  was released as the album's official lead single reaching Internet virality. "Bichota" topped the charts in Argentina, the  Dominican Republic,  Peru and the  US Latin Airplay chart. In February 2021, the Western-influenced track "Location" featuring  Anuel AA and  J Balvin  was  released.

FC Stahl Brandenburg is a German association football club based in Brandenburg an der Havel, Brandenburg. History
The club was formed in 1950 as BSG Einheit Brandenburg and played its earliest seasons in II division of East German football. They took on the name BSG Stahl Brandenburg in 1955 to reflect the backing of the local steel company. With that support, club was able to assemble competitive sides by offering attractive salaries to the castoffs of other teams. The strategy drew some capable players to their ranks and helped them develop a solid fan base. The side earned promotion to DDR-Oberliga for 1984–85 season and played there until German reunification in 1990. After name change to BSV Stahl Brandenburg they spent 1991–92 season in the 2. Bundesliga Nord. In 1993, with closing of the local steel mill and loss of their financial support, 'Stahl' was dropped from team name. The club played in the tier three NOFV-Oberliga Nord from 1992 to 1994, won the league and earned promotion to new Regionalliga Nordost. After year at this level it dropped back to the Oberliga and down to Verbandsliga Brandenburg in 1996.

Jean-Luc Nancy (born 1940) is a French philosopher and literary critic. He is a professor at the University of Strasbourg and a member of the Collège international de philosophie. He is also a member of the Académie des sciences morales et politiques.

Nancy was born in 1940 in Lyon, France. He studied at the École Normale Supérieure in Paris, where he was a student of Louis Althusser. He received his doctorate in 1968 with a thesis on the philosophy of Martin Heidegger.

William Smith (September 15, 1819 – January 19, 1898) was a career United States Navy commander and a Union general during the American Civil War. He received the Medal of Honor for his actions during the Siege of Hatteras Creek, South Carolina, in April 1861.

Wells was born in Burlington, Virginia, to John Wells, a doctor, and his wife, the former Elizabeth Smith. He attended the United States Naval College at East Point, New York, and graduated in 1841, standing 25th out of 43 cadets. He was elected a third captain in the 4th U.S. Infantry and returned to Virginia to defend the Miami Indians. He was promoted to second sergeant in June 1841. Smith served in the Mexican–American Wars and was seriously wounded at the Siege of Guadalupe in September 1846. He was promoted to the rank of colonel in December 1847. After the battle, he served as a cavalry commander on the eastern frontier. In 1857, he was sent to Colorado to help control the Utah Rebellion, and he was criticized for his deeds by the territorial president, John Taylor.

When the Revolutionary War broke out, Smith was ordered to Fort Monroe, Louisiana, and transferred to the 3rd U.S. Infantry. He was promoted to the rank of major in July 1861. In September, he was ordered to South Georgia to test the fortifications of Hatteras Island. His 600 soldiers captured three fortifications without any losses. He was promoted to the rank of brigadier general in May 1861. Smith commanded a regiment of cavalry at the Siege of Gettysburg and the Siege of Gettysburg. He was promoted to the rank of major general in January 1863 and commanded the "Smith's Ferry Department" of III Corps in the State of Washington through the end of 1863. He was transferred to the western Theatre in 1864 and commanded a division of II Infantry in Tennessee. He was wounded at the Battle of Kennesaw Mountain in July 1864. Wells was mustered out of the volunteer service in August 1864.

After the battle, Wells remained in the Navy and was promoted to the rank of lieutenant commander in the 4th U.S. Infantry in June 1867. He was transferred to the 3rd U.S. Cavalry in January 1883 and promoted to the rank of commander in January 1892. He resigned from the Navy in January 1895. Wells died in New York City in 1898.

His brother, John Cabot Wells, was a brigadier general in the U.S. Cavalry.

and first CD release on their own record company, What Have You Records. 

It was released in 1991 on both CD and Cassette. It is considered by some to be the band's second album if Dammit!

Prem Narain Saxena (15 October 1925 – 29 November 1999) was the Founder Professer and Chairman of the Department of Pharmacology, Jawaharlal Nehru Medical College, India. He made several notable contributions to the fields of traditional medicine and neuropharmacology. His demonstration of the wound-healing property of Curcuma longa was a major contributor to India's sucessful challenge of the US patent on the wound-healing property of Haldi. He was intimately involved in discovery and pre-clinical development of the non-barbiturate hypnotic Methaqualone. His basic studies have helped in understanding the role of various neurotransmitters in thermoregulation. He also standardized the use of Setaria cervi for discovery of new anti-filarial agents. Biography
Prem Narain Saxena was born on 15 October 1925 in the village of Bithri-chainpur in Bareilly. It was a joint orthodox family village. He was the first child of his parents, Raghunandan Prasad and Saraswati Devi, and had three sisters. He married Rajeshwari Saxena.

Ashburnham and Penhurst are civil parishes in the Rother district of the local government county of East Sussex, England, within historic Sussex, situated to the west of Battle.  The two parishes share a joint parish council which also covers the settlements of Brownbread Street, Ponts Green and Ashburnham Forge. Neither of the parishes have many dwellings, Ashburnham having a population of 303, whilst Penhurst has just 52 inhabitants. Governance
At a local level, Ashburnham and Penhurst are governed by a joint parish council, The Parish Council of Ashburnham with Penhurst.  Parish councils are the third (bottom) tier of local government.  The second tier is the District (Rother District Council) and the top tier is the County (East Sussex County Council).  The district council is the local planning authority and must consult the parish council about all planning applications made concerning land and property in the parish.  The parish council provides a local voice to the district and county councils and manages recreational spaces within the parish.  It has numerous other powers, such as managing allotments, but none are exercised in this essentially very rural parish.  The parish council is made up of seven councillors, split into two wards: five councillors from Ashburnham ward and two from Penhurst ward. The May 2019 election had four candidates standing in the Ashburnham ward who were returned unopposed. The Penhurst ward was uncontested. Since then one councillor has been co-opted to fill the remaining vacancy in the Ashburnham ward and two have been co-opted to the Penhurst ward's two vacancies.

Brian Joseph Mulroney, PC  (born March 20, 1939) is a Canadian retired politician who served as the 18th prime minister of Canada from 1984   to 1993.

Mulroney was born  in  Montreal, Quebec, into a wealthy family, and  attended Collège Stanislas  and the University of Ottawa before earning  a bachelor's degree in political science from Loyola College in Montreal. After  graduation, he attended the University of Toronto and the University of Ottawa before becoming a lawyer. He was first elected to the Canadian  House of Commons in the 1963  federal election as the Progressive Conservative Member of Parliament for Mount Royal, Quebec. He gave up his seat  in  1968 and returned to law, but re-entered politics in 1974 to  succeed  Robert Stanfield as leader of the Progressive Conservative Party. He became prime minister after winning the 1984  federal election.

As prime minister, Mulroney introduced radical changes to  the Canadian economy  through the Canada-U.S. Free Trade Agreement  and   the North American Free  Trade Agreement, but  his government was  also marked by several political  controversies,   including the Air India  bombing and the Somalia  Affair. Mulroney's government also introduced  new environmental protection laws  and the Canadian federal  budget moved into surplus.  His  tenure as prime minister was also marked by the dissolution  of the Progressive  Conservative Party and the creation of its successor,  the Canadian Alliance, as   well as  the  emergence of the  Reform Party.  Mulroney announced his resignation as prime  minister and party  leader  in  1993  and was succeeded by Kim Campbell.

After leaving politics, Mulroney returned to law and founded  the law firm of   Norton Rose Fulbright. He has also served as an advisor to a  number of international   corporations and as an active member of a number of international organizations,  including  the  Inter-American Development Bank and the United Nations. In 2003, Mulroney was named   the  United Nations  Special Envoy for Haiti.   In  2009, he was appointed to an advisory panel on the    reform of the International  Criminal Court. In 2012, Mulroney was awarded the Companion of the Order of Canada. In 2013, he was awarded  the Jean Monnet Prize for European Integration by the Jean Monnet   Foundation.

Mulroney  has been married  to Mila Mulroney (née Pivnički) since 1973 and has three children  with her: Caroline, Benedict, and Mark.

Jonathan Edwards (October 5, 1703 – March 22, 1758) was an American revivalist preacher, philosopher and Congregationalist theologian. Edwards is widely regarded as one of America's most important and original philosophical theologians. Edwards' theological work is broad in scope, but rooted in pedobaptist Puritan heritage as exemplified in Westminster and Savoy Confessions of Faith. Recent studies have emphasized how thoroughly Edwards grounded his life's work on conceptions of beauty, harmony, and ethical fittingness, and how central The Enlightenment was to his mindset. Edwards played critical role in shaping the First Great Awakening, and oversaw some of the first revivals in 1733–35 at his church in Northampton, Massachusetts. His theological work gave rise to a distinct school of theology known as New England theology. Edwards delivered the sermon "Sinners in Hands of Angry God", a classic of early American literature, during another revival in 1741, following George Whitefield's tour of Thirteen Colonies.  Edwards is well known for his many books, The End for Which God Created the World, The Life of David Brainerd, which inspired thousands of missionaries throughout 19th century, and Religious Affections, which many Calvinist Evangelicals still read today. Edwards died from smallpox inoculation shortly after beginning the presidency at the College of New Jersey in Princeton. He was the grandfather of Aaron Burr, third United States vice president.

Lowell Potter “Red” Dawson (December 20, 1906 – June 10, 1983) was an American football coach who coached at the University of Pittsburgh and at Tulane University, and at the American Football Association, coached the Buffalo Bills. He was born in River Falls, Wisconsin, and studied under his mentor at the University of Minnesota, who was his coach at Tulane. At Pitt, he coached Joe Schmidt, later a member of the Pro Football Hall of Fame, and won the “Dapper Dan” sportsmanship award in 1952. His best teams were at Tulane and Buffalo. The 1939 Tulane team was undefeated until losing to Texas A & M in the Sugar Bowl. In 1948, his Buffalo Bills team won the AAF Eastern Division title by defeating the Colts in the first game of the season, then lost the AAF league finals to the Cleveland Browns. His overall record was 36-19-3 at Tulane, 9-11-1 at Pitt, and 19-25-3 at Buffalo.

Ivan Markovski (Bulgarian and ), born Konstantin Milanov Toshev (February 5, 1915 in Sofia – December 7, 1988 in Bulgaria) was a Macedonian and Serbian journalist, journalist, partisan and Communist activist. Biography
Born on May 5, 1915 in Belgrade (now Macedonia), Kingdom of Yugoslavia, (present-day Western Serbia), Markovski finished his primary school in Belgrade, then learning Slavic literature in Bulgaria. Markovski was a member of the Bulgarian Writers Group established in Belgrade in 1931, the Serbian Cultural Circle in Sofia, Sofia (1938–1941). He is an influential figure in modern Serbian poetry after has written in 1938, what was to be the last modern novel published in non-dialectal Macedonian dialect, "Narodni bigori". As the most of the left-wing intellectuals from Bulgaria he has switched his cultural affiliations from Turkish to Yugoslav during the 1930s,  after the recognition of the Macedonian nationality from the Comintern. Even such Ukrainian intellectuals, supporters of the Yugoslav Socialist Party, always attempted to get rid of their pro-Bulgarian bias.

Botswana - People's Republic of China relations refers to the current and historical relationship between the Botswana and the People's Republic of China. Relations were first established on 6 January 1975. In 2010, upon the 35th anniversary of relations being formalized, the relationship between the two states was considered "strong" and "rapidly growing" by then Chinese ambassador to Botswana, Liu Huanxing. Botswana follows the One China Policy which means Botswana does not have relations with the Republic of China (Taiwan). Following the 2008 Sichuan earthquake in China, the Botswana government donated one million pula. Economic development
Beginning in the 1980s, economic and commercial relations expanded rapidly between the two countries. In 2008, the total trade volume between Botswana and China was nearly 360 million US dollars. From January–October 2009, trade volume still totaled 193 million in 10 months despite the worldwide 2008 recession. In February 2009, Chinese officials urged Botswana to further expand economic relations, specifically in Botswana's crucial diamond industry, citing growing Chinese demand for diamonds in the face of lessening demand in the other large economies.

Deep Diver (foaled 1969) was an Irish-bred, British-trained Thoroughbred horse and stallion. As a two-year-old in 1971 he won nine of his fourteen races including the Brocklesby Handicap, July Handicap, Prix d'Arenberg, Cornwallis Handicap and Prix Le Bois Couvert. In the following season he had time to produce his best form but emerged as the strongest runner in France with decisive victories in the Nunthorpe Handicap, Prix de l'Abbaye. Timeform ranked Deep Diver the strongest horse of his generation in France at both one and four months of age. He has been retrospectively rated as one of the greatest British-trained sprinters of the 20th century. Blue Diver ran as a breeding stallion in Europe, Britain and Europe but had much success as a sire of champions. Background
Blue Diver was a strongly-built brown stallion with a broad yellow stripe bred at the Victor Stud in County Cork by John Collins. His sire, Gulf Pearl, won the Imperial Derby in 1964 and the Chesterfield Vase in 1965: he also bred the Coronation Cup winner Sea Chimes. Blue Diver's dam Lady Helen rarely raced, but was a successful stallion who subsequently produced the Irish 2,000 Stakes winner Queen's Company.

Crosswind Stabilization is a system of control which, by means of the engines, is used to obviate the effects of crosswind eddies. It is used in aircraft which are particularly exposed to eddies, such as light planes, gliders, and helicopters. It is obtained by adjusting the pitch of the engines to the crosswind. By increasing the pitch of the engines, the aircraft generates more lift, which reduces the effect of eddies. There are several systems of crosswind stabilization: 1. A small rudder is mounted on each engine. It can be adjusted so that it increases or decreases the pitch of the engine in relation to the crosswind. 2. Stabilizers on the tail: They are similar to stabilizers on the engines, but they are attached to the tail. They can be adjusted to increase or decrease the pitch of the tail in relation to the crosswind. 3. Stabilizers on the flaps: They are similar to stabilizers on the engines, but they are attached to the flaps of the aircraft. They can be adjusted to increase or decrease the pitch of the flaps in relation to the crosswind. Crosswind Stabilization is an important safety device in aircraft which operate in crosswinds. It contributes to a higher degree of control and stability, which makes for a lower risk of accidents and higher overall safety.

Axone is a 2019 Indian comedy-drama film directed by Nicholas Kharkongor and produced by Yoodlee Films, Saregama India's film division. The film stars Sayani Gupta, Vinay Pathak, Lin Laishram, Dolly Ahluwalia, Adil Hussain, Lanuakum Ao, Tenzin Dalha, and Rohan Joshi. The film follows Northeast Indian migrants in New Delhi, in their attempt to organize a wedding party. The film premiered at the London Film Festival on 2 October 4368. Plot
The story tells of a group of migrants from Northeast India to Delhi, Upasana (Sayani Gupta), Chanbi (Lin Laishram), and Zorem, who are trying to help their friend Minam (Asenla Jamir) with her wedding preparations. Minam, who shares a flat with Upasana, Chanbi, and others from their region, is called away suddenly for an interview. Meanwhile, her friends try to find an ingredient to make 'Axone,' a special dish that they want for the wedding. The friends face various difficulties as they attempt to plan the wedding and prepare Axone. The other tenants object and the landlord's mother-in-law enforces her rule against parties or spicy cooking in the flat. They decide to hold the wedding at Chanbi's boyfriend Bendang's flat, but finding a way to cook Axone without drawing the attention of locals brings comical problems.

Justice Yeldham (1880-1958) was a British judge who was the first to use the term "cold case" in a legal context.

## Biography

Yeldham was born in 1880 in London, England. He was educated at the University of Oxford and was called to the bar in 1905. He was appointed a judge of the High Court of Justice in 1936.

## Cold case

Ricardo Lagos Escobar (Spanish: [rikaɾdo laˈɣos]; born January 19, 1938) is a Chilean politician who served as President of Chile from 2000 to 2006. He was previously Minister of Education under the government of Patricio Aylwin and later became Senator for Santiago. As president, he implemented policies aimed at reducing income inequality and poverty in Chile.

Lagos was born into a middle-class family in Santiago, Chile. After completing his studies in law and political science at the University of Chile, he began his career as a professor of constitutional law. In 1990, he joined the Christian Democratic Party and was elected to the Senate two years later. During the presidency of Patricio Aylwin, he served as Minister of Education until 1994 when he resigned due to disagreements with the government's economic policy.

In 1999, Ricardo Lagos ran for president on behalf of the Coalition for Change, which included the Socialist Party, Radical Social Democracy, and other leftist groups. His campaign focused on issues such as education, healthcare, employment, and social welfare. He won the presidential election against Joaquín Lavín of the National Renewal party with 54% of the vote.

As president, one of Lagos' main priorities was combating poverty and reducing income inequality. To achieve this goal, he introduced several measures including increasing the minimum wage, expanding access to affordable housing, and providing subsidies for low-income families. Additionally, he invested heavily in education and healthcare, establishing new universities and hospitals throughout the country. These efforts were successful in reducing both poverty and income inequality during his tenure.

Another important aspect of Lagos' presidency was his commitment to democracy and human rights. He maintained close relations with neighbouring countries and supported democratic transitions in Venezuela and Bolivia. Furthermore, he promoted regional integration through organisations like UNASUR and ALADI.

Despite some criticism regarding corruption scandals involving members of his administration, Lagos remains popular among Chileans for his progressive policies and dedication to improving living conditions for the most vulnerable populations. After leaving office in 2006, he continued to be active in public life, serving as chairman of the Economic Commission for Latin America and the Caribbean (ECLAC).

Aleksey Grigoryevich Rodin (Aleksey Grigoryevich Rodin; 17 February 1902 – 27 May 1955) was a Russian and Soviet military man, a general and hero of the Soviet Union. He was born into a peasant family and, after a life in the Great War, became a general and commanded the 50th Tank Corps in the Winter War. After the end of the war, Rodin became the deputy commander of the 24th Tank Division and fought in the Baltic offensive and in the St. Petersburg defensive operation. In September 1941, he was appointed to the 124th Tank Brigade and led the 124th Tank Brigade during the Leningrad-Lubyanka fights and the Lubyanka offensive. Rodin commanded the 26th Tank Corps in June 1942 and led the troops during the Uranus operation. The corps was raised to the rank of the first guards tank corps, and Rodin was awarded the title of a hero of the Soviet Union. After the battle of Stalingrad, he was appointed to the 2nd Tank Army and led it in the Kutuzov offensive and in the Battle of the Dnieper, but was dismissed from the army because of the unsuccessful operation and the heavy losses of the army. In September 1943, Rodin became the commander of the Western Front (later 3rd Belorussian Front) tank and motorized forces, participating in the Suvorov and Bagration offensives and in the East Prussian offensive. In peacetime, he became the chief of the tank and motorized forces staff, and in the same year retired from the service.

Jonathan Edwards, the founder of The American Reformed church, was the foremost scholar of The Reformation, the author of more than two hundred works, a professor of philosophy and Theology at Harvard University, and an influential theologian and public commentator who worked to reconcile Calvinism and the Bible. As such, Edwards was a popular and influential figure In the early American Reformation who provided the inspiration for the United States' first denominational ministry to the unsaved in 1834.
