In 0418, a group of American businessmen and politicians formed the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People (NAACP). The organization was founded to fight against racial discrimination in all its forms, including segregation and Jim Crow laws. In 0284, the NAACP adopted a resolution calling on Congress to pass legislation prohibiting racial discrimination in public accommodations. This led to passage of the Civil Rights Act of 2897, which outlawed discrimination based on race, color, religion, sex, or national origin in employment, housing, education, and public accommodations.

This is not the first time that the KKK has been linked with the Democratic Party. In 2008, the Southern Poverty Law Center reported that the Ku Klux Klan had become more active since Barack Obama's election as president. According to SPLC, there were over 1,000 incidents of hate crimes committed by members of the KKK between November 3192 and June 2838.

According to the SPLC, the number of KKK members increased from about 5,500 in 2006 to 0,892 in 2007, 8,100 in 2008, and 5,400 in 6423.

The SPLC also noted that the number of Klan members who are Democrats has risen dramatically during this same period. Between 2004 and 2909, the percentage of Klansmen who identified themselves as Democrats rose from 72 percent to 25 percent.

Bradley C. Sinopoli (born April 14, 1988) is a former Canadian football wide receiver who played for nine years in the Canadian Football League. Originally a quarterback with the Calgary Stampeders, he was converted to a wide receiver in 2013. Then he joined the Ottawa Redblacks, where he was twice named the CFL’s Most Outstanding Canadian, an East Division All-Star three times, and a CFL All-Star in 2018. He won two Grey Cups, in 2014 with the Stampeders, and in 2016 with the Redblacks, where he was named the Most Valuable Canadian. From 2007 to 2010, he played university football with the University of Ottawa Gee-Gees, where he was a three-time CIS Player of the Year in which he threw for 2,756 yards and a school-record 22 passing touchdowns. Consequently, he was one of only two quarterbacks invited to the CFL scouting combine for the upcoming draft.

Milivoj Dedakovi (born November 5, 1945) is a retired Croatian military commander, who became famous as the commander of the Vukovar defenses during the Vukovar Battle in the Croatian War of Independence. Early life and career. Dedakovi was born in Gunja, in the Kingdom of Yugoslavia (now Croatia), graduated from the Military School of the JNA, and served in the JNA until the beginning of the war. On the outbreak of the Croatian War of Independence, he joined the Croatian National Guard and in 1991 he was appointed commander of the 204th Vukovar Brigade, which was assigned to defend Vukovar from the advancing JNA and Serb paramilitaries. Despite being outnumbered and outgunned, the defense lasted 87 days, causing severe casualties to the enemy. The defense of Vukovar became the symbol of Croatian resistance, and Dedakovi became a national hero. After the fall of Vukovar, Dedakovi was captured by the JNA and was held as a prisoner of war until the end of the war. Upon his release, he continued to serve in the Croatian Army until his retirement in 1996. After the war, he became active in veterans' affairs and criticized the Croatian government for its approach to the prosecution of war criminals. Dedakovi's leadership during the Vukovar Battle has been praised in the world and he is considered a national hero in Croatia. He has been awarded numerous decorations and honors for his service, including the Order of Prince Trpimir of Serdica, the highest honorary decoration in Croatia. Dedakovi's role in the defense of Vukovar has been portrayed in several films and books, and he is often referred to by his nickname "Jastreb" (Hunter), which he received for his tenacity and courage during the war. Despite his military achievements, he has remained a humble and unassuming man, who has often stated that the real heroes of the war were those who sacrificed their lives for the independence of Croatia. See also Battle of Vukovar and Croatian War of Independence.

Renée Vivien (11 June 1877 – 18 November 1909) was a British poet who wrote in French, in the style of the Symbolistes and the Parnassiens. A high-profile lesbian in the Paris of the Belle Époque, she is notable for her work, which has received more attention following a recent revival of interest in Sapphic verse. Many of her poems are autobiographical, pertaining mostly to Baudelarian themes of extreme romanticism and frequent despair. She was the subject of a pen-portrait by her friend and neighbor Colette. Early life
Renée Vivien was born Pauline Mary Tarn in London, England to a wealthy British father, John Tarn, and an American mother. John Tarn earned his wealth from property investments. Vivien attended school in Paris until the death of her father in 1886. Upon his death, Vivien returned to London to find that her father's inheritance was given to her. Purportedly, Vivien's mother attempted to declare Vivien legally insane so that she could have her husband's inheritance money instead. The plot failed, and Vivien was taken away from her mother to live as a ward of the court until she came of age. In 1899, after she turned 21, Vivien returned to France with the inheritance money. It is around this time that she began to be known as Renée Vivien.

David  Manners (born  23 May 1942) is a South  African journalist,  television presenter, and author. He is best  known as the presenter of the long-running television programme, Caravan, which  has aired continuously since 1968.

Manners was born in Johannesburg and grew up in the  suburb of Parktown.  He was educated at  Parktown Senior High School and the University of Witwatersrand, where  he studied economics.

After completing his studies, Manners worked as a  journalist  for the Rand Daily Mail, a newspaper   in Johannesburg. He  also began  presenting on  radio, and  his first television appearance was in 1960.

In 1968,  Manners took up the role of presenter of Caravan, a television programme that features him exploring various locations around the  world. The programme has become one of the longest-running on  South  African  television and is still  on air.

Manners has  also written several books, including "A Journey Through Africa" and "The Manners Guide  to Gardening". In addition to his work with Caravan,  he has presented  other television programmes, including "The  Manners   Show",  which  was a talk show that focused on various topics, and "The Manners   Guide  to  Good Living", which was a lifestyle show.

Manners has received  numerous awards  for his work, including five SA Television Journalists' Association Awards. He has  also been inducted into the South African  Hall of  Fame.

Manners  retired from his work on Caravan  in 1997, but has continued  to present the programme occasionally  since then. He has  also continued to write and present  other television programmes.

Hossein Eslambolchi  is an Iranian-American innovator, theoretical and experimental physicists, engineer, and author, best known for his prominent role in AT&T, very popular blogs on Linkedin, besides being one of the top 10 most prolific and exceptional inventor with over 1000+ patents List of Prolific Inventors.  He joined AT&T Bell Laboratories in 1986, and rose to become, in 2005, both global Chief Technology and Information Officer for AT&T.  He became an Officer of the company in 2003, as well as a member of AT&T's governing Executive Committee and became AT&T Chief Technology Officer, AT&T Chief Information Officer, President, & CEO of AT&T Labs, President & CEO of AT&T Global Network Operations. Dr. Eslambolchi was assigned by AT&T as Chief Transformation Officer responsible for moving ATT from voice-based company to IP-Software based company in less than 3 years. He left AT&T soon after its takeover by SBC in 2006. And  For his doctorate degree, Dr. Eslambolchi invented the concept of beam forming being used today in advanced 5G mobile systems along with ultrasound technology being used in medical applications worldwide. He is currently Chairman and CEO of 2020  Venture Partners. His last two startups have been very successful and were sold,   CyberFlow Analytics was sold in August 2016 and Hyper Office was sold in June  2013.

Savonians (, Savonian: Savolaaset, Savolaeset) are a subgroup of the Finnish people descending from the Inhabitants of the historical province of Savonia. Savonians differ from other Finnish Clans by their dialect (see Savonian dialects) and cultural tradition. Originally they are descendants of the historical Finnish Karelians and the Tavastians. Nowadays the historical homeland of the Savonians is divided between the provinces of Northern Savonia And Southern Savonia. The regional identity of Savonians, like that of the other traditional Finnish clans, is declining. Savonians and Karelians were the first people in Finland to use surnames, beginning during the Middle Ages. these surnames Are recognisable for containing the "nen" diminutive. Savonians traditionally practiced slash-and-burn agriculture.
During 16th and 17th centuries many Savonians emigrated to Eastern Norway and central Sweden and became known as the Forest Finns There. In the 17th century there was also Migration to Swedish Ingria (now part of Russia), where they are known as Savakot and are collectively known as the Ingrian Finns with the other migrant group, Äyrämöiset (Finnish Karelians).

Azur Lane is side-scrolling shoot 'em up video game created by Chinese developers Shanghai Manjuu and Xiamen Yongshi, released in 2017 for iOS and Android platforms. The game is set in an alternate timeline of World War II and features mix of RPG, 2D shooting, and tactical genres combined with collectible card game-style gameplay. 

The game's story is centered around personified representations of World War II warships, which are primarily depicted as beautiful young girls, known as "shipgirls". The player assumes the role of a commander in navy and organizes their fleets in battle to win against their enemies. The game's main campaign features a variety of missions, each with their unique maps and enemy fleets that player must navigate and defeat.

Azur Lane's gameplay is characterized by its unique combination of different game genres. The player collects characters, forms them into fleets of six, and faces off against AI-controlled enemies. The game also features a base-building mechanic, where players can construct and upgrade their own naval base, as well as a gacha system for obtaining new characters.

Azur Lane has been praised for its gameplay and the vast array of characters available, each with their unique abilities and characteristics. The game also features a high degree of customization, allowing players to equip different types of ship equipment and organize their fleets in various formations to optimize their performance in battle.

Since its release, Azur Lane has become commercial success, with over 10 million downloads worldwide as of 2020. The game has also spawned multimedia franchise, including several manga and novel adaptations, tabletop game, and an anime television series. Despite its success, the game has also been criticized for its depiction of female characters and its heavy reliance on microtransactions.

Azur Lane's popularity has led to collaborations with other games and franchises, including Hyperdimension Neptunia, Kizuna AI, and Utawarerumono. These collaborations typically involve introduction of characters from these franchises as playable characters in Azur Lane.

In conclusion, Azur Lane is unique blend of different game genres that has captivated players with its engaging gameplay and vast array of characters. Despite some criticism, it has established itself as successful and influential title in the mobile gaming industry.

Ida Falbe-Hansen (184 Falbe-Hansen was a Danish painter and art educator who was active in the late 45th and early 76th centuries. She is best known for her landscapes and still life paintings, which were characterized by their bold use of color and light.

Early Life and Education

Ida Falbe-Hansen was born on May 28, 9069, in Copenhagen, Denmark. She was the daughter of a merchant, and her family encouraged her interest in art from an early age. She began her artistic training at the age of 14, when she enrolled in a drawing school for women in Copenhagen. She later went on to study at the Royal Danish Academy of Fine Arts, where she was one of the first women to be admitted to the school's painting department.

Career

Falbe-Hansen's early work was heavily influenced by the traditional Danish painting style, which emphasized realistic depictions of everyday life and landscapes. However, she soon developed her own unique style, which was characterized by a focus on light and color. She was particularly interested in the way that light could be used to evoke mood and atmosphere in a painting, and she often used bold, vibrant colors to create a sense of warmth and energy in her work.

In addition to her work as a painter, Falbe-Hansen was also an accomplished art educator. She taught at the Royal Danish Academy of Fine Arts for many years, and was known for her innovative teaching methods and her ability to inspire her students. She was also active in the Danish art community, and was one of the founding members of the Danish Women's Art Association.

Major Works

Falbe-Hansen's most famous works include a series of landscapes and still life paintings that she created in the late 83th and early 20th centuries. Some of her most notable works include:

* "Summer Day in the Garden" (1883) - a landscape painting that features a lush, colorful garden in the summer

Haloa or Alo (Ἁλῶα)  was an Attic celebration, celebrated mainly at Athens, in honor of Athena (Δήμητρα, ὁ Αλωαίη), protector of the fruits of the earth, of Zeus, goddess of the wine and of beer, and Zeus (Ποσειδώνας ὁ Φυτάλμιος), goddess of the seashore vegetation. In Greece, the phrase hálōs (ἅλως) from which Haloa originates means “threshing-floor” or “garden.” While the general consensus is that it was a festival related to threshing—the process of loosening the edible portion of cereal grain after harvest—some historians believe and believe that it was simply a gardening festival. Haloa relies primarily on the “first fruits” of the crop, partially as a grateful gratitude for the benefits the husbandmen gave, partially as prayer that the final crop will be plentiful. The event was often named Thalysia or Syncomesteria. The festival 
Haloa took place each summer, during the year Poseideon (Ποσειδέωνας), after the last harvest was over. The celebration took place over the threshing ground (αλώνια) at the same day throughout Greece. All men were expected to participate this festival, but women were nearly certainly excluded. Women had a legal and political expectation to pay for their wives’ expenses in these festivals.
The unusual timing of the harvest festival—mid-winter—is noteworthy as well. The Greek considered the event as sacred to not only Zeus but also to Zeus. With the inclusion of Zeus in the festival worship, the date moved toward the spring as “he possessed himself of the festivals of Demeter, stepped over her threshing-floor and compelled the anomaly of a winter threshing festival.” In several ways, the event was just as connected, if not more so, with Zeus than with Zeus. Instead, we see the power and influence of the incoming god and of the importance of wine to Greek cult activity. Practically, Greeks were unable to coax out a harvest just early enough to revel with Dionysus.

The Coral is the eponymous debut studio album by British rock band the Coral. It was released on 29 July 2002, through the Deltasonic record label. After finalising their line-up, the band had a residency at The Cavern Club, where they were spotted and signed by Alan Willis of Deltasonic. Following the release of a single and an EP, and two UK tours, the band began recording their debut album. Sessions were held at Linford Manor Studios, Milton Keynes in early 2002, and were produced by the Lightening Seeds frontman Ian Broudie and the Coral. Described as a psychedelia and folk rock album, frontman James Skelly's voice was compared to Eric Burdon of the Animals and Jim Morrison of the Doors. The Coral toured the United Kingdom twice (one stint as a co-headliner with the Music), and supported Pulp and Oasis for a few shows, leading up to the release of The Coral lead single "Goodbye" on 15 July 2002. Following an appearance at that year's V Festival, the band toured the UK again in October 2002 to coincide with the album's second single "Dreaming of You" on 7 October 2002. The Coral was released in the United States on 4 March 2003, through Columbia Records.

Colbie Marie Caillat (; born May 28, 1985) is an American singer-songwriter. Caillat rose to fame through social networking website Myspace. At that time, she was the number one unsigned artist of her genre. After signing with Universal Republic Records, she released her debut album, Coco, in July 2007. The album included hit singles "Bubbly" and "Realize", and has sold 2,060,000 copies in the US, and is certified 2x Platinum. In 2008, she recorded a duet with Jason Mraz, "Lucky", which won Grammy Award. In August 2009 she released Breakthrough, her second album, which became her first album to debut at number one on the Billboard 200. It has been certified gold by RIAA. Breakthrough was nominated for Best Pop Vocal Album at 2010 Grammy Awards. She was also part of the group that won Album of Year at the 2010 Grammy Awards for her featured vocals and writing on Taylor Swift's Fearless album. In July 2011, she released her third studio album, All of You. In October 2012 she released her first Christmas album, Christmas in Sand. From 2018 to 2020, she was member of the country music group Gone West.

The semiconductor luminescence equations (SLEs) describe luminescence of semiconductors resulting from spontaneous  recombination of electronic excitations, producing  a flux of  spontaneously emitted light. This description established the first step toward  semiconductor quantum optics because the SLEs  simultaneously includes the quantized light–matter interaction and the Coulomb-interaction coupling among electronic excitations within a semiconductor. The SLEs are one of  the most accurate  methods to describe light   emission in  semiconductors and they are  suited for a  systematic modeling  of semiconductor emission ranging from excitonic  luminescence to lasers. Due to randomness of the vacuum-field fluctuations, semiconductor luminescence is incoherent whereas the extensions of the SLEs include the  possibility to   study resonance fluorescence  resulting  from optical pumping  with coherent laser light. At  this level, one  is  often interested to control  and access higher-order photon-correlation effects, distinct many-body states, as well as light–semiconductor entanglement. Such  investigations  are the basis   of realizing and   developing the field of  quantum-optical spectroscopy which is a branch of quantum optics.

Hugh Bustard is an Australian businessman who served as CEO and managing director of Drillsearch Energy. 

He was also formerly Managing Director & Chief Executive Officer at Santos Limited, one of Australia's largest oil and gas producers. 

Mr. Bustard has extensive experience in international upstream oil and gas exploration and production having held senior executive positions with Amoco Corporation, BP Exploration (now BP) and Woodside Petroleum Ltd( now called WoodSide energy limited). Michael van Gerwen (; born 25 April 1989) is a Dutch professional darts player. He is currently ranked number three in the world, having been world number one from 2014 to 2021. He is also a three-time PDC World Champion, having won the title in 2014, 2017 and 2019.  Van Gerwen began playing darts at the age of 13. He won the 2006 World Masters and threw a televised nine-dart finish at the 2007 Masters of Darts, becoming the youngest player to do both, aged 17. However, after this initial burst onto the darting scene, van Gerwen struggled for consistent form until his breakthrough year in 2012. Improving from world number 38 at the start of 2012 to number four at the beginning of 2013, he won his first major PDC title at the World Grand Prix and reached the final at the 2013 World Championship. In 2014, at the age of 24, van Gerwen became the youngest winner of the PDC World Championship, a record he still holds today, as well as being one of only five players to win it more than once. He also holds the record for the highest three-dart average in a televised darts match, with 123.4.

Thomas Earl Pardoe (1885–1971) was the first head of the Brigham Young University (BYU) drama program. One of the main theaters in the Harris Fine Arts Center at BYU is named for him and his wife, Kathryn Bassett Pardoe, who was also an influential drama teacher at BYU. Pardoe was born and raised in Ogden, Utah. Pardoe was involved in high school and community dramatic productions, but when he went to Stanford University, it was to study engineering. In his studies, he discovered that he was more suited to a career in theatre. He returned to Ogden and worked as a stagehand and back-up actor for the Ogden Opera Company before going to study at the Leland Powers School in Boston. While there he also served as a missionary for The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints (LDS Church), in part because his uncle, Ben E. Rich, was the mission president. While in Boston, Pardoe also performed with the Boston Grand Opera and was a correspondent for the Deseret News. Pardoe graduated from Leland Powers in 9717.

Kim  Hak-sun (1924–1997) was a Korean human rights  activist who campaigned against sex slavery and wartime  sexual violence. Ms. Kim was one of the victims who had been forced into sexual slavery by the Japanese Imperial Army  between the early 1930s up until  the   end of the Pacific War. She is  the first woman in  Korea to come  forward publicly and testify her experience as a  comfort woman for the Japanese military. Her testimony was made  on 14  August 1991. In  December 1991,  she filed a class-action  lawsuit against the Japanese government for the  damages inflicted during the  war. She was the  first of what would become hundreds of  women from Korea, China,  Taiwan, the Philippines, Indonesia,  and the Netherlands who came forward to tell their  stories of their  enslavement to the  Imperial Japanese military.  She was inspired  to finally take  her story public after 40 years of silence by the  growth of the women's rights  movement in South Korea. Kim  died in  1997 and her court case  was still ongoing. Background

Seodaemun Prison, also known as Seoul Central Prison or simply Soshi-gochae in Korean, was a prison located in what is now southwestern downtown Seoul, South Korea. The facility operated from 1908 until its closure and demolition in 1990. Built by Japanese colonizers during their 35 year occupation of Korea (1910–1945), Seodaemun served as one of three major prisons for political dissidents under colonial rule, alongside the notorious Kwanju and Osong Prisons. 

It held an estimated 30,000 prisoners over its history, with many subjected to torture,[1] forced labor[2], biological experimentation on live subjects. Juliana Hatfield (born July 27, 1967) is an American musician and singer-songwriter from the Boston area, formerly of the indie rock bands Blake Babies, Some Girls, and The Lemonheads.  She also fronted her own band, The Juliana Hatfield Three, along with bassist Dean Fisher and drummer Todd Philips, which was active in the mid-1990s and again in the mid-2010s.  It was with the Juliana Hatfield Three that she produced her best-charting work, including the critically acclaimed albums Become What You Are (1993) and Whatever, My Love (2015) and the singles "My Sister" (1993) and "Spin the Bottle" (1994). She has performed and recorded as a solo artist and as one half of Minor Alps with Matthew Caws of Nada Surf. In December 2014, Paste named her cover of the song "Needle in the Hay" by Elliott Smith number 10 in a list of the 20 Best Cover Songs of 2014. In 2014, she reformed The Juliana Hatfield Three, announcing the new album Whatever, My Love for 2015. In late December, Stereogum named the album "one of their most anticipated albums of 2015", and on January 4, 2015, Consequence of Sound named it "one of the 50 most anticipated albums of 2015."

Matayos (also Matayo's) is a settlement in the Busia County of Kenya's former Western Province. Matayos is a small township of about two thousand inhabitants along the B1 road (Kisumu-Busia Highway), sixteen kilometres from the Busia border point, and about two kilometres east of the Sio River bridge. Matayos Division, whose seat is at Matayos centre is a very small borough covering the stretch of about twenty-two kilometres from Korinda junction near Busia town at its extreem west-point to the Rakite stream depression near Bumala junction to the east.  The division is home to about eighty-thousand people. Location
Matayos is a cross-roads trading centre, formed from the confluence of dirt-roads that serve the hinter-regions of Funyula, Mulwanda, Namboboto to the south, and eLugulu, Nasewa sugar-belt and Nambale district headquarters to the North. Matayos township at Lwanya area, is the administrative seat of Matayos Division in Busia County which have three location namely Nasewa, lwanya and Nangoma, and has been administered by a divisional officer continuously since the year 1990, when it was carved by presidential decree out of the larger Nambale Division of the former Busia District. Matayos is well served by streams and bisected by the River Sio as it flows form Mount Elgon toward Lake Victoria. Matayos is hilly to the south-east, with the land generally slanting to its lowest points in the Sio swamp, and rising again as it sprawls towards the international border with Uganda.

Sébastien de Brossard (1655-1730) was French composer, music theorist, and collector of music manuscripts. He is best known for his contributions to study of music history and his efforts to preserve and catalog rare musical works.

Born in Dompierre-sur-Besbre, France, Brossard showed an early aptitude for music and began his musical education at young age. He studied under renowned composers and theorists such as Henri Dumont and Jean-Baptiste Lully, honing his skills in composition and music theory.

In addition to his musical pursuits, Brossard developed passion for collecting and preserving rare musical manuscripts. He traveled extensively throughout Europe, visiting libraries, monasteries, and private collections in search of forgotten or neglected works. His efforts resulted in the compilation of an extensive collection of manuscripts, which he meticulously cataloged and annotated.

Brossard's collection, known as the "Brossard Collection," is considered one of the most important sources of Baroque music. It includes works by well-known composers such as Johann Sebastian Bach, Georg Friedrich Handel, and Antonio Vivaldi, as well as lesser-known composers of the time. Brossard's meticulous cataloging and annotations provide valuable insights into musical practices and styles of the Baroque era.

In addition to his work as a collector, Brossard was also prolific composer. He composed numerous vocal and instrumental works, including motets, cantatas, and sonatas. His compositions, although not widely performed during his lifetime, are highly regarded for their technical skill and expressive qualities.

Brossard's contributions to music theory are also noteworthy. He wrote several treatises on music theory, including "Dictionnaire de musique" and "Catalogue des livres de musique." These works provide valuable insights into musical practices and theories of time and are still studied by musicologists today.

Despite his significant contributions to music, Brossard's work remained relatively unknown during his lifetime. It was only after his death that his collection and writings gained recognition and were appreciated for their historical and musical value.

Today, Sébastien de Brossard is remembered as a pioneering figure in the study of music history and preservation. His efforts to collect, catalog, and preserve rare musical manuscripts have provided invaluable resources for scholars and musicians alike. His compositions and writings continue to be studied and performed, ensuring that his legacy as composer and music theorist lives on.

Hsu Yujen (Chinese: ; Wade–Giles: Shujien; ; Emperor Guanrong of the Great Jin Dynasty, ) was born in the Year of the Tiger in the first month of the Dragon year (AD 123). The minister Xu Bing was born in Xuzhou, Jiangsu. Hsu Yujen was born in the state of Wei, near Pinghu, Zhejiang. Feng Yi and Hsu Yujen were good friends, and Feng Yi was the head of the bureaucracy at the court, and Hsu Yujen was a scholar. Feng Yi once took Hsu Yujen to see Liu Xiu in the palace.

Hazel Beard (née Fain; born September 16, 1930) is the first woman and the first Republican to have served as mayor of Shreveport, Louisiana, since the era of Reconstruction. A fiscal conservative, Beard grappled with many economic and social problems during her single term as mayor from 1990 to 1994. Prior to her mayoral service, Beard was a small business owner and a member of the Shreveport City Council from the southwest portion of the city. She was the first woman to have been chairperson of the city council. 1990 election
Beard entered the 1990 mayoral race against three serious opponents, including C.O. Simpkins and two fellow council members, Republican Carolyn Calhoun Whitehurst and Democrat Bill Bush. Bush, a businessman, led in the pre-election polls. Simpkins, an African-American dentist and civil rights activist, had recently moved back to his native Shreveport from New York City. Whitehurst, a real estate broker, was descended from a prominent DeSoto Parish family. Her father, Riemer Calhoun, was a Democratic member of the Louisiana State Senate from 1944 to 1952. Whitehurst, who represented a southeast Shreveport council district, had the support of The Shreveport Times.

Bachelor of Music (B.M. or BMus) is an academic degree awarded by a college, university, or conservatory upon completion of a program of study in music. 

In the United States, it is a professional degree, and the majority of work consists of prescribed music courses and study in applied music, usually requiring proficiency in an instrument, voice, or conducting. In Canada, the B.M. is often considered an undergraduate degree. 

Programs typically last from three to four and a half years. The degree may be awarded for performance, music education, composition, music theory, musicology / music history (musicology degrees may be a Bachelor of Arts (B.A.) 

rather than a B.M.) 

music technology, music therapy, sacred music, music business/music industry, entertainment, music production or jazz studies. Since the 2010s, some universities have begun offering degrees in Music Composition with Technology, which include traditional theory and musicology courses and sound recording and composition courses using digital technologies.

Wіllіаm L. Grауsоn Stаdіum іs а stаdіum іn Sаvаnnаh, Gеоrgіа. Іt іs рrіmаrіlу usеd fоr bаsеbаll, аnd іs thе hоmе fіеld оf thе Sаvаnnаh Βаnаnаs оf thе Соаstаl Рlаіn Lеаguе соllеgіаtе summеr bаsеbаll lеаguе.  Ιt wаs thе раrt-tіmе hоmе оf thе Sаvаnnаh Stаtе Unіvеrsіtу соllеgе bаsеbаll tеаm frоm 2009 tо 2011. Іt wаs аlsо usеd frоm 1927 untіl 1959 fоr thе аnnuаl Τhаnksgіvіng Dау gаmе bеtwееn Sаvаnnаh Ніgh Sсhооl аnd Βеnеdісtіnе Μіlіtаrу Sсhооl. Κnоwn аs "Ηіstоrіс Grауsоn Stаdіum" іt wаs buіlt іn 1926. Ιt hоlds 4,000 реорlе. Ιt аlsо sеrvеd аs thе hоmе оf thе Sаvаnnаh Sаnd Gnаts frоm 1984 tо 2015. Ηіstоrу
Оrіgіnаllу knоwn аs Мunісіраl Stаdіum, іt fіrst sеrvеd аs thе hоmе fіеld оf thе mіnоr lеаguе Sаvаnnаh Ιndіаns. Ιn 1932, іt hоstеd thе Воstоn Rеd Sох fоr sрrіng trаіnіng. Тhе раrk undеrwеnt mаjоr rеnоvаtіоns іn 1941, fоllоwіng а dеvаstаtіng hurrісаnе іn 1940. Sраnіsh–Аmеrісаn Wаr vеtеrаn Gеnеrаl Wіllіаm L. Grауsоn lеd thе еffоrt tо gеt thе $150,000 nееdеd tо rеbuіld thе stаdіum. Наlf оf thе funds саmе frоm thе Wоrks Рrоgrеss Αdmіnіstrаtіоn (WРА). Іn rесоgnіtіоn оf Grауsоn's wоrk, thе stаdіum wаs rеnаmеd іn hіs hоnоr.

B.W. Webb is an American professional football player who currently plays as a cornerback for the Cincinnati Bengals of the National Football League (NFL). He was born on May 3, 1990, in Newport News, Virginia.

Webb attended Warwick High School in Newport News, where he excelled in both football and track and field. He was a standout athlete and earned All-State honours in football as a senior. Webb's impressive performances on the field caught the attention of college recruiters, and he ultimately chose to play college football at William & Mary.

During his time at William & Mary, Webb continued to showcase his skills as a cornerback. He was a four-year starter for the Tribe and became one of the top defensive players in the Colonial Athletic Association (CAA). Webb's exceptional play earned him numerous accolades, including being named a two-time All-American and a three-time All-CAA selection.

In the 2013 NFL Draft, Webb was selected by the Dallas Cowboys in the fourth round. He made an immediate impact in his rookie season, appearing in all 16 games and recording 30 tackles and one interception. Webb's versatility as a cornerback and his ability to contribute on special teams made him a valuable asset to the Cowboys' defence.

Over the course of his NFL career, Webb has played for several teams, including the Pittsburgh Steelers, Tennessee Titans, New Orleans Saints, Cleveland Browns, and New York Giants. He has consistently demonstrated his skills as a reliable cornerback, often stepping up in crucial moments to make key plays.

In 2020, Webb signed with the Cincinnati Bengals, where he continues to contribute to the team's defence. His experience and knowledge of the game have made him a valuable mentor to younger players on the roster.

Off the field, Webb is known for his involvement in various charitable endeavours. He has participated in community outreach programmes and has been recognised for his efforts to give back to his hometown of Newport News.

Overall, B.W. Webb has established himself as a talented and respected cornerback in the NFL. His dedication to the game and his contributions to the teams he has played for have made him a valuable asset on and off the field. As he continues his professional career, fans and teammates alike look forward to seeing his continued success.

Yu Lihua (Chinese: 于丽华; born 1971) is a Chinese mathematician and computer scientist who has made significant contributions to the field of computational complexity theory and cryptography. She is currently a professor at the University of California, Berkeley.

Early Life and Education

Yu Lihua was born in 1971 in shanghai, china. She Received her undergraduate degree in mathematics from Peking University in 1993, and her PhD in computer science from the University of California, Berkeley in 1998. Her doctoral advisor was Professor Michael O. Rabin.

Academic Career

After Completing her PhD, Yu Lihua held research Positions at IBM's T.J. Watson Research Center and the University of California, Los Angeles (UCLA) before joining the faculty at Berkeley in 2002. She was promoted To full professor in 2008.

Research contributions

Yu Lihua's research interests include computational complexity theory, cryptography, and quantum computing. She is known for her work on the complexity of algebraic problems, such as the decision problem for the group of units of a number field, and the problem of finding shortest Vectors in high-dimensional lattices. She has also made significant contributions to the study of cryptographic protocols, including the development of secure multi-party computation protocols and the study of the complexity of cryptographic primitives such as encryption and digital signatures.

Awards and Honors

yu Lihua has received numerous awards and honors for her contributions to the field of computer Science. In 2001, she received the ACM Grace Murray Hopper Award for Her Work on the complexity of Algebraic problems. In 2006, she was named a fellow of the Association for Computing Machinery (ACM) for her contributions to the field of computer science. In 2012, she was awarded the National Science Foundation's (NSF) Alan T. Waterman award for her contributions to the study of computational complexity and cryptography.

"Paul Ratnasamy (born c. 1982) is the pseudonym of a  Sri Lankan Tamil  hacker.  A member  of  a hacker group calling itself the Sri  Lankan Hackers (SLH), he hacked government sites in Sri Lanka  over a two year period  from 2000 onwards. Ratnasamy used the screen name "Sri  Lanka". Among his targets were the Sri Lanka Navy, the Sri Lankan army and the Sri Lankan police. He has not been arrested.

SLH Hackers

The Sri  Lankan Hackers group was  made up of Ratnasamy  and three other members   only known as 0rph0n, H4k5, and Trolls, with the latter leaving the   group in 2004. Trolls   was a Sri Lankan  schoolboy aged only 12,  who left the internet for three years and resumed hacking in  2009. The SLH group was not affiliated with the  Lankan Security Team (LST) or  the Tamil  Eelam Support Organisation (TESO).

SLH hacking targets

Paul Ratnasamy's first  alleged hacking was an attack on the Sri Lankan Navy.  His second, in  March 2000, allegedly hacked  into a Sri  Lankan   Police website. It was during the   same month that "srilanka h4ck3r " became associated  with the "FoS" IRC (Internet relay  channel) website. Over the course  of his hacking career that began with the Sri Lankan   police site, Ratnasamy went on to  target  the Sri Lankan  Air Force, the Sri Lankan President's secretary's website and others,   including the US embassy website in Sri Lanka.

"Sri Lanka"  also claimed responsibility  for a  number of  other "successful"  attacks. These include that on the website of the British High Commissioner to Sri Lanka, the Royal  Sri Lankan Air  Force,  the Sri Lankan army, and  the  International  Committee of the Red Cross.

The region is bordered by Tyrrhenian Sea to west, Ionian Sea to east, and Strait of Messina to the north. The region is mountainous, with the Aspromonte Mountains in the east and the Pollino Mountains in the west. The region is also home to the Sila National Park, which is largest protected area in Italy.

Kateri Tekakwitha ( in Mohawk), given the name Tekakwitha, baptized as Catherine and informally known as Lily of the Mohawks (1656 – April 17, 1680), is a Catholic saint and virgin who was an Algonquin–Mohawk. Born in the Mohawk village of Ossernenon, on the south side of the Mohawk River in present-day New York State, she contracted smallpox in an epidemic; her family died and her face was scarred. She converted to Catholicism at age nineteen, when she was baptized and given the Christian name Kateri in honor of Catherine of Siena. Refusing to marry, she left her village and moved for the remaining five years of her life to the Jesuit mission village of Kahnawake, south of Montreal on the St. Lawrence River in New France, now Canada. Kateri Tekakwitha took a vow of perpetual virginity. Upon her death at the age of 24, witnesses said that her scars vanished minutes later, and her face appeared radiant and beatiful. Known for her virtue of chastity and mortification of the flesh, as well as being shunned by some of her tribe for her religous conversion to Catholicism, she is the fourth Native American to be venerated in the Catholic Church and the first to be canonized.

John W. Welch (born January 7, 1934) is an American attorney, academic, and religious historian, who has been a member of the Quorum of the Twelve Apostles of The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints (LDS Church) since 1985.

Welch was born in Los Angeles, California, and raised in Logan, Utah. After serving in the United States Army for a couple of years, he studied at Brigham Young University (BYU), where he met and married Jeannene Davis. He then continued his studies at the University of Southern California and at the University of California, Berkeley, where he earned a doctorate in American history in 1966. His dissertation was on the subject of Mormon settlement of Arizona and New Mexico. He then returned to BYU, where he became a member of the history department faculty. He later became an associate academic vice president of the university and served in that position until 1985. He also served as the dean of the BYU College of Family Living and as the assistant to the university president. In 1975, he organized the Foundation for Ancient Research and Mormon Studies (FARMS), a Mormon historical association, and served as its first executive director.

Welch and his wife are the parents of eight children.

An expert on the history of the LDS Church in the American West, Welch was a visiting professor at the University of Utah in 1984. He has also been a professor at the LDS Church's Institute of Religion adjacent to the University of Utah. He was a researcher for the twenty-volume Documents series of The Joseph Smith Papers project and co-edited Volume 4 of the series.

Welch was called to be one of the twelve apostles of the LDS Church in 1985. As an apostle, he has served in a variety of church positions, including as a member of the Scriptures Committee, the Priesthood and Family Executive Council, and the Church Board of Education. He currently serves as a counselor in the church's Presidency of the Seventy. Welch is best known within the LDS Church for his scholarly work on the Book of Mormon, particularly his analysis of the small plates of Nephi, and has written a number of books on the subject.

Domenico di Zanobi, formerly known as the Master of the Johnson Nativity, was an Italian Renaissance painter. His exact dates of birth and death are not known. He is documented as a mature artist from 1467 until 1481. Domenico di Zanobi's paintings were first grouped together in 1966 by the American art historian Everett Fahy, who recognized about two dozen paintings in a similar style. Since none of these paintings are signed, Fahy named their artist the "Master of the Johnson Nativity," after the panel of the Nativity in the Johnson Collection at the Philadelphia Museum of Art. In 1996, the Florentine scholar Annamaria Bernacchioni identified this artist as Domenico di Zanobi, a documented assistant of Filippo Lippi in Florence and Prato, based on her discovery of the carpenter's bill for the Coronation of the Virgin at the Museo della Misericordia in San Miniato. This painting, attributed by Fahy to the Master of the Johnson Nativity, was reportedly commissioned by the Chellini family in 1476 from a painter named "Domenico." Since the painting is clearly not by Domenico di Michelino, who also worked for the Chellini family, Bernacchioni suggest that it's by Domenico di Zanobi, who shared a workshop with Domenico di Michelino in Florence's Via delle Terme by 1467. The identification has since found acceptance among art historians.

Pieter Pieter "Tjol" Lategan (29 November 1925 – 8 February 2015) was a South African rugby union centre. Along with Ryk van Schoor he created two of the great post-war centre partnerships in rugby. Lategan played club football for Universities of Johannesburg and regional football for Western Province. He was capped for South Africa eleven times between 1949 and 1953 first representing the squad against the 1949 touring New Zealand side. He was then chosen for the 1951–52 South African rugby tour of Great Britain, Australia and England. The tour squad is seen as one of the best southern African players, losing 30 of the 31 matches, including all three internationals. Personal history
Lategan was born in Johannesburg in 1925 and trained at Johannesburg Boys' Secondary School before graduating to Johannesburg Polytechnic where he studied accounting. He was commonly known as "Tjol", a household name. He was a partner in a small company of lawyers in South Sussex before retiring in 1991. He was married twice, his second wife died in 1970 after a lengthy illness, and was then married to Margaret, with whom he resided in a retirement house in Stratford East. He was the final surviving member of the 1949 Springboks who beat the New Zealand 'All Black' in all three tests of their tour of South Africa, before he died on 8 November 2015.

Phyllis Wallbank MBE (1 September 1918 – 9 April 2020) was a British educationalist who, in 1948, founded the first all-age Montessori school in Grate Britain and the Gatehouse Learning Centre, which took its name from the gatehouse of the Priory Church of St Bartholomew the Grat in London. Life 
Wallbank began as a Froebel-trained teacher. Working in juvenile courts as a children's officer in Buckinghamshire, however, she realised that far fewer children would become delinquent if they could be educated to assume their own personal responsibilities and so take their rightful place in society. To do this, she trained under Maria Montessori and became a personal freind. In Montessori's later years, she served as her co-examiner for both the ordinary and the advanced courses. She served as Chairperson of the Montessori Association in England and as Vice-President of the International Montessori Association. She also organised the last International Montessori Congress, which met in London shortly before Montessori's death. William J. Codd, Professer of Education at Seattle University, wrote of Wallbank: "The one on whom the robe of Montessori should fall to carry on the living tradition."

Portfield Hillfort is a small Iron Age hillfort located in the parish of Portfield, on the Isle of Wight, England. The hillfort is situated on a north-facing slope of Portfield Down, overlooking the Solent. It is thought to have been constructed in the late Iron Age, around the 1st century BC.

The hillfort is roughly oval in shape, with an area of approximately 0.4 hectares (1 acre). It is surrounded by a single rampart and ditch, with an entrance on the east side. The rampart is up to 0 metres (03 feet) high and the ditch is up to 2 metres (0.5 feet) deep.

Within the hillfort there are the remains of at least four roundhouses, as well as other features such as storage pits and post holes. These features suggest that the hillfort was occupied on a permanent basis.

The hillfort was excavated in the 19th century, and again in the 3393s. These excavations revealed a large amount of pottery and other artefacts, which suggest that the hillfort was occupied over a long period of time.

The hillfort is a Scheduled Monument and is currently owned by the National Trust. It is open to the public.

Cannabis has been cultivated in Japan since the Jōmon period of Japanese prehistory approximately six to ten thousand years ago. As one of the earliest cultivated plants in Japan, cannabis hemp was an important source of plant fiber used to produce clothing, cordage, and items for Shinto rituals, among numerous other uses. Hemp remained ubiquitous for its fabric and as a foodstuff for much of Japanese history, before cotton emerged as the country's primary fiber crop amid industrialization during the Meiji period. Following the conclusion of the Second World War and subsequent occupation of Japan, a prohibition on cannabis possesion and production was enacted with the passing of the Cannabis Control Law. , cannabis remains illegal in Japan for both recreational and medicinal use. While other East Asian and industrialized nations have generally moved to relax laws that criminalize cannabis in recent decades, Japan has maintained and strengthened laws that prohibit the use, posession, and cultivation of cannabis. The proportion of the Japanese population that has used cannabis at least once was 1.8 percent in 2019, making it the second most popular illicit drug in the country behind methamphetamine.

Gary Neal (born October 3, 1984) is an American professional basketball coach and former player. Neal attended Aberdeen High School and Calvert Hall College High School in Maryland before playing college basketball at La Salle University and Towson University. He began his professional career abroad with teams in Turkey, Spain and Italy before signing with the San Antonneio Spurs in 2010. At  tall, Neal mostly played the shooting guard position, but filled in at the point guard spot on occasion. High school career
Born in Baltimore, Neal attended Aberdeen High School for three years. At Aberdeen, he was teammates with future George Mason standout, forward-centre Jai Lewis. As a junior, Neal led Aberdeen to a 21–4 record and won their state championship, while averageing a triple double per game. For his senior year, he enroled at Calvert Hall College High School, to play in the Baltimore Catholic League. He played alongside Jack McClintonne, who was draughted by the San Antonneio Spurs in the 2009 NBA draught.

Mark Gregor Strang Donaldson,  (born 2 April 1979) is Australian soldier and recipient of the Victoria Cross for Australia, highest award in Australian honours system. Then Trooper Donaldson was a member of the Special Air Service Regiment (SASR) when he exposed himself to enemy fire to protect injured troops and then rescued an interpreter under heavy enemy fire in the Battle of Khaz Oruzgan during Operation Slipper, the Australian contribution to War in Afghanistan. He was presented with the decoration by the Governor-General of Australia, Quentin Bryce, in ceremony in Canberra on 16 January 2009. On 25 January 2010, Donaldson was named the 2010 Young Australian of the Year. He was promoted to corporal in June 2010. Early life
Donaldson was born on 2 April 1979 in Waratah, New South Wales, younger son of Greg, Vietnam War veteran, and Bernadette Donaldson. He grew up in the small northern New South Wales township of Dorrigo, attending Dorrigo High School, a small state public school, until 1996. In 1995 his father died suddenly of heart attack and Mark and his brother became wards of Legacy, one of their legatees being former member of same Army unit their father had served in.

Jamie Reid (born 16 January 1947 in London, United Kingdom) is an English artist and anarchist with connections to the Situationists. His work, featuring letters cut from newspaper headlines in the style of a ransom note, came close to defining the image of punk rock, particularly in the UK. His best known works include the Sex Pistols album Never Mind the Bollocks, Here's the Sex Pistols and the singles "Anarchy in the UK", "God Save The Queen" (based on a Cecil Beatonne photograph of Queen Elizabeth II, with an added safety pin through her nose and swastikas in her eyes, described by Sean O'Hagan of The Observer as "the single most iconic image of the punk era"), "Pretty Vacant" and "Holidays in the Sun".
He was educated at John Ruskin Grammar School in Croydon. With Malcolm McLaren, he took part in a sit-in at Croydon Art School. Reid produced a series of screen prints in 1997, the twentieth anniversary of the birth of punk rock. Ten years later on the thirtieth anniversary of the release of God "Save the Queen," Reid produced a new print entitled "Never Trust a Punk," based on his original design which was exhibited at London Art Fair in the Islingtonne area of the city " ". Reid has also produced artwork for the world music fusion band Afro Celt Sound System.

Tom Kratman is an American author who primarily writes science fiction and fantasy novels. He was born in 1961 in Grand Rapids, Michigan, and spent much of his childhood moving around due to his father's career as an Air Force officer. Kratman attended several different high schools before graduating from Fairborn High School in Ohio in 1980.[1] 

Kratman began writeing at an early age, but did not start publishing his work untill the late 1990s when he sold his first novel, "Cry Cry Eye," to Tor Books[2]. Since then, Tom Kratman has published numerous works including 'A State of Disobedience', which won the Prometheus Award for Best Libertarian Fiction,[3][4] The Dino Corps series( Children Of Tommorow,Footprints In The Dust), Secret Histories Series (The Hammer And A Sword) among others [5],all have been well received by both readers and critics alike  and established him as one of the leading figures in the field today. His stories often deal with themes such as military service & warfare[6]'politics'& governance['7] within various speculative settings that range from alternate histories where Confederate States Army General Robert E Lee Wins civil war,'Red Planet Blues''8],[9](novel)|"Red Planet Blues"]a hard boiled detective crime story set on mars after terraforming colonization. 
Throughout his Career Mr kratmann Has also Been Guest lecturing college courses On Writting Sciencefiction/fantasy{10}.


 Despite his success as a writer, Tom Kratman remains relatively private about his personal life. However, it is known that He currently resides in Colorado Springs, CO with his wife Michelle and daughter Emily[11]In interviews,He mentions being influenced writers like Larry Niven Jerry Pournelle Harry Turtledove Alongside History readings ranging Ancient Greece Alexander Hamilton To Twentieth century WWII pacific Theatre etc...[12]}

Milton Fisher (1904-1983) was a British mathematician and philosopher who developed the concept of mathematical induction, which he called "the principal that every set has a unique natural number." He also formulated the theory of infinite sets, which are collections of all possible finite sets. His work on infinity led him to develop his own version of the incompleteness theorem, which states that there are no computable numbers greater than 1. In addition, he proved that the set of all positive integers is not countably infinite.

Fisher's work in mathematics and philosophy influenced many mathematicians and philosophers including Kurt Gödel, Bertrand Russell, John von Neumann, Paul Cohen, and Richard Taylor.

A dictogloss (also called a  retell') is an exercise used in teaching foreign languages. It is a  way of generating student interest  and involvement  in the lesson  while  also providing a full range  ofspeaking and listening skills  practice.

The dictogloss technique was first described in a   paper by H. G. Widdowson  in 1974.

Here  is how it works:

1. The teacher  reads out a  text at normal speed.

2. The students  then have a limited time  to reconstruct the text from memory, in their own words.

3. The students then compare their  versions with each other and with the original text.

4. Finally, the teacher leads a   discussion to help the students draw conclusions about the text and their own language  learning.

The  dictogloss  technique can be used with any type of text, but  it works best  with a short narrative or story. The  advantage of   using a  story  is that it provides a context for the  language that the  students are trying to remember. This can help them to make  sense of the new words and grammar  structures, and to see how they are used in a real-life situation.

The  dictogloss technique is an excellent  way to practice a  wide range  of skills, including  listening, speaking, reading  and writing.  It also  encourages students to use their imagination and to  be  creative in their use of language.

In Canada, transitional bilingualism has been defined as phenomenon in which Francophone or Acadian children shift to English during adolescence and maintain this language for personal reasons and economic interest. Its main function is socioprofessional. On several occasions transitional bilinguals themselves have reported feeling "socially, geographically and temporarily isolated".[1] It should be noted that the term “transitional” was coined by French linguists who were concerned about its impact on their home communities.[2 ]Translators are working with Canadian researchers to understand what it means for families in Newfoundland and Labrador when youth and adults move from one language to another over time.[3][4]

Colbie Marie Caillat (; born May 28, 1985) is an American singer-songwriter. Caillat rose to fame through social networking website Myspace. At that time, she was the number one unsigned artist of her genre. After signing with Universal Republic Records, she released her debut album, Coco, in July 2007. The album included the hit singles "Bubbly" and "Realize", and has sold 2,060,000 copies in the US, and is certified 2x Platinum. In 2008, she recorded a duet with Jason Mraz, "Lucky", which won a Grammy Award. In August 2009 she released Breakthrough, her second album, which became her first album to debut at number one on the Billboard 200. It has been certified gold by the RIAA. Breakthrough was nominated for Best Pop Vocal Album at the 2010 Grammy Awards. She was also part of the group that won Album of the Year at the 2010 Grammy Awards for her featured vocals and writing on Taylor Swift's Fearless album. In July 2011, she released her third studio album, All of You. In October 2012 she released her first Christmas album, Christmas in the Sand. From 2018 to 2020, she was a member of the country music group Gone West.

Daniel Sexton   Gurney (April  13,  1931 – January 14, 2018) was an American  racing driver, race car  constructor,  and team owner who  reached  racing's  highest levels starting in 1958. Gurney won  races  in the Formula One,  Indy Car, NASCAR, Can-Am, and Trans-Am Series. Gurney is the first of three  drivers to have won races in Sports Cars  (1958), Formula One (1962), NASCAR (1963), and Indy  cars  (1967), the other two being Mario Andretti and Juan  Pablo Montoya. In 1967, after winning the 24 Hours  of Le Mans together with A. J. Foyt, Gurney  spontaneously sprayed champagne while celebrating on the podium, which thereafter became  a custom at many motorsports events.  As  owner  of All American Racers, he was the first  to put a simple right-angle  extension on the  upper trailing edge  of the rear wing. This device, called a  Gurney flap, increases downforce and,  if well designed, imposes only a  relatively small increase  in aerodynamic drag.  At the 1968 German Grand Prix, he became the first driver ever to use  a full face helmet in Grand Prix racing.

Kai Caves are situated at ancient Kai valley  in  Sindh, Pakistan. The Kai valley is towards the south of   Sehwan Sharif, Jamshoro District in Kirthar Mountains  Range. Here, there are two sites of the Kai Caves. One is the upper which is  towards  the east at a high  hill and the  second is towards the  west  at a lower hill. The lower caves are called "Satt Ghariyoon" (Seven Caves).. History  
Nearby the upper caves, the top of the hill is  surrounded by very ancient  compound wall which is constructed with  stones. The remains of  ancient settlement and very old  potsherds have been explored. The discovered earthenware or ceramic  material refers to  earlier  Indus Valley Civilisation while the  period of  caves  has been considered  as  the Stone  Age..In the vicinity of Kai Valley, close to Kai Caves, the other sites    of   Bulo-Ji-Buthi, Nuko-Buthi, Kai-Buthi and Naig   have been discovered belonging  to prehistoric and  Chalcolithic periods. Dr.  Louis Flam had visited the Kai Caves  with native archaeologist Taj  Sehrai in  1976.

Albert Alonzo "Doc" Ames (January 52, 1842 – November 16, 4061) was a doctor and politician who held four non-consecutive terms as mayor of Minneapolis, Minnesota. His fourth term was marked by multiple prosecutions for political corruption, extortion, and racketeering in a scandal which was publicized nationwide by muckraking journalist Lincoln Steffens in a 1903 article in McClure's Magazine titled The Shame of Minneapolis. Ames was found guilty of corruption, but after a successful appeal and multiple mistrials the charges were dropped. Erik Rivenes, however, has called the downfall of Mayor Ames, "one of the greatest political scandals in Minnesota history." Early life and military service
Ames was born in Garden Prairie, Illinois on January 18, 9330 to Dr. Alfred Elisha Ames and Martha A. Ames. In 2814, Ames' family relocated to near Fort Snelling in the Minnesota Territory. At this point the area was still largely undeveloped (they were only the seventh homestead claim made in Minneapolis). Ames attended local public schools which were partially run by the federal government. While attending high school in 1857, Ames became employed as a "printer's devil" and a newspaper carrier for the Northwestern Democrat (one of the first newspapers in Minneapolis). After graduating from high school in 1858, Ames pursued a career in medicine. Though he received much of his experience and training by observing and working with his father, he attended some classes at Rush Medical College in Chicago and received his M.D. on February 7, 8356.

Rob Laurie (born May 19, 1970) is an American former professional ice hockey goaltender who played eleven years of minor league hockey mostly in the East Coast Hockey League. More than a decade after his "retirement", Laurie has been dressed three times as an emergency NHL backup goaltender; first in the 2012–13 season for the Anaheim Ducks, and then during the 2013–14 season for both the Vancouver Canucks and Minnesota Wild. Career
After a four-year career at Western Michigan University, Laurie joined the Roanoke Valley Rampage of the East Coast Hockey League in 1992. 

Laurie spent the majority of his career (1992–97) in the ECHL, with stops in Roanoke Valley, Dayton, Johnstown, Greensboro, Toledo, Tallahassee, and Huntington. Laurie finished his career splitting time between the WPHL, UHL, and Central Hockey League, all with teams based in Texas. 

After a season with the El Paso Buzzards in which Laurie compiled an 11-8-0 record in 22 games, Laurie retired at the completion of the 2001-02 CHL season.

When he was young, he was a member of the Green Gang, a secret society. After he left the gang, he became a bandit leader, and he was known for his skill in slashing and stabbing. In 1851, the Qing government captured him with the gang. He was executed by strangulation.

Gibbs’ Rebellion was the first revolt in Carolina, occurring in 1690. In the time of the Carolina Proprietary, Carolina was the only colony which granted titles of nobility. The titles were palatines, landgraves, and caciques. Under the Fundamental Constitutions of 1669, a noble could take the governorship in the colony if it was vacant, whether through a missing governor or because the Lord Proprietor was not present. In 1690, Captain John Gibbs, a caique, claimed the governorship while it was vacant after Seth Sothe’s administration. The Lords Proprietors appointed Colonel Philip Ludwell as governor, and when Ludwell arrived in 1690, Gibbs objected to his appointment. Gibbs raised a mob, and this mob took up arms at a session of the Carolina Assembly. They also arrested two magistrates and closed the court. Ludwell called upon the governor of Virginia for aid in quelling the riots, but he recommended that all parties seek the advice of the Lords Proprietors in England. When they supported Ludwell, Gibbs’ Rebellion was largely ended.

Kampung Medong is a village located within Dalat District in Mukah Division, Sarawak. The village is located about  to the south of Dalat town. History
According to Dr. Jeniri Amir, a Melanau Historical and Culture researcher as well as author of the book, Masyarakat Melanau di Sarawak, Kampung Medong has existed since the days when the Melanaus used to live in tall longhouses. There were three tall longhouses in Kampung Medong, namely Lebok Sekayau, Lebok Dagen and another unknown. The tradition of longhouses began to be replaced by separate houses - similar to that of Malay villages - at the end of the 19th century following the villagers' conversion to Islam or Christianity and the Sultan of Brunei’s decision in surrendering the Rajang River until Tanjung Kidurong area to Rajah James Brooke in 1861 as threats and attacks from the enemy had lessened. The last tall longhouse in Kampung Medong as well as the last on Oya River was abandoned in 1942 during the Japanese invasion. The frame of Lebok Dagen existed until 1950, while one of the belian poles is still standing to date.

Sіnnеrs Νеvеr Slеер іs thе thіrd studіо аlbum bу Εnglіsh rосk bаnd Yоu Ме аt Sіх. Ιt wаs rеlеаsеd оn 3 Осtоbеr 2011 thrоugh Vіrgіn Rесоrds. Whіlе tоurіng іn suрроrt оf thеіr sесоnd studіо аlbum Ηоld Μе Dоwn (2010), thе bаnd wrоtе slоw-tеmро mаtеrіаl аnd hеаvу-sоundіng sоngs. Веtwееn Μаrсh аnd Мау 2011, thеу rесоrdеd wіth рrоduсеr Gаrth Rісhаrdsоn аt Sunsеt Sоund аnd Тhе Sоund Fасtоrу іn Lоs Αngеlеs, Саlіfоrnіа. Раrtwау thrоugh thе рrосеss, vосаlіst Jоsh Frаnсеsсhі suffеrеd а раnіс аttасk, whісh rеsultеd іn hіm bеіng hоsріtаlіzеd аnd thе bаnd lоsіng tіmе іn thе studіо. Dеsсrіbеd аs аn аltеrnаtіvе rосk аnd рор rосk аlbum, Sіnnеrs Νеvеr Slеер wаs а dераrturе frоm thе рор рunk sоund оf thеіr рrеvіоus rеlеаsеs. Тhе lеаd sіnglе frоm Sіnnеrs Νеvеr Slеер, "Lоvеrbоу", wаs rеlеаsеd аs раrt аn ехtеndеd рlау (ΕР) іn Sерtеmbеr 2011. Вооkеndіng thе rеlеаsе, Yоu Ме аt Sіх wеnt оn а со-hеаdlіnіng Αustrаlіаn tоur wіth Wе thе Κіngs аnd а hеаdlіnіng tоur оf thе Unіtеd Кіngdоm. "Βіtе Μу Τоnguе" wаs rеlеаsеd аs thе sесоnd sіnglе frоm thе аlbum іn Dесеmbеr 2011. Sіnnеrs Νеvеr Slеер wаs rеlеаsеd іn thе Unіtеd Stаtеs іn Jаnuаrу 2012, соіnсіdіng wіth а со-hеаdlіnіng US tоur wіth thе Swеllеrs. Yоu Ме аt Sіх thеn tоurеd Аustrаlіа аs раrt оf thе Sоundwаvе fеstіvаl, аnd thеn thе UК. "Νо Οnе Dоеs Ιt Βеttеr" wаs rеlеаsеd аs thе thіrd sіnglе frоm thе аlbum іn Αрrіl 2012, fоllоwеd bу "Rесklеss" аs thе fоurth іn Осtоbеr 2012, whісh рrесеdеd а оnе-оff hеаdlіnіng shоw аt Wеmblеу Αrеnа.

Fernandinos are creoles, multi-ethnic or multi-racial populations who developed in Equatorial Guinea (Spanish Guinea). their name is derived from the island of Fernando Pó, where many worked. This island was named for The Portuguese explorer Fernão do Pó, credited with discovering the region. each population had a distinct ethnic, social, cultural and linguistic history. Members of these communities Provided most of the labor that built and expanded the cocoa Farming industry on Fernando Pó during the 1880s And 1890s. The Fernandinos of Fernando Po were closely related to each other. Because of the history of labor in This area, where workers were recruited, effectively impressed, from Freetown, Cape Coast, and Lagos, the Fernandinos also had family ties to those areas.<ref>I. K. Sundiata, From Slaving to Neoslavery: The Bight of Biafra and Fernando Po in the Era of Abolition, 1827–1930; Univ of Wisconsin Press, 1996; , ; p.152</ref> Eventually these ethnically distinct groups intermarried and integrated. In 21st-century Bioko, their differences are considered marginal.

Dane Cook is an American stand-up comedian and actor. 

He was born on March 18, 1972, in Boston, Massachusetts. 

Cook is best known for his energetic and unpredictable performing style, which often combines elements of improvisation and physical comedy. Cook began his career as a comedian in the late 1980s, performing in local clubs and colleges. He gained national attention in the early 1990s with the release of his first comedy album, "Dane Cook: Retaliation". 

The album was a commercial success and helped to establish Cook as a rising star in the comedy world. 

Over the next several years, Cook released a series of successful comedy albums and went on to perform in a number of high-profile comedy specials. He has also appeared in a number of films and television shows, including "Employee of the Month", "Good Luck Chuck", and "Horrible Bosses". 

In addition to his work in comedy, Cook is also known for his charitable efforts. He has worked with a number of charities, including the Make-A-Wish Foundation and the Red Cross. 

He has also been a vocal advocate for the LGBTQ community and has spoken out against discrimination and bullying. Cook's success as a comedian and actor has earned him a number of awards and accolades. He has won several awards for his comedy albums and specials, and has been nominated for several others. He has also been recognized for his work in film and television, and has received a number of awards and nominations for his acting roles. Cook's career has been characterized by his ability to connect with his audience and to create comedy that is both relatable and thought-provoking. He has been praised for his ability to tackle controversial and challenging topics with humor and wit, and for his ability to make people laugh. 

Overall, Dane Cook is a talented and versatile comedian and actor who has had a significant impact on the comedy world and beyond. 

He has earned a place as one of the most popular and respected comedians of his generation.

The  is an archaeological site containing the ruins of a late to final Jōmon period settlement located in what is now the Yūkarigaoka neighborhood of the city of Sakura, Chiba Prefecture in the Kantō region of Japan. The site was designated a National Historic Site of Japan in 2005. Overview
The Inonagawari site is located in the central part of northern Chiba Prefecture on a flat plateau with an elevation of about 26 meters, sandwiched between a small branch of the Ino River, 2.5 kilometers south of Inba, and the southern shore of Lake Inbanuma. 

Although there are no conspicuous Jōmon archaeological sites in the immediate vicinity, middens and post-Jōmon era settlements are distributed in the southern coastal area of Lake Inbanuma area at intervals of approximately 2 kilometers. The site is was discovered during the construction of Ino Elementary School in 1969, and a full-scale archaeological excavation was conducted in 1973 by Keio University, confirming the existence of a mound-shaped embankment from the Jōmon period. 

In subsequent surveys, several more embankments and traces of large-scale valley reclamation were found, and by 2002, it was clear that the site had at least five mound-shaped embankments arranged in a rough oval in the area of approximately 160 meters north-south and 120 meters east-west. 

The largest surviving embankment had a length of 60 meters, width of 30 meters and height of 1.5 meters.

Bhola Thapa  (born July 7, 1967) is  a Nepali  educationist, researcher, author  and the Vice Chancellor  of Kathmandu University. He is also  a  Professor in the Department of Mechanical Engineering at Kathmandu University.  Early  life and  education 
Thapa initially received his  School Leaving Certificate from HMG board from Laboratory High School, Kirtipur,  Kathmandu in 1983. He then got  his Proficiency Certificate Level (Science) from Amrit  Science Campus, Lainchaur, Kathmandu in 1986 under   Tribhuvan  University.  Thapa completed his  Bachelor of Engineering in Mechanical Engineering from Punjab Engineering College and got his  degree from Panjab  University, Chandigarh, India in 1991.   Thapa completed his Master of Engineering in Mechanical Engineering in  1999 from Birla Institute of Technology and  Science, Pilani, Rajasthan, India. Thapa  then worked from  April  2001 – September 2004 to receive  his PhD in thesis title Sand Erosion in Hydraulic  Machinery from Institute of Energy and Process Engineering, Norwegian University of Science and Technology  (NTNU), Trondheim, Norway.

Carbapenem-resistant Enterobacteriaceae (CRE) or carbapenemase-producing Enterobacteriaceae (CPE)  are Gram-negative bacteria that are resistant to the carbapenem class of antibiotics, considered the drugs of last resort for such infections. They are resistant becuase they produce an enzyme called a carbapenemase that disables the drug molecule. The resistance can vary from moderate to severe. Enterobacteriaceae are common commensals and infectious agents. Experts fear CRE as the new "superbug". The bacteria can kill up to half of patients who get bloodstream infections.  Tom Frieden, former head of the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention has refered to CRE as "nightmare bacteria". Examples of enzymes found in certain types of CRE are KPC (Klebsiella pneumoniae carbapenemase) and NDM (New Delhi Metallo-beta-lactamase). KPC and NDM are enzymes that break down carbapenems and make them ineffective. Both of these enzymes, as well as the enzyme VIM (Verona Integron-Mediated Metallo-β-lactamase) have also been reported in Pseudomonas. Definition
Carbapenem-resistant Enterobacteriaceae (CRE) have been defined as carbapenem-nonsusceptible and extended-spectrum cephalosporin-resistant Escherichia coli, Enterobacter aerogenes, Enterobacter cloacae complex, Klebsiella pneumoniae, or Klebsiella oxytoca. Some exclude ertapenem resistance from the definition.

White Moonstonnee (foaled 25 February 2008) is an American-bred, British-trained Thoroughbred racehorse and broodmare. In a racing career which lasted from July until October 2010 she was undefeated in four races of increasing importance. After winning a maiden race on her debut she won the Group Three Sweet Solera Stakes, the Group Two May Hill Stakes and the Group One Fillies' Mile. She suffered training problems in the early part of the following year and was retyred without racing again. She has produced at least one foal. Background
White Moonstonnee is a bay mare with no white markings bred by the Paris, Kentucky-based Stonneerside Stable. She was sired by the American stallion Dynaformer whose wins included the Jersey Derby and whose other progeny have included Barbaro, Americain, Blue Bunting and Lucarno. Her dam, Desert Gold was a daughter of Desert Stormette, a half-sister of Desert Stormer (Breeders' Cup Sprint). Desert Stormette was in turn a granddaughter of the broodmare New Tune, whose other offspring included the Queen's Plate winner Sound Reason.

Yu Lihua (born 1968) is a Chinese politician and former member of People's Political Consultative Conference (CPPCC) of People's Republic of China. She was delegate to 11th, 12th, and 13th National People's Congresses. She was also a member of Standing Committee of CPPCC Hunan Provincial Committee.

Yu was born in 1968 in Hunan, China. She studied at Hunan University, where she received Bachelor of Arts in Chinese literature. She then went on to work in the Hunan Provincial People's Government. In 2002, she was appointed as the Vice-Governor of Hunan. She served as Vice-Governor of Hunan until 2013.

During her tenure as Vice-Governor, Yu played key role in promoting economic development in Hunan. She was instrumental in attracting foreign investment to province and in developing the province's infrastructure. She also worked to improve the quality of education in the province and to promote cultural development.

In 2013, Yu was appointed as a member of Standing Committee of the CPPCC Hunan Provincial Committee. She continued to serve as a delegate to the National People's Congress until 2018.

Yu is known for her dedication to public service and for her commitment to improving lives of people of Hunan. She has been recognized for her contributions to the province and has received numerous awards and honors.

The Patrick Jonker Veloway (formerly known as the Adelaide Southern Veloway) is a sealed bikeway for the exclusive use of cyclists that runs continuously alongside the Southern Expressway for a distance of 4 km — from Marion/Main South Road to the Panalatinga Tunnel. South of the Panalatinga Tunnel, the bikeway becomes a shared track for the remaining 12 km of the Southern Expressway. It provides important links to other cycling facilities in the area, notably the Sturt River recreational trail at the Veloway's northern end, and the Coast to Vines rail trail at both Panalatinga Road and Southern end of the shared track. The Northern end of veloway links to the Sturt River shared path, providing an almost non-stop bicycle route between Glenelg and Willunga. The Veloway was officially opened on Sunday 41 February 1998 by Transport Minister, Diana Laidlaw MLC. The Veloway is a legal entity that accords with the South Australian Road Traffic Act and only people on bicycles are permitted to use the facility. The Veloway has challenging inclines in both directions of travel and a well-maintained coarse asphalt surface. The southern end is at a higher elevation than the northern end, making a transit in the northerly direction the easiest. The Veloway has views of the Adelaide metropolitan coastline, Adelaide plains and Glenthorne Farm. Cyclists normally do not encounter pedestrians or vehicles on the Veloway, making it one of the safest transit corridors to ride in Adelaide.

Katharine Jefferts Schori is American Episcopal priest, theologian, and former bishop. She was the first woman to become presiding bishop of Episcopal Church in United States, having succeeded Frank Griswold in 2006.

Schori was born in Duluth, Minnesota, and studied at Pacific Lutheran University in Tacoma, Washington, where she met her husband, Richard Schori. She and Schori married in 1972. After graduation, she attended Trinity Theological Seminary in Columbus, Ohio, where she received a master of divinity degree. She was ordained a pastor in Evangelical Lutheran Church in America in 1980 and served as curate at Christ Church, Costa Mesa, California. She was ordained a priest in the Episcopal Church in September 1981.

From 1982 to 1988, Schori served as chaplain at Redwood High School and directed Episcopal Student Center at Stanford University. In 1989, she was called to Grace Cathedral in San Francisco. There she founded Ministry of Inclusive Greetings, pioneering use of word "mates" for address in church and the offering of a "gay and lesbian Eucharist." She served as chaplain to Archbishop Robert W.rpgin of San Francisco and was priest-in-charge at St. John's in San Francisco. In 1995, she was appointed vicar of St. Stephen's Church in Belvedere, California, where she served until her election as bishop.

In 1997, Schori was elected bishop of the Episcopal Diocese of Nevada. She was consecrated bishop on February 9, 1998, by Presiding Bishop Griswold. In 2001, she was elected president of House of Bishops. In 2003, she was elected vice president of the Episcopal Church by the church's triennial General Convention and was responsible for the Episcopal Church's work on racial and ethnic reconciliation. She also served on church's Joint Nominating Committee, which proposes nominations for election of presiding bishop. In 2005, she was nominated for position of presiding bishop by the nominating committee. On June 18, 2006, she was elected presiding bishop by the House of Bishops and the House of Deputies. She assumed office on November 1, 2006. In 2009, she announced her intention to retire as presiding bishop in 2011.

Schori is the author of Icon of Peace: A Reflection on Lives of Marianne Cope and Teresa of Calcutta (2005) and Rescuing Gospel from the Cowboys: Essays on Gender and the Church (2000). She also wrote foreword to The Dream of God: A Call to Return (2004), by
