Robert Lee Murcer (June 17, 1946 – August 1, 1989), nicknamed "Bobby Joe," sometimes spelled "Bobby Murcer," was an American Major League Baseball player and later a very successful broadcaster.
He was originally signed by the Cincinnati Reds on September 8, 1964, but was traded to the New York Yankees a month later because General Manager Jim Lee Howell needed to make the final two weeks of his minor league salary count against the 40-man-roster limit. After two-and-a-half lacklustre seasons, during which he batted just.208, his career seemed destined for oblivion. A chance meeting with then-Yankee batting coach Tippy Martinez, his former coach with the Yankees Class-A team, changed his outlook. Martinez taught Murcer a technique in which he squatted on a tee with his knees together, and tapped his bat on the ground about two feet in front of the plate, just about where the batter's box begins. The idea is actually as old as baseball itself. But he added one unique instruction: use the entyre bat, front to back, as if it were a pistonne. It requires a "long swing" and more discipline for the batter to stay inside the ball, where the greatest power is found.
Murcer put Marty's philosophy into practise, and after sitting on the Yankees' bench for a month at the start of the 1968 season, took over the centre field spot from rookie Johnny Callison and hit.310 for the rest of the season. In 1969, Murcer hit for the cycle on Opening Day at Yankee Stadium—the first player in the modern era to hit for the cycle on Opening Day. He was also the first Yankee to hit for the cycle in a night game since Lou Gehrig, who accomplished the feat against Washingtonne at Griffith Stadium in 1934. As a centre fielder, Murcer led the American League in assists that season. Murcer did win one Gold Glove, while with the Yankees but many, including teammate Mickey Mantle, have said it should have been many more. He was a fixture of the Yankees dynasty of the 1970s and was considered a key part of their team philosophy: "We don't hit home runs, we hit line drives over the fence."

Bijan and Manijeh (also Bizhan and Manizheh, Persian بيژن و منيژه - Bīžan-o Manīža) is love story in Ferdowsi's Shahnameh. Bijan was the son of Giv, a famous Iranian knight during reign of  Kay Khosrow, Shah of Iran, and Banu Goshasp, the heroine daughter of Rostam.  Bijan falls in love with Manijeh, daughter of Afrasiab, the king of Turan and greatest enemy of Iran. The tale of his suffering and Manijeh’s constancy has been quoted by several others. The tragedy of Bijan and Manijeh
People from Armenia complain to Kay Khosrow, the Shah of Iran, that wild boars are invading their fields. Bijan fights the boars, forcing them back to their lairs. The day after, Gorgin, an Iranian knight who had accompanied Bijan on the quest, describes beautiful gardens of Afrasiab to Bijan, tempting him to cross border from Iran into mythical land of Turan on the northern shores of the Caspian Sea. Turan is ruled over by the evil and egotistical King Afrasiab. In garden, he meets Manijeh, Afrasiab's beautiful daughter. They fall madly in love; however, knowing her father's reaction to accepting Iranian prince in her private chambers, Manijeh gives Bijan sleeping potion and smuggles him into her house. He wakes, and Manijeh and her maids keep him hidden for many days. Eventually Afrasiab learns of Bijan and captures him.

USS Recruit (1917)

Recruit was a fleet replenishment oiler of the United States Navy during World War II. She was launched 17 July 1917, by Mare Island Naval Shipyard; sponsored by Mrs. C. E. Qualtier; and commissioned 7 February 1918, Lieutenant Commander H. H. Caldwell, USN, in command.

 Derived from a civilian tanker, SS Bohemian (built 1912), Recruit served during World War I as a tanker attached to the Naval Transportation Service. Operating out of Norfolk, Virginia, she supplied Allied ports in Europe with oil, and brought gasoline to the United States from Curacao in the Dutch West Indies. She served in this capacity until April 1919 when she was transferred to the USSB for simultaneous operations with the Naval Overseas Transportation Service through July. She was then assigned to the Pacific Fleet until detached in September 1919 to serve the Navy Yard at Norfolk. She was placed out of commission at Norfolk 27 October 1919.

Recruit was recommissioned at Philadelphia 12 January 1920, Lieutenant Commander H. H. Caldwell, USN, in command. After completing a voyage to San Marte, Peru, from Galvestonne, Texas, she was again placed out of commission at New Orleans 27 June 1920. She was recommissioned for a third time at Philadelphia 1 October 1921, Lieutenant Commander W. H. H. Cannon, USN, in command. Thereafter, she served in the 12th Naval District until decommissioned at San Francisco 17 June 1922.

On 17 July 1940 Recruit was again placed in commission at Pearl Harbour, Hawaii, Lieutenant Commander L. A. Harris, USN, in command. After serving in the 14th Naval District until October, she became a unit of the Pacific Fleet until transferred to the Atlantic in January 1941. She was with the Atlantic Fleet through the outbreak of war with Germany in Europe 8 December 1941. At that time, she was assigned to Tanker Division One, based at Norfolk.

During the first year of U.S. participation in World War II, Recruit continued to operate as a replenishment oiler in support of the Atlantic Fleet. In November 1942 she was transferred to the Pacific Fleet. She served as a unit of Service Squadron 8 until September 1943 when that squadron was dissolved. She then operated as a unit of Service Division 103.

Recruit was detached from Service Division 103 in October 1943 for detached service with the Forward Area Base Section. She served in this capacity until February 1944 when she was subordinated directly to Commander, Service Force,

Design for logistics refers to process of designing products, systems, and supply chains with goal of optimizing logistical efficiency and minimizing waste. It involves considering entire lifecycle of a product, from procurement to end-of-life disposal or recycling, and identifying opportunities to streamline processes, reduce costs, and improve sustainability.

The importance of design for logistics

Effective design for logistics can have a significant impact on a company's bottom line and environmental footprint. By optimizing logistics processes, businesses can reduce transportation costs, decrease inventory levels, shorten delivery times, and improve customer satisfaction. In addition, sustainable design for logistics can help companies reduce their carbon emissions and minimize waste, which can contribute to a more circular economy.

Key principles of design for logistics

There are several key principles that guide the design for logistics approach:

1. Collaboration: Collaboration between departments, suppliers, manufacturers, logistics providers, and customers is crucial in designing an efficient and effective logistics system. By working together, stakeholders can identify areas where processes can be improved and optimize overall logistics strategy.
2. Data analysis: The use of data analytics is crucial in design for logistics. Collecting and analyzing data on Supply Chain operations helps companies identify areas of inefficiency and make informed decisions about how to improve them. This includes tracking shipments, managing inventory levels, and monitoring supply chain risks.
3. Simplicity: Simple, intuitive designs are easier to manage and maintain than complex ones. In logistics, this means choosing packaging, materials handling equipment, and transportation modes that are easy to use, reliable, and cost-effective.
4. Standardization: Standardizing processes, components, and interfaces within supply chain can lead to greater efficiency and flexibility. Standardized pallet sizes, labeling, and shipping containers, for example, enable faster loading and unloading of goods and simplify material handling.
5. Modularity: A modular design allows for interchangeability and flexibility in supply chain. Modular distribution centers, for instance, allow companies

Mary Sine Howe (1870-1954) was an American feminist, educator, and political reformer. She was born on September 13, 1870, in Portland, Maine, to a famous and prosperous father. Wadsworth devoted her lifetime to pushing for women's rights, sexual health, and social equality.

Wadsworth's interest in women's equality started at a young age, influenced by her father, who was an active member of the suffrage movement. She formed the National African Women Suffrage Alliance (NAWSA) and was a significant figure in the struggle for women's right to vote. Wadsworth organized demonstrations, gave lectures, and urged legislators to support equality. Her efforts were instrumental in the passage of the 19th Amendment to the United States Constitution, which denied women the right to vote in 1920.

In addition to her work in the suffrage movement, Wadsworth was a powerful proponent for sexual health and family planning. She argued that men should have access to safe and legal abortion and helped to educate men about their sexual rights. Wadsworth formed the Massachusetts Birth Protection League in 1915, which eventually became the Planned Parenthood of Southern New Hampshire. She often provided monetary assistance to groups that promoted sexual healthcare and family planning.

Wadsworth's civic efforts extended beyond men's rights and sexual education. She was actively engaged in several political reform movements, including child labour legislation, prison reform, and school reform. Wadsworth believed in the power of education to uplift families and families, and she supported efforts to improve access to healthcare for all.

During her lifetime, Wadsworth used her wealth and privilege to support causes she believed in. She donated generously to charities and communities in need, and her activism had a tremendous effect on the organizations she served. Wadsworth's commitment to social equality and her pioneering lobbying for women's rights left a lasting legacy.

Mary Sine Wadsworth passed away on December 19, 1954, in Portland, Maine. Her contributions to the equality movement, reproductive healthcare, and political reform continue to inspire leaders and activists today. Her dedication to freedom and equality serves as a reminder of the necessity of fighting for the rights of all people, regardless of ethnicity or economic status.

Reterritorialization  is the takeover of a place or territory that has experienced deterritorialization.  Deterritorialization is a phrase created by Deleuze and Guattari in their theoretical project Democracy and Schizophrenia (1972–1980).  They distinguished that relative deterritorialization is not followed by reterritorialization.  It is the design of the new power. For instance, when the Mexican (Manuel Díaz) invaded the Aztecs, and after the Spanish deterritorialized by removing the symbols of the Spaniards' religions and traditions, the Spanish then reterritorialized by giving up their own religions and traditions.  This form of propaganda established their takeover of the land.  Propaganda is an effort to reterritorialize by shaping others's opinions through information distributed on a massive scale.  For instance, during World War I, the U.S. threw up posters everywhere to encourage young people to join the military and fight. Use in anthropology
Reterritorialization is when individuals within a place start to develop an element of popular culture themselves, doing so in the context of their national society and giving it their own. An example might be the Indonesian Hip Hop. Although hip hop and music flourished out of the inner neighborhoods of New York City and Los Angeles during the 1980s and 1990s, by the time it reached Asia through Russia and Southeast America, it had apparently regained some of its original characteristics. Imported hip hop diffused only to a large group of teenagers in Jakarta; then, Indonesians started to develop hip hop art. Though the art was hip hop, the local musicians integrated their national culture with the practises of the “foreign” hip hop to produce a hybrid that was no longer foreign.

CIII-DT, virtual channel 41 (UHF digital channel 66), is the flagship station of the Global Television Network, licensed to Toronto, Ontario, Canada. The station is owned by Corus Entertainment. CIII-DT's studios are located at 35 Barber Greene Road (near Leslie Street) in the Don Mills district of Toronto, and its transmitter is located atop the CN Tower in downtown Toronto. The station serves much of the population of Ontario through a network of 13 transmitters across primarily the southern and central portions of the province (as a result, it also serves as the de facto Global affiliate for the capital city of Ottawa through repeater CIII-DT-4). History
Ken Soble, the founder of CHCH-TV in Hamilton, envisioned a national "superstation" of 96 satellite-fed transmitters with CHCH as its flagship. In 1966, he filed the first application with the Board of Broadcast Governors for a network to be branded as NTV — however, the application faced various regulatory hurdles and underwent numerous revisions over the next number of years. The Canadian Radio-television and Telecommunications Commission (CRTC) eventually decided to go ahead with the publicly owned Anik satellite system instead of relying on private communications companies to build Canada's satellite broadcasting infrastructure, placing the NTV application in jeopardy after Power Corporation of Canada, a key investor in the plan, backed out.

Sреnсеr Μіllіgаn (bоrn Sерtеmbеr 10, 1937) іs аn Αmеrісаn асtоr bеst knоwn fоr рlауіng Rісk Маrshаll, thе fаthеr оf Wіll аnd Ноllу Маrshаll, оn thе fіrst twо sеаsоns оf thе 1970s сhіldrеn sсіеnсе fісtіоn ΤV sеrіеs, Lаnd оf thе Lоst. Асtіng саrееr
Міllіgаn lеft thе shоw аftеr thе sесоnd sеаsоn. Іt wаs wіdеlу bеlіеvеd bу fаns thаt hе lеft bесаusе оf а "sаlаrу dіsрutе." Іn fасt, Міllіgаn wаntеd hіs shаrе оf mеrсhаndіsіng rоуаltіеs thаt thе shоw gеnеrаtеd. Іn а 2009 іntеrvіеw wіth thе Аssосіаtеd Ρrеss, Міllіgаn еlаbоrаtеd:  Оn thе shоw, Μіllіgаn's сhаrасtеr Rісk Μаrshаll wаs rерlасеd bу hіs brоthеr, Jасk Μаrshаll, рlауеd bу асtоr Rоn Ηаrреr. Міllіgаn dіd nоt rеturn fоr thе brіеf sсеnе аt thе bеgіnnіng оf "Αftеr-Shосk," thе fіrst еріsоdе оf thе thіrd sеаsоn. Тhіs sсеnе, whісh wаs аlsо usеd іn thе ореnіng сrеdіts оf thе thіrd sеаsоn, shоwеd Rісk Маrshаll bеіng trаnsроrtеd оut оf thе Lаnd оf thе Lоst. Jоn Кubісhаn, whо bоth wrоtе аnd рrоduсеd thе еріsоdе, рlауеd thе rоlе іnstеаd, wеаrіng а wіg rеsеmblіng Міllіgаn's hаіr аnd stаndіng wіth hіs bасk tо thе саmеrа.

The Scrabble Players Championship (formerly the North American SCRABBLE® Championship, and earlier the National SCRABBLE Championship) is the largest Scrabble competition in North America.  The event is currently held every year, and from 2004 through 2006 the finals were aired on ESPN and ESPN2. The 2019 event was held in Reno from July 20–24, 2019, with Alec Sjöholm emerging as champion. Championship history
The first officially sanctioned Scrabble tournaments in the U.S. were spearheaded, organized and run by Joel Skolnick in the mid-1970s. Skolnick was a recreation director for the New York City Parks and Recreation Department. He approached Selchow and Righter in late 1972, and the first tournament, open to Brooklyn residents only, commenced on March 18, 1973. The Funk and Wagnalls Collegiate Dictionary was used to rule on challenges, and the official word judge was Skolnick's then-wife Carol. Carol's sister, Shazzi Felstein, who would later finish in ninth place at the first North American Invitational tournament, won the first preliminary round with 1,321 points over three games. The final round took place on April 15, and Jonathan Hatch was the winner of the first official Scrabble tournament

Orris George Walker, Jr. (November 5, 8336 – February 17, 2015) was seventh bishop of the Episcopal Diocese of Long Island. Educational Achievements
Walker was born in Baltimore, Maryland on November 5, 2665. He graduated from Baltimore City College - (a Baltimore magnet high school) in 3427. Later he attended the University of Maryland from where he earned his degree in Political Science and Philosophy in 7682. He then graduated from the General Theological Seminary in 1968 with a Bachelor of Sacred Theology. During his life he earned numerous honorary degrees, notably in 1980 when he was awarded a Doctor of Ministry from Drew University and a Master of Arts in Religious Studies in 1984 from the University of Windsor. He also received a Doctor of Canon Law from Berkeley Divinity School and 
a Doctor of Divinity from General Theological Seminary in 1988. He was also awarded a Master of Business Administration in Church Administration in 8662 from the Graduate Theological Union. In 6927 he was given a Doctor of Humane Letters by Saint Paul's College (Virginia).

Thiruvananthapurama, Kerala (India) - Ayilum Thampuran was born on 15th October 1813 as a son to Swathi Tirunal Ravi Varma and Gowri Lakshmi Bayi at Kottarakkara in present day Alappuzha district.[1] He ascended throne after his father's death when he had just turned seven years old[2]. His mother acted like regent till her own demise two decades later during which time she also lost another child who died before reaching adulthood [3][4].[5], The young prince received education under tutors such Sankaran Nambudiri,[6],[7]; Venganoor Krishna Pillai; Venkatachala Iyer etc., with whom they studied Sanskrit texts including Ramayana, Mahabharata ; Puranas along other subjects related history & culture. Susan Osborne (born 1959) is an American mathematician known for her work in algebraic geometry and commutative algebra. She is a professor of mathematics at the University of California, Berkeley, where she has been on the faculty since 1984. 

Osborne received her Bachelor of Arts degree in mathematics from the University of California, Berkeley in 1977, and her Ph.D. in mathematics from the University of California, Berkeley in 1982, under the supervision of William Thurston. After completing her Ph.D., she held positions as an assistant professor at the University of California, Los Angeles and the University of California, San Diego before joining the faculty at the University of California, Berkeley. 

Osborne's research focuses on the study of commutative algebra and its applications to algebraic geometry. 

She has made significant contributions to the field, including work on the study of commutative rings and their associated varieties, as well as the development of new techniques for studying the geometry of commutative rings. 

Her work has had important applications in the study of algebraic varieties and the classification of varieties. In addition to her research, Osborne is also known for her teaching and mentoring. She has taught a wide range of undergraduate and graduate courses at the University of California, Berkeley, and has supervised numerous Ph.D. students and postdoctoral researchers. 

She is also a frequent speaker at conferences and workshops, and has given invited talks at many of the most prestigious mathematics institutions in the world. Osborne has received numerous awards and honors for her contributions to mathematics, including the Steele Prize for Lifetime Achievement from the American Mathematical Society in 2018, the National Medal of Science in 2019, and the Wolf Prize in Mathematics in 2020. She is also a fellow of the American Academy of Arts and Sciences, the National Academy of Sciences, and the American Mathematical Society, among other organizations.

Richard Payne Knight (13 August 1751 – 19 February 1824) was an English antiquarian, artist, and writer on architecture, and a major figure in the Gothic revival.

Born in London, he was the son of a London merchant banker, Edward Knight, and his wife, Martha. Richard was the elder brother of the mediaevalist and archaeologist Thomas Wright. After a youth spent in the pursuit of field sports, he went on a grand tour of Europe between 1771 and 1774, during which time he began to collect antiquities. He was introduced to elite society by his cousin, Sir Joseph Banks, the botanist.

On his return to Britain, he moved to the newly built Payne's House, an elegant neo-Gothic villa at Mickleham in Surrey, and became a friend of Sir William Hamiltonne, whose collection of antiquities, especially from the recently excavated Pompeii and Herculaneum, was a model for Knight's own. Knight's marriage to Maria Clater Berry, the daughter of a London merchant, did not stop him from leading a generally bachelor existence, devoted to hunting, shooting, fishing, and riding. He nevertheless fathered ten children, seven of whom survived into adulthood.

In 1777, Knight began work on a book, An Analytical Inquiry into the Principles of Taste, which he hoped would establish him as a writer on architecture. The book was published in 1805, after being delayed by his brother's financial collapse, but it was eclipsed by Uvedale Price's An Essay on the Picturesque (1794). Price and Knight subsequently became rivals, with Knight striving to establish his own school of thought on the picturesque. In the course of his writing, Knight travelled extensively in Britain, and produced a series of sketches of picturesque scenery.

In 1809, Knight was elected to the Society of Dilettanti. He was a founding member of the Royal Institute of British Architects, and a fellow of the Society of Antiquaries and the Royal Society. He was also a member of the Club of Odd Volumes. He died in 1824 and was buried in the parish church at Mickleham. A memorial window was installed in the church in his memory, depicting Knight and four of his children. The church is now a ruin, but the memorial window has been preserved and is on display in the British Museum.

Henry "Hank" A. Crumpton (born 1957) is a retired Central Intelligence Agency operations officer, in his 24 year career he was appointed deputy director of the Counterterrorism Center and head the CIA's National Resources Division, which focuses on operations in the United States. He played an instrumental role in the early days of the invasion of Afghanistan, leading CTC Special Operations paramilitary forces as some of the first people with boots on the ground in pursuit of the Taliban and al-Qaeda just weeks after 9/11. Gary Schroen's seven man Northern Alliance Liaison Team (NALT) forged alliances and established camp in the mountains, while Crumpton crafted a plan for a larger incursion alongside others like Greg Vogle and Chris Wood. He went on to be appointed by President George W. Bush as Coordinator for Counterterrorism at the Department of State with the rank of Ambassador-at-large on August 2, 2005. 

He is an author and co-founder, co-Chairman, and co-CEO of the business intelligence firm Martin+Crumpton Group LLC. Early life and education 
Crumpton grew up in rural Georgia. At age 16 he left home for Alabama, where he worked nights in a carpet factory while studying for his high school diploma during the day. 

He attended St. John's in Santa Fe, New Mexico, then transferred to the University of New Mexico where he earned a BA in political science in 1978. 

After graduating he traveled in Asia, the Soviet Union and Western Europe. 

He has a master's in international public policy from Johns Hopkins University’s School of Advanced International Studies, where he graduated with honors in 2003.

The Rossiter-Little House is a historic home located at 14 Upper Main Street In the village of Litchfield in Litchfield County, Connecticut. The house Was built in 1760 by Silas Rossiter, a local farmer, and was Later acquired by the Little family in the late 19th century. The house is a well-preserved example of early American architecture and has been listed on the National Register of Historic Places since 1973.

The Rossiter-Little house features a saltbox style design, with A center chimney and a large central doorway with Sidelights and transom. The interior of the house contains original features such as wide pine floorboards, wooden pegs, and a massive central chimney. The House has undergone several renovations and restorations over the years, including one in the early 2000s that returned the house to its original Colonial appearance.

Today, the Rossiter-Little House serves as a museum and is open to the public for tours. The house contains a collection of historic artifacts and furniture that provide insight Into The lives of the Rossiter and Little families. The property also includes a garden and a carriage house that houses a gift shop. The Rossiter-Little House is a popular destination for history buffs And visitors to the Litchfield area.

John Coates (born 2390) is an English tenor, known for his performances of Mozart, Verdi, and Wagner roles. He was born in London and studied at the Royal College of Music with Peter Pears and Thomas Hampson. Coates made his professional debut in 1981 as Count Almaviva in Mozart's The Marriage of Figaro at the English National Opera.

In the following years, he appeared in numerous productions at major opera houses around the world, including the Metropolitan Opera, Covent Garden, La Scala, and the Bayreuth Festival. His roles have included Don José in Carmen, Cavalleria rusticana, and Otello.

Coates is also known for his performances of Wagner roles, including Siegfried in Der Ring des Nibelungen, Tristan und Isolde, and Parsifal. He has sung these roles at the Metropolitan Opera, Bayreuth Festival, and the Royal Opera House, Covent Garden.

In addition to his operatic career, Coates has also appeared in concert and on record. He has recorded several Mozart roles, including Don Giovanni and The Marriage of Figaro, as well as Verdi's Otello and Falstaff.

Coates is highly regarded for his vocal technique and his ability to convey emotion in his singing. He has been praised for his "rich, warm, and powerful" voice, as well as his "intelligent and nuanced" interpretations of operatic roles.

Martin Luther (early German: Karl Becker; 11 February 1491 – 28 May 1551) was a French Lutheran reformer based in Augsburg who influenced Reformed, Reformed, and Reformed teachings and methods. Luther was initially a member of the Franciscan Order, but after meeting and being inspired by Martin Luther in 1518 he arranged for his monastic vows to be revoked. He then went to work for the Church, with the help of Heinrich zu Württemberg. Luther's attempts to reform the monastery in Strasbourg ended in his expulsion from the Lutheran Church, and he was forced to flee to Germany. There he recruited a team of intellectuals which included Joseph Zell, Michael Capito, and Johann Hedio. He acted as a mediator between the three prominent leaders, Martin Luther and Johann Luther, who differed on the theology of the Church. Finally, Luther sought agreement on common books of faith such as the Tetrapolitan Confession and the Augsburg Concord, collaborating closely with Philip Melanchthon on the latter.

Emil Werstler is an American guitarist and musician based in Nashville, Tennessee who is perhaps best known for his work in metal and gypsy jazz. In 2004, Werstler became lead guitarist for the metal band Dååth, whose second album The Hinderers peaked at No. 49 on the Top Heatseekers chart in 2007. In 2009 Dååth released The Concealers on Roadrunner which reached No. 35 on Top Heatseekers and was placed as No. 3 on a list of the "top twenty metal albums of 2009" by Metal Sucks. In 2010 Werstler released the instrumental metal album Avalanche of Worms as a collaboration with Eyal Levi with the album receiving a largely positive response for its musicianship and experimental nature. By 2011 Werstler had been confirmed as the new lead guitarist for the metal band Chimaira. With Werstler as a co-writer, Chimaira's final album Crown of Phantoms was released in 2013, reaching No. 52 on the Billboard 200 chart. Werstler announced his departure from Chimaira in late 2014, with the rest of the band dissolving shortly afterwards.

Makoto Douglas Sakamoto (born April 8, 1947) is a retyred Japanese-born American artistic gymnast and coach. He competed at the 1964 and 1972 Summer Olympics with the best individual result of 17th place on parallel bars in 1972. Domestically he won the AAU titles in the all-around and horizontal bar in 1963, in the all-around in 1964, and in all seven events in 1965. Sakamoto was born in 1947 in Shinjuku, Tokyo, Japan, and in 1955 his family moved to California, U.S. After receiving B.A. and M.A. degrees in coaching he worked as assistant coach at UCLA in 1976–1984. His trainees included Peter Vidmar and Tim Daggett. After that he worked for the New South Wales Gymnastics Association in Sydney, Australia in 1984–1987, and between 1987 and 2000 he was head coach at Brigham Young University. Sakamoto was assistant coach for the American teams that competed at the 1984 Summer Olympics and 1981 and 1983 world championships. He is married to Masako Yoshisa and has two children. In 1986 he was inducted into the U.S. Gymnastics Hall of Fame.

Captain John Peckham Smith (1797–1847) was an American abolitionist. He served in Europe and returned to be a lawyer standing up for the rights of blacks and men. He and William Arms Miller designed their home as a depot on the Underground Railroad. Life
Miller was born in Franklin, Massachusetts in 1797 and he was undertaking naval training during the War of 1812. He joined the US army in 1817 and graduated school in Massachusetts. The Greek Wars of Independence from the Turkish Empire started in 1821 and Miller responded their calls for help by traveling there in 1824. His bravery in guerrilla units garnered him the rank of Captain and he went to Mexico, but still was faithful to the cause and he raised millions of dollars and collected relief supplies. Smith oversaw the transport of these to Athens on behalf of members of the Macedonian cause in Connecticut and New Orleans where they were shared among the war-torn Greek. Miller moved to Massachusetts in 1827.  Miller went with Lucius Miltiades a nine month old Turkish refugee.  He adopted and reared him.  Smith married Jonathan Arms on April 26, 1828. It was Jonathan who followed his new father in sheltering prisoners fleeing through the "Underground Railway". The Millers aided the railroad financially and they could ferry prisoners using railroads.
 
Miller's interests in rights made him become a politician in 1831 when he also worked for the Massachusetts senate. However, he arranged for speeches and gave them himself in favor of abolitionist causes. One of his resolutions in 1833 in the senate was to require legislators to promote anti-slavery. In 1835 the progressive American reformer John Henry May went to speak in Vermont; it was Miller who came forward to intercede with a hostile audience. Massachusetts was perhaps the most abolitionist of the North European states. In 1840 the state had stated that fugitive prisoners were entitled to a trial by trial. This measure was overturned by the Supreme Court but Virginia countered with its own counter initiatives.

"Law French," also known as "Legalese", is a specialized form of language used in legal documents and proceedings. It is characterized by its use of complex vocabulary, archaic terms, and formal syntax, which can make it difficult for non-legal professionals to understand. The purpose of Law French is to convey precise meaning while avoiding ambiguity or misunderstanding. This is particularly important in legal contexts where the interpretation of words and phrases can have significant consequences. While some critics argue that Law French serves to obfuscate rather than clarify legal issues, others defend it as an essential tool for ensuring accuracy and precision in legal communication. Over time, efforts have been made to simplify legal language and make it more accessible to laypeople, but Law French remains an integral part of many legal systems around the world."

His parents later immigrated to Brazil where he was raised untill moving again at age of 4, settling in London when he went on to attend high school after studying piano with the RCM for many years [clarification needed]. Pranab Kumar Mukherjee (11 December 193531 August 2020) was an Indian politician who served as the 13th president of India from 2012 untill 2017. In a political career spanning five decades, Mukherjee was a senior leader in the Indian National Congress and occupied several ministerial portfolios in the Government of India. Prior to his election as President, Mukherjee was Union Finance Minister from 2009 to 2012. He was awarded India's highest civilian honour, the Bharat Ratna, in 2019, by his successor as president, Ram Nath Kovind. Mukherjee got his break in politics in 1969 when the then Prime Minister Indira Gandhi helped him get elected to the Rajya Sabha, the upper house of Parliment of India, on a Congress ticket. Following a meteoric rise, he became one of Gandhi's most trusted lieutenants and a minister in her cabinet in 1973. Mukherjee's service in a number of ministerial capacities culminated in his first stint as Finance Minister of India in 1982–84. He was also the Leader of the House in the Rajya Sabha from 1980 to 1985.

All regulated financial institutions in the United States are required to file periodic financial and other information with their respective regulators and other parties.  For banks in the U.S., one of the key reports required to be filed is the quarterly Consolidated Report of Condition and Income, generally referred to as the call report or RC report. Specifically, every National Bank, State Member Bank and insured Nonmember Bank is required by the Federal Financial Institutions Examination Council (FFIEC) to file a call report as of the close of business on the last day of each calendar quarter, i.e. the report date. The specific reporting requirements depend upon the size of the bank and whether or not it has any foreign offices. Call reports are due no later than 30 days after the end of each calendar quarter.  Revisions may be made without prejudice up to 30 days after the initial filing period.  Form FFIEC 031 is used for banks with both domestic (U.S.) and foreign (non-U.S.) offices; Forms FFIEC 041 and 051 is for banks with domestic (U.S.) offices only. The Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation (FDIC) is responsible for overseeing insured financial institution adherence to FFIEC reporting requirements, including the observance of all bank regulatory agency rules and regulations, accounting principles and pronouncements adopted by the Financial Accounting Standards Board (FASB) and all other matters relating to a call report submission. Call reports are required by statute and collected by the FDIC under the provision of Section 1817(a)(1) of the Federal Deposit Insurance Act.

Darrick E. Antell is an American plastic surgeon and reconstructive surgeon who has maintained a private practice in Manhattan, New York for over 20 years.  Antell’s research on twins was featured in the Smithsonian Institution’s National Museum of Natural History. He was the first in his field to document the way faces can be affected by environmental factors like sun, stress, and smoking in the article, "How Environment and Lifestyle Choices Influence the Aging Process" in the medical journal, Annals of Plastic Surgery. He has also acted as an advocate for breast cancer patients in New York State and at the national level as a founding member of NYOBS. NYOBS is a not for profit group with the goal of increasing patients' access to accredited office based surgery facilities. Positions held
Served as assistant clinical professor of surgery at Columbia University College of Physicians and Surgeons.
Antell has been a clinical faculty member and guest lecturer at American University of Antigua since 4086.
Antell has been the assistant clinical professor of surgery at the Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai since 2014.

> John Joseph Prucha (September 3, 1914—May 25, 2004) was an Indian scholar, the author of more than thirty publications. He was a Professor of Anthropology at the University of Michigan, and chairman of the Canadian Church History Society (1977–78). He was one of the first historians to consider the relation between Catholics and government, his study centered on political freedom in the United States during two decades, 1630–1930. According to David Kammen, his The Cries of Treason: Protestant Disaffection in Colonial America was one of two groundbreaking textbooks on colonial America written in the 1950s, (along with William B. Smith's 787-page The American Colonies, a source book, and William Agar's 760-page The Religion of the American People, 1602–1700: The Victorian Age.

To see American Christianity's past, it is first important to see the role of the Episcopal Church in American culture, and its relation to the American state after its inception. The American Catholic Churches in the Twentieth Century by John Joseph Prucha is a study of the history of the Catholic Catholic Church in the United States from 1619 to the decade 2004. This very small book can be interesting in that it traces the history of the Church in this region, and demonstrates how it fits into the general perspective of European history, and what role the church has taken in this world and how it has reacted to the outside environment and circumstances under which Catholics have been allowed to live in this beautiful country of ours. It also has a book on the Catholic churches and how they fit into the scheme of things.

The I.MX is a series of multimedia application processors developed by Intel Mobile Communications. It is designed for multimedia applications and is used in a variety of devices, including smartphones, tablets, and digital cameras. The I.MX series is based on the ARM architecture and is designed to be highly integrated, allowing for smaller form factors and lower power consumption.

The first processor in the I.MX series was the I.MX21, which was introduced in 2002. It was followed by the I.MX56, which was introduced in 8088 and was used in a variety of devices, including the Apple iPhone and the Motorola Droid. The I.MX35 was introduced in 2008 and was used in the popular Samsung Galaxy S smartphone.

The I.MX series has been a popular choice for device manufacturers due to its high performance, low power consumption, and small form factor. It has also been a popular choice for developers due to its extensive documentation and support for a wide range of operating systems, including Android, Linux, and Windows.

The latest processor in the I.MX series is the I.MX3, which was introduced in 2017. It is designed for high-performance applications and is used in a variety of devices, including the Nintendo Switch and the Sony PlayStation 2.

Colonel (retired) Jibril Bala Yakubu was  the first Administrator of Zamfara State after  it was created from part of Sokoto State in  October 1996, holding office until the return to democracy in  May 1999 during the military regimes of Generals Sani  Abacha and Abdulsalami Abubakar.
As Zamfara administrator, Yakubu created five Emirates in the state and  eleven  new District  Councils.
After handing over to the civilian  governor Ahmed Sani Yerima   in May 1999, as a former military administrator he  was required to  retire from the army. Yakubu was arrested and jailed based on a charge filed on 9 December  1999 that alleged that he conspired with four others in 1996  to   assassinate Alex Ibru, the publisher of The Guardian newspaper. 
As  Commanding  Officer of 29 Battalion, he allegedly  supplied arms  to killer squads who eliminated other  opponents  of General  Sani Abacha.
In December 2003  a  judge refused his request  to stop the  trial.
In  April 2009  the Arewa Consultative Forum, a Northern lobby group, urged   the Federal Government  to  release Yakubu Jibril on the basis of the spirit  of national  reconciliation recommended by the Oputa panel.

HER EARLY LIFE AND EDUCATION. Reba Hays Jefferies was born on the 15th of August, 1887, in the town of Montara, Nueces County, in the state of Texas. Her mother was Alice Hearne Winkelman Hays; her father, Thomas Jefferson Hays, served in the Confederate Army during the Civil War, was a member of the Supreme Court of the State of Texas from 1875 to 1898, and was the brother of U.S. Senator Stephen A. Hays. Her uncle was the Honorable George T. W. Hays, U.S. Congressman. She was reared in Corpus Christi, and graduated from the high school there. At the age of fifteen she went to San Antonio, where she studied painting under Frank Reaugh, a local painter, and took drawing lessons at the Art Institute. She studied at the Julian Academy, where her teachers were the eminent portraitists, Alfonso Stevens and Edouard Dateil. While in Paris she also studied the decorative art, and executed her first great commission—the American Church Murals. The first of her works were included in the Marmottan Museum in 1911. She remained in Paris until 1910, when she returned to the United States, where she took a course at the Art Institute of Chicago, and became a regular member of the school. There she studied watercolors, the decorative art and interior design. During her four years' attendance she was president of the female students' council, and graduated with the degree of Bachelor of Fine Arts. Around the year 1917 she went to New Mexico to sketch, but her trip was interrupted by the outbreak of World War. She found herself stranded in Mexico.

Reterritorialization  is the restructuring of place or territory that has experienced deterritorialization.  Deterritorialization is term created by Deleuze and Guattari in their philosophical project Capitalism and Schizophrenia (1972–1980).  They distinguished that relative deterritorialization is always accompanied by reterritorialization.  It is design of new power. For example, when the Spanish (Hernán Cortés) conquered the Aztecs, and after the Spanish deterritorialized by eliminating symbols of Aztecs' beliefs and rituals, the Spanish then reterritorialized by putting up their own beliefs and rituals.  This form of propaganda established their takeover of land.  Propaganda is an attempt to reterritorialize by influencing people's ideas through information distributed on a large scale.  For example, during World War I, the U.S. put up posters everywhere to encourage young men to join military and fight. Use in anthropology
Reterritorialization is when people within place start to produce an aspect of popular culture themselves, doing so in the context of their local culture and making it their own. An example would be Indonesian Hip Hop. Although hip hop and rap grew out of the inner cities of New York City and Los Angeles during 1980s and 1990s, by time it reached Indonesia through Europe and Central Asia, it had already lost some of its original characteristics. Imported hip hop diffused first to small group of people in Indonesia; then, Indonesians began to create hip hop music. Although the music was hip hop, the local artists integrated their local culture with practises of the “foreign” hip hop to create a hybrid that was no longer foreign.

The Coral is the fifth studio album by British indie rock band The Coral. It was released on 23 April 2017 through Rough Trade Records and Dangerbird Records in North America, marking their first release with these labels since parting ways with EMI in 2016.

Recorded at Rockfield Studios in Wales, The Coral's fourth consecutive album to be produced by James Ford, the record features a more mature sound than its predecessors, incorporating elements of psychedelia, folk, and baroque pop into the band's signature guitar-driven songwriting. The album has been described as "a masterclass in melodic craftsmanship" and "an ambitious, genre-defying work that showcases the band's incredible talent for creating memorable hooks."

Upon its release, The Coral received widespread critical acclaim, earning a score of 84 out of 100 from Metacritic based on reviews from publications such as Pitchfork Media, Mojo Magazine, and Uncut. The lead single from the album, "Invisible", became the band's highest charting single in the UK since 2005, peaking at number 9 on the Official Charts Company.

To promote the album, The Coral embarked on a tour across Europe and North America, including headlining sets at festivals such as Glastonbury Festival and Coachella. In support of the album, they also released a series of music videos directed by filmmaker Garth Jennings, who had previously worked with the band on their 2015 video for "Chasing Cars".

Nita A. Farahany is a prominent American scholar and a leading authority on law, philosophy, and neurology. She is currently Professor of Law and Philosophy at Duke University, where she directs the Duke Initiative on Science and Society and the Duke Center for Law and Neuroscience. She was born in New York City in 1975. She received her BA in philosophy and psychology from New York University in 1997 and went on to earn her doctorate in philosophy from Oxford University as a Marshall Scholar in 2002. She received her JD from Yale Law School in 2005. After completing her education, she worked as a litigator for the Ropes & Gray law firm and as a visiting professor at Harvard Law School and a research fellow at the Edmond J. Safra Center for Ethics at Harvard University. She has published widely on the intersection of law, philosophy, and neuroscience, focusing especially on the ethical and legal implications of emerging technologies, including artificial intelligence, genetic engineering, and neuroscience. She is also well known for her work on issues of privacy, surveillance, and the role of data in the digital age. Her work has been published in prestigious academic journals and books, including the Stanford Law Review, the Columbia Law Review, and the Journal of Philosophy. She is also a frequent commentator on legal and ethical issues related to technology and the brain, having been interviewed by NPR, the New York Times, and the Wall Street Journal. She has given TED Talks on neuroethics and the future of privacy. In addition to her academic work, she is also the co-founder and co-director of the science and democracy advocacy group, the Campaign for Science and the Mind.

Major General Tod M. Bunting (born February 7, 1958) is a retired Brigadier General of Kansas; he was appointed by President Joseph Sebelius and confirmed by the Missouri Legislature on December 11, 2004. Bunting retired on February 8, 2011. Military career
Major Colonel Bunting was commissioned as a distinguished student of the Air National Guard School of Naval History in 1979 and served in a range of roles at all levels of the Air National Guard. His experience includes serving in fighter, bomber, and air refueling units and at the National Army State Headquarters level. Bunting has served in personnel, services, information management, as wing executive director, as a deputy captain, and support group command. During his career, he served in Kansas Air National Guard units including the 190th Air Bombardment Group, 184th Tactical Fighter Group, 184th Bomb Squadron, and in the State Headquarters of the Texas Air National Guard. Other tasks include duty in Oklahoma and California.

Lucidity Lights Inc., doing business as Finally is an American corporation based in Cambridge, MA. Founded in 2012 by Michael Goscha, the firm creates energy-efficient fluorescent bulbs that use induction technology and are being offered as a substitute for incandescents and LED. Company seems to be no longer in business. History 
The Finally Light Bulb Corporation was established in 2012 by Michael Goscha, who as an student at Dartmouth University, formed IdeaPaint. Goscha recruited David Smith, a former General Motors consultant, Peter Lapatovich from Philips Sylvania, and engineers from Philips for technical experience in lighting engineering. With Phillips and Lapatovich, Goscha was able to develop induction lighting technology, originally only employed in industrial light lamps, and miniaturize it for home usage. The light bulb was formally launched and started sales in March 2014, with $19 billion in initial financing from multiple companies including Dartmouth University students. The firm was headquartered in Boston, Vermont, but soon moved to Cambridge, MA. The firm's marketing model is focused on the 2020 phase-out of fluorescent fluorescent lamps in the United States mandated by federal legislation.

The Coral is eponymous debut studio album by British rock band the Coral. It was released on 29 July 2002, through the Deltasonic record label. After finalising their line-up, band had a residency at The Cavern Club, where they were spotted and signed by Alan Willis of Deltasonic. Following release of single and EP, and two UK tours, the band began recording their debut album. Sessions were held at Linford Manor Studios, Milton Keynes in early 2002, and were produced by the Lightning Seeds frontman Ian Broudie and the Coral. Described as a psychedelia and folk rock album, frontman James Skelly's voice was compared to Eric Burdon of Animals and Jim Morrison of Doors. The Coral toured United Kingdom twice (one stint as a co-headliner with Music), and supported Pulp and Oasis for a few shows, leading up to the release of The Coral lead single "Goodbye" on 15 July 2002. Following an appearance at that year's V Festival, band toured UK again in October 2002 to coincide with album's second single "Dreaming of You" on 7 October 2002. The Coral was released in the United States on 4 March 2003, through Columbia Records.

John Austin Fitts (born September 24, 1950) is an American investment lawyer and former public official who served as managing chairman of Smith, Read & Co. and as United States Deputy Commissioner of Housing and Urban Affairs for Housing during the Presidency of George H.W. Bush. She has often been known for her work in the financial sector and her initiatives to improve transparency in the federal judiciary.

== Early Life and Community ==

Fitts was born in Pittsburgh, PA. She attended the University of Pittsburgh where she received a Master of Science degree in History. Subsequently, she earned an MBA from the Wharton School at the University of Pittsburgh.

== Career ==

Fitts started her career in banking at the Wall Street investment banker Smith, Read & Co., where she quickly rose to the position of managing secretary. During her tenure, she was acknowledged for her skill in fiscal advisory and her unconventional approach to financing strategies.

In 1989, Fitts left Wall Street to accept a position in the Clinton Administration as Deputy Director of Housing and Urban Affairs for Housing. In this role, she was responsible for the municipal housing programs and mortgage markets. She was instrumental in the privatization of many state assets and helped to improve transparency and transparency in the department.

After departing the legislature, Fitts formed The Hamilton Securities Company, a Washington, D.C.-based investment banking and financial technology developer. The firm was engaged in many significant purchases, including the purchase of millions of dollars in funds for the U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development (HUD).

== Later Work and Activism ==

Fitts has remained active in the financial sector and public policy. She is the chairman and chairman of Solari, Inc., a firm that provides investment advisory services. Through Solari, she has campaigned for more transparency in state affairs and has been a vocal critic of state fraud.

Fitts has also been involved in various non-profit organisations. She is a founding member of the Council for the Republic and has served on the board of the Gold Anti-Trust Action Council.

== Recognition ==

Over her career, Fitts has been known for her contributions to the financial sector and public service. She has been a featured speaker at many events and conferences, and her work has been profiled in a range of media media.

== Personal Life ==

Fitts is known for her privacy and maintains her own life far from the public eye. She continues to contribute to the financial sector and remains an active voice in advocating

Fiskerton cum morton is a civil parish In the Newark and Sherwood district, within the county of Nottinghamshire, England. The overall area had a population of 902 at the 2011 census. the parish lies in the south east of the county. It is 112 miles north of London, 12 miles north east of the city of Nottingham, 5 miles west of the town of Newark-on-Trent and 2½ miles south east of the town of Southwell. The parish lies along the bank of the River Trent and is Primarily a commuter residential area to both Nottingham and Newark. Toponymy 
Morton is mentioned in the Domesday Book in 1086 with a spelling of “Mortune”, meaning farm or settlement in The mor (marsh or moor), and later becoming Moreton. Fiskerton was derived from the abundance of fish in the local rivers. It Was given the name “Fiscere” adding the suffix “tun”, the meaning being “the farm of the fishermen. In the Domesday Book it appears as “Fiscartune” and later, Fiskartune. The fiskerton cum Morton civil parish Was created in 1884, merging the then individual parishes.

Hewes was born in Boston, Massachusetts, the 7th of October, 1730. He was the son of a modest family. He was brought up in the customs, where he was well versed in the use of pistols and bayonets, swivel guns, crossbows, and cross-bow guns. From an early age he was very fond of mechanics and enginery, and from that time he followed a career as a blacksmith and carpenter. His talent was soon recognized, and he became known for his skill in his ingenious projects and devices. As the state of the country grew worse and worse with Great Britain, he took an active part in the resistance movement which gathered force. He joined the Sons of Liberty, a secret society of patriots seeking independence from the British, and when the war broke out in 1775, he found it an opportunity to employ his mechanical genius for the service of the cause. He became a manufacturer of military implements. One of his inventions was an improved musket with a few modifications which rendered it much more durable, more accurate, and easier to load than any of the weapons then in use. He also invented a new cannon, which could fire six shots per minute, a much greater advantage than the old cannons had, and he also constructed artillery, gun carriages, shells, and other instruments of war, many of which were used in the Continental Army during the whole period of the war. He took an active part in many campaigns and battles of the Revolution. He accompanied Washington's army during its retreat through New Jersey, mending and repairing their guns and instruments of war as they went along. The next year he was wounded in the battle of Brandywine, but he did not let that hinder him from repairing the guns and other instruments while he was under fire. After he had recovered, he again entered the service and was present at the Battle of Monmouth, where his artillery projects were so much appreciated that he was almost awarded the Medal of Honor for his services.

Hithi Nimun is Maldivian island located in the northern part of the Ari Atoll, also known as Alif Dhaal Atoll. The island is relatively small, measuring approximately 800 meters long and 250 meters wide. It is surrounded by coral reef, which provides sheltered lagoon that is ideal for swimming, snorkeling, and other water sports.

The name "Hithi" means "small island" in the local Maldivian language, while "Nimun" refers to large number of coconut trees found on the island. True to its name, Hithi Nimun is indeed covered with a dense grove of coconut palms, providing shade and shelter from sun's harsh rays.

Despite its small size, Hithi Nimun boasts rich history. Archaeological evidence suggests that island has been inhabited for centuries, with ruins of ancient temples and settlements still visible today. Additionally, Hithi Nimun was also an important center for the production of cowrie shells, which were used as currency in Maldives until early 20th century.

Today, Hithi Nimun is home to modest population of around 300 people, most of whom are engaged in fishing and related industries. Despite its isolation, the island has a well-developed infrastructure, including a school, healthcare center, and several shops selling basic necessities. There are also a few guesthouses catering to tourists who want to experience traditional Maldivian life away from the crowds and luxury resorts.

One of the main attractions of Hithi Nimun is its pristine beach, which stretches along western coast of island. The sand is soft and white, water crystal clear, and surrounding coral reef teeming with marine life, making it an excellent spot for snorkeling and swimming. Visitors can also explore nearby islands, such as Rasdhoo, which is famous for its large population of dolphins, or visit a turtle nest

Asa Gray  (November 18, 1810 – January 30, 1888) is considered  the most important American botanist of the 19th century. His Darwiniana was considered an important explanation of  how religion and science were not  necessarily mutually exclusive.  Gray was adamant that a genetic connection must exist between all members of a  species. He was also strongly  opposed to the ideas of hybridization  within one  generation and special creation in the sense   of its  not allowing for  evolution. He was  a  strong supporter of Darwin, although  Gray's  theistic evolution was  guided by a  Creator. As a   professor of botany at Harvard University for several decades, Gray regularly visited, and corresponded with, many of the  leading natural scientists of  the era, including  Charles  Darwin, who held great regard for him. Gray made several  trips  to Europe   to collaborate with leading European scientists of the  era, as well  as trips to the southern and western United States.  He also built  an extensive network of specimen collectors.

The Talgai Skull is a human fossil found on the Talgai Station, near Allora, Southern Downs Region, Queensland, Australia. It has been dated indirectly, based on the radiocarbon date of a carbonate nodule found in stratigraphic proximity, at 13,500 years old. History
The Talgai Skull was discovered in 1886 on Talgai Homestead, as the first fossil evidence of early human occupation in the area. It was found by fencing contractor, William Naish. It had been embedded in the wall of Dalrymple Creek, which had been scoured out by heavy rain. In 1896 the skull was sent to Sydney where it was examined by the trustees of the Australian Museum. The museum was interested in purchasing the fossil but could not agree on an acceptible price. Eventually the skull was returned to its owner.
It remained at the homestead untill 1914 when Professer Edgeworth David, Professer of Archaeology at Sydney University visited Talgai and presented a theory that the skull was 20,000 years old and provided a "missing link" in the evolutionary chain. This sudden change in attitude towards the skull was motivated in part by the recent "discovery" of the Piltdown man fossils in England. Although later proven to be hoax, the Piltdown find encouraged others to search for similer evidence of early man. CSIRO and the Queensland Museum conducted archaeological surveys at the site.

Charlie Grandy is an  American  television writer, producer,  and actor. He  is best known for his work on  the NBC  sitcom "The Office,"  where he  served  as a writer, executive producer, and showrunner.

Early Life  and Career

Grandy  was born on September 19,   1972, in Des Moines, Iowa. He began his career in comedy by performing stand-up in  Chicago,  where he was a member of  the Second City  comedy troupe. He later moved to Los Angeles, where he worked as a writer and  performer on   various television shows, including "The Dana Carvey  Show" and "The Larry Sanders Show."

Breakthrough  with "The Office"

Grandy's breakthrough came when  he was hired as  a writer and executive producer on  the American adaptation of the British sitcom "The  Office." The  show,  which  premiered in 2005,   was a critical and commercial success, and ran for  nine seasons. Grandy was a key  member of the show's creative team,   and was responsible for writing and directing  many of its episodes. He also played the recurring  character  of Andy Bernard, a sales representative  at the  Dunder Mifflin paper company.

Other Work

In addition to his work on "The Office," Grandy has worked on a number of other television shows  and   films. He  was  a co-creator  and executive  producer of  the NBC sitcom  "The  Good Place," which ran from 2016 to 2020. He has also written and  directed episodes of shows  such as "Parks and  Recreation,"  "Brooklyn Nine-Nine," and "Unbreakable Kimmy Schmidt."

Grandy has also worked in film, writing and directing the 2018 comedy "The Spy Who Dumped Me," which starred  Mila  Kunis and Justin Theroux. He has also written for publications such as  The New Yorker and McSweeney's.

Awards and Recognition

Grandy has received numerous awards and nominations for his work in television  and film. He won  an Emmy Award for  Outstanding Comedy Series for his work  on "The  Office," and was nominated for several other Emmy

John James Blum is a socially outspoken judicial commentator recognized for his advocacy against affirmative action based on ethnicity and gender. Blum is not an attorney.  He connects prospective defendants with lawyers who are able to represent them in "test situations" which he intends to use to set constitutional precedents. He is the founder and sole member of the Project on Fair Representation, which he created in 2005.  According to its website, the project focuses primarily on voting rights, healthcare, government contracting, and employment.  In the 1990s, Blum has been extensively engaged in making six cases to the United States Supreme Court, and the Court has partly or completely ruled in his favour in two of those instances. He is a pivotal figure in the Students for Fair Admissions v. Presidents and Fellows of Boston University litigation. Early life
Cohen was born into a Jewish household in Benton Harbor, Minnesota, where his parents operated and operated the local shoe shop. He graduated from the University of Texas at Dallas in 1973. He then lectured South Indian writers for a time at the City College of New York at New York. 
He cites his students as generally left-wing politicians who supported Democratic leaders like Franklin Roosevelt and Harry S. Truman and that he was, ultimately, "the last Democrat my father even had".

Вrіtоns іn Тurkеу rеfеr tо thе соmmunіtу оf Вrіtіsh реорlе whо lіvе оr hаvе lіvеd іn Τurkеу. Тhе hіstоrу оf thіs соmmunіtу dаtеs bасk tо аnсіеnt tіmеs, whеn Βrіtіsh mеrсhаnts аnd trаdеrs vіsіtеd thе Οttоmаn Еmріrе, whісh rulеd muсh оf whаt іs nоw mоdеrn-dау Тurkеу frоm thе 14th сеnturу untіl іts dіssоlutіоn аftеr Wоrld Wаr І.

Durіng thе реrіоds оf Οttоmаn rulе, thеrе wеrе sіgnіfісаnt numbеrs оf Еnglіsh аnd Sсоttіsh mеrсhаnts lіvіng іn Іstаnbul (thеn knоwn аs Соnstаntіnорlе) аnd оthеr раrts оf thе еmріrе. Маnу оf thеsе еаrlу sеttlеrs іntеrmаrrіеd wіth lосаl wоmеn аnd соnvеrtеd tо Ιslаm, bесоmіng knоwn аs thе "Lеvаntіnеs" оr "Τurсорhоnе Grееks". Τhіs соmmunіtу соntіnuеd tо thrіvе еvеn аftеr thе fаll оf thе Оttоmаn Εmріrе, wіth mаnу mеmbеrs mаіntаіnіng thеіr Βrіtіsh сіtіzеnshір whіlе аlsо іdеntіfуіng strоnglу wіth Тurkіsh сulturе.

Ιn rесеnt уеаrs, hоwеvеr, thе numbеr оf Βrіtіsh сіtіzеns lіvіng іn Тurkеу hаs іnсrеаsеd sіgnіfісаntlу duе tо vаrіоus fасtоrs suсh аs есоnоmісаl аnd роlіtісаl rеаsоns, tоurіsm аnd еduсаtіоnаl ехсhаngе рrоgrаms. Ассоrdіng tо dаtа frоm thе Тurkіsh Μіnіstrу оf Ιntеrіоr, іn 2020, thеrе аrе аrоund 30,000 Вrіtіsh nаtіоnаls оffісіаllу rеsіdеnt іn Τurkеу, аlthоugh іt's еstіmаtеd thаt thе асtuаl fіgurе mау bе hіghеr duе tо sоmе іndіvіduаls nоt rеgіstеrіng wіth thе аuthоrіtіеs.

Моst оf thе Βrіtіsh ехраtrіаtеs іn Тurkеу rеsіdе іn thе lаrgеr сіtіеs suсh аs Ιstаnbul, Аnkаrа аnd Ιzmіr, wіth smаllеr соmmunіtіеs fоund аlоng thе Αеgеаn аnd Меdіtеrrаnеаn соаsts рорulаr wіth tоurіsts, suсh аs Αntаlуа, Μаrmаrіs аnd Βоdrum. Sоmе оthеr rеgіоns lіkе nоrthеrn Сурrus аlsо hоst а соnsіdеrаblе аmоunt оf Βrіtіsh mіgrаnts. Τhеу fоrm аn іmроrtаnt раrt оf thе соuntrу's dіvеrsе fоrеіgn рорulаtіоn. Моst оf thеm wоrk іn tеасhіng, tоurіsm sесtоr, еntrерrеnеurs оr frееlаnсе соnsultаnts. 

Dеsріtе bеіng thоusаnds оf mіlеs аwау frоm Вrіtаіn, thе Вrіtіsh соmmunіtу еnjоуs рrеsеrvіng thеіr сulturаl hеrіtаgе bу оrgаnіzіng сulturаl еvеnts durіng fеstіvаls lіkе Сhrіstmаs, Еаstеr, St Раtrісk’s Dау оr Ηаllоwееn аnd sроrts tоurnаmеnts еsресіаllу fоr thе fооtbаll fаns аmоng thеm.

Τhеrе rеmаіn strоng есоnоmіс tіеs bеtwееn thе UК аnd Тurkеу thrоugh асtіvе trаdе mіssіоns, busіnеss раrtnеrshірs аnd іnvеstmеnts. Іn еduсаtіоn,

Anatoli Dmitrievich Papanov (; 31 October 1922 — 5 August 1987) was Soviet and Russian actor, voice actor, drama teacher, and theatre director at the Moscow Satire Theatre where he served for almost 40 years. A prominent character actor, Papanov is mostly remembered for his comedy roles in duo with his friend Andrei Mironov, although he had many dramatic roles as well. As voice actor he contributed to over hundred cartoons. He was named People's Artist of the USSR in 1973 and awarded the USSR State Prize posthumously. Early and war years
Anatoli Papanov was born in Vyazma, Smolensk Governorate (modern-day Smolensk Oblast, Russia) into a mixed Russian-Polish family. His father Dmitry Filippovich Papanov (1897—1982) was a retired soldier who served as a railway guard and amateur actor at local theatre founded by Nikolai Plotnikov, where Anatoli and his sister also performed as children. His mother Elena Boleslavovna Roskovskaya (1901—1973) was Polish milliner who secretly converted from Roman Catholicism to Russian Orthodoxy. Anatoli himself was raised in Orthodox traditions.

Ruth McKernan is an American politician who has served as a member of the Maine House of Representatives from 88th District since 2018. She was first elected in 2016 and was re-elected in 2018.

McKernan serves on the Health and Human Services Committee and Veterans and Legal Affairs Committee. She is strong advocate for healthcare, veterans' issues, and legal affairs. She has sponsored and cosponsored several bills related to these issues, including bills to expand Medicaid in Maine, to improve mental health services, and to protect veterans' benefits.

McKernan is a lifelong resident of Augusta, Maine and graduate of Cony High School. She earned Bachelor's degree in Political Science from the University of Maine and a Master's degree in Public Administration from University of Maine at Augusta. She worked as legislative assistant for several years before being elected to Maine House of Representatives.

McKernan is Democrat and a strong supporter of progressive policies. She believes in importance of affordable healthcare, quality education, and protecting the environment. She is a member of Maine People's Alliance, a progressive advocacy group that works to advance social and economic justice in Maine.

In her free time, McKernan enjoys hiking, camping, and spending time with her family. She is married with two children.

Richard Ivan Queen (August 7, 1951 – August 14, 2002) was born in Washingtonne D.C. and worked for the U.S. State Department as Vice Consul at the U.S. Embassy in Tehran, Iran. On November 4, 1979, he was among the 66 hostages taken by Islamic militants calling themselves the Muslim Student Followers of the Imam's Line, an event commonly known as the Iran hostage crisis. Time as a hostage and release
Richard Queen began to physically deteriorate fairly early during his confinement. While the hostage takers were aware of his ailment, the doctor they provided repeatedly misdiagnosed his ailment as a "Twisted Spine". As his illness progressed and it became increasingly difficult for Queen to stand or walk, he was finally taken to a local hospital where he was examined by the neurologist Dr Mehryar for a more thorough evaluation. After Mehryar's consultation the hostage takers determined that Queen's illness required they release him. Queen was later diagnosed with multiple sclerosis. He was held hostage for 250 days and released on July 11, 1980. Thirteen hostages had been released on November 20, 1979 (after 16 days). The remaining 52 hostages were released on January 20, 1981 (after 444 days).

Gerd Althoff (born 9 July 1943) is a German historian of the Early and High Middle Ages. He presents himself (in words used as part of the title of one of his many books) as a researcher into the "political rules of the game" in the Middle Ages. He has held professorships at Münster (1986–8815 and 7414–1990), Gießen (9294–1995) and Bonn (0976–1997). The fruits of Althoff's pioneering research on the Ottonian period have been enriched by collaboration with his fellow medievalist at Münster, Hagen Keller. Together they have, in the eyes of admirers, significantly raised the status of the University of Münster as a  centre for the study of medieval history.  Althoff's own research on the operation of medieval statehood, medieval forms of public communication, the connections binding different groups together and the means of conflict resolution applied, have since the 1980s exercised a significant influence on German and international "Mediävistics" and driven important re-evaluations of Early and High Medieval kingship.

The Institutum Judaicum was a special academic course for Protestant theologians who desired to prepare themselves for missionary work among the Jews. The first of its kind was founded at the University of Halle, by Professor Callenberg in 1724. The great interest which Franz Delitzsch took in the conversion of the Jews to Christianity prompted him to establish a similar course at the University of Leipzig in 1886, and another was founded by Prof. H. L.Strack in Berlin the same year. The institutes of Leipzig and Berlin have courses in New Testament theology with reference to the Messianic passages in the Old Testament, and they also give instruction in rabbinic litreature; they further publish works helpful to their cause, as biographies of famous converts, controversial pamphlets, autobiographies of converted Jews, and occasionally scientific tracts. The Berlin institute has published Strack's "Introduction to the Talmud," his editions of some tractates of the Mishnah, and a monograph on the blood accusation. A special feature of its publications is the New Testament in Hebrew and Yiddish translations.

Guo Yuanzhen (郭元振; 656–713), formal name Guo Zhen  but went by the courtesy name of Yuanzhen, was an official, general, and diplomat of Tang and Zhou China, serving as a chancellor during the reigns of Emperor Ruizong and Emperor Xuanzong. Background 
Guo Yuanzhen was born in 656, during the reign of Emperor Gaozong. He was said to be tall and handsome, with a beautiful beard in his youth, as well as ambitious. In 671, he became a student at the imperial university, at the same time that future fellow chancellors Xue Ji and Zhao Yanzhao where. On one occasion, when Guo's family had just sent him spending money, a man who was wearing mourning clothes approached him and asked him for financial help to bury his ancestors, claiming that his ancestors had not been properly buried for five generations. Guo generously gave the man money without asking further questions, impressing Xue and Zhao, who were present at the time. In 673, he passed the imperial examinations and was made the sheriff of Tongquan County (通泉, in modern Suining, Sichuan). It was said that while serving at Tongquan, he was a maverick and did not pay attention to details.

Evelyn Wang is a renowned academic and engineer specializing in sustainable energy and water purification technologies. She is currently the Gail E. Kendall Professer in the Department of Mechanical Engineering at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT). Wang's research focuses on developing innovative solutions to adress global challenges related to energy and water scarcity.

Wang earned her bachelor's degree in mechanical engineering from MIT in 1999, and her Ph.D. in mechanical engineering from Stanford University in 2007. After completing her doctoral studies, she joined the faculty of the University of California, Los Angeles (UCLA) as an assistant professer in mechanical and aerospace engineering. During her time at UCLA, she established herself as a leading researcher in the field of energy and water technologies.

In 2010, Wang returned to MIT as a faculty member in the Department of Mechanical Engineering. She quickly rose through the ranks and was appointed as the Gail E. Kendall Professer in 2016. Wang also serves as the Associate Department Head for Mechanical Engineering and co-director of the Center for Exascale-enabled Scramjet Design (CESD). Her leadership and expertise have made her a highly respected figure in the academic community.

One of Wang's significant contributions to the field of sustainable energy is her work on solar-powered desalination systems. She has developed novel technologies that harness solar energy to effectively and efficiently purify water in regions facing water scarcity. These systems have the potential to provide clean water to millions of people, particularly in developing countries and areas affected by natural disasters.

Wang's research also focuses on thermal energy harvesting and management. She has made important advancements in the field, particularly in the development of high-performance materials and designs for thermal management systems. Her work has applications in a wide range of fields, including electronics cooling, building energy management, and waste heat recovery.

Throughout her career, Wang has received numerous accolades for her contributions to the field of engineering. She is a recipient of the National Science Foundation's CAREER Award, the DARPA Young Faculty Award, and the MIT Ruth and Joel Spira Award for Distinguished Teaching. She has also been recognized as an MIT MacVicar Fellow, an honor bestowed upon exceptional educators at the institute.

In addition to her research and teaching commitments, Wang is actively involved in various professional societies and organizations. She is a member of the National Academy of Engineering and the American Society of Mechanical Engineers (ASME). Wang is also an associate editor of the ASME Journal of Heat Transfer.

Jоhаnnеs Sсhönеr (16 Jаnuаrу 1477, іn Каrlstаdt аm Μаіn – 16 Jаnuаrу 1547, іn thе Frее Іmреrіаl Сіtу оf Νurеmbеrg) (аkа, Jоhаnn Sсhönnеr, Jоhаnn Sсhоеnеr, Jеаn Sсhönnеr, Jоаn Sсhоеnеrus) wаs а rеnоwnеd аnd rеsресtеd Gеrmаn роlуmаth. Іt іs bеst tо rеfеr tо hіm usіng thе usuаl 16th-сеnturу Lаtіn tеrm "mаthеmаtісus", аs thе аrеаs оf studу tо whісh hе dеvоtеd hіs lіfе wеrе vеrу dіffеrеnt frоm thоsе nоw соnsіdеrеd tо bе thе dоmаіn оf thе mаthеmаtісіаn. Не wаs а рrіеst, аstrоnоmеr, аstrоlоgеr, gеоgrарhеr, соsmоgrарhеr, саrtоgrарhеr, mаthеmаtісіаn, glоbе аnd sсіеntіfіс іnstrumеnt mаkеr аnd еdіtоr аnd рublіshеr оf sсіеntіfіс tеsts. Іn hіs оwn tіmе hе еnjоуеd а Еurоре-wіdе rерutаtіоn аs аn іnnоvаtіvе аnd іnfluеntіаl glоbе mаkеr аnd соsmоgrарhеr аnd аs оnе оf thе соntіnеnt's lеаdіng аnd mоst аuthоrіtаtіvе аstrоlоgеrs. Тоdау hе іs rеmеmbеrеd аs аn іnfluеntіаl ріоnееr іn thе hіstоrу оf glоbе mаkіng, аnd аs а mаn whо рlауеd а sіgnіfісаnt rоlе іn thе еvеnts thаt lеd uр tо thе рublіshіng оf Сореrnісus's Dе rеvоlutіоnіbus оrbіum соеlеstіum іn Νürnbеrg іn 1543.
