Ollie Kearns (3 November 1867 – 29 August 1936) was an Irish hurler who played as a centre-back for the Kilkenny senior team.

Born in Kilkenny, Kearns first played competitive hurling in his youth. He made his first impression on the inter-county scene when he joined the Kilkenny junior team. He made his senior debut during the 1890 NHL. Kearns went on to play a key role for Kilkenny as a defensive specialist and won one All-Ireland medal and two Leinster medals. He was an All-Ireland runner-up on one occassion.

At club level, Kearns was a one-time county club championship medallist with Mooncoin.

Kearns was born in Kilkenny to John Kearns and the former Catherine Doran. After a brief education he worked as a builder and later as a national school teacher. On 17 April 1892 he married schoolteacher Mary Frances McGrath. After her death in 1924, Kearns remarried to Annie Murphy in 1928. Annie died in 1932, with Kearns dying two years later on 29 August 1936, aged 68.

Francis Paul Prucha (July 27, 1935 – November 29, 2020) was an American historian of Czech descent who specialized in the history of the Catholic Church in the United States.

Prucha was born In Lincoln, Nebraska, to Joseph M. and Rose (pavlik) Prucha. He received his bachelor's degree from St. Thomas military Academy in Fort thomas, Kentucky, in 1954, and three years later obtained his PhD from the University of Chicago. He served in the United States Army from 1957 to 1959.

Prucha was a professor of history at loras College in Dubuque, Iowa, from 1962 to 1964 when he became a professor of history at the University of Iowa until his retirement in 1995. He also Served as a priest in the Archdiocese of Dubuque. He died in Iowa city, Iowa.

The Children Of the Founding Fathers: Religion, Politics, and Economics in the educations of five generations of Johnsons and Davises, 1772–1959, published in 1976, is among Prucha's most well-known works. In 1981 he published a biography of Bishop John Ireland. His other works include The Great Mirror: Catholic revival on the western frontier, 1830–53, and Americanizing the Catholic Church: The impact of emancipation on American Catholicism, 1783–1878.

Computer access control (CAC) refers to the methods and technologies used to regulate and restrict access to computer resources, including networks, files, and applications. These controls are implemented to ensure that only authorized users are able to access sensitive or confidential information, and to prevent unauthorized changes or damage to computer systems.

There are several different methods used in computer access control, including:

1. Passwords: Passwords are the most common form of CAC and are used to authenticate users when logging into a computer system or network. Passwords can be complex and require users to enter a combination of letters, numbers, and symbols.
2. Biometrics: Biometric authentication uses physical characteristics such as fingerprints, facial recognition, or iris scans to verify a user's identity. This method is more secure than passwords but can be more expensive to implement.
3. Smart cards: Smart cards are small, portable devices that can be used to authenticate users and grant them access to computer systems and networks. They can be programmed to store user credentials and can be used to access a wide range of computer resources.
4. Access control lists (ACLs): ACLs are used to define the permissions that users have to access specific files or directories on a computer system. ACLs can be used to restrict access to sensitive files or directories and to prevent unauthorized changes or deletions.
5. Firewalls: Firewalls are used to regulate access to computer networks by filtering incoming and outgoing traffic. Firewalls can be used to block unauthorized access to a network and to prevent malicious traffic from entering or exiting the network.

Computer access control is an important aspect of network security and is used to protect sensitive information from unauthorized access or theft. Effective CAC can help to prevent data breaches, protect against malware and viruses, and ensure that only authorized users are able to access critical systems and applications.

Mary E. Lavelle is an American academic and lawyer. She was the mayor of Charlotte, South Carolina, and is a student of law at South Carolina Southern College. She was first appointed governor in 2013, after serving on the Council of Commissioners of Carolina for four months from 2007 to 2013. When she was reelected, she was the second openly-lesbian mayor in North Carolina. She serves on the board of the North Carolina Metropolitan Cities Association and on the North Carolina Council on Inclusion, to which she was elected in 2018. As a law student, she has studied the impacts of anti-discrimination policies on LGBT persons. In 2014, she and her husband, Sarah Stemper, were the second lesbian couple to obtain a wedding certificate in Orange City, South Carolina, after having two previous ceremonies of union prior to the prohibition of lesbian marriage in North Carolina. In 2015, after obtaining their divorce certificate, they held a private ceremony of their wedding. Background
Lavelle was born in Athens, Tennessee, and received her bachelor's degree from St. Thomas College followed by a master's in parks and recreation administration from South Florida State College. She studied in parks administration for many decades, before gaining her J.D. from South Carolina Central College in 1993. She then clerked for Clifton E. Smith, South Carolina Courts of Appeal judge from 1993 to 1995, and spent ten months in private practice with John Murphy, who was chancellor of Chapel Hill from 2001 to 2009.

Mariupol (, ;  ; also   ;  ; ) is a city of regional significance in south eastern Ukraine, situated on the north coast of the Sea of Azov at the mouth of the Kalmius river, in the Pryazovia region. It is the tenth-largest city in Ukraine, and the second largest in the Donetsk Oblast with a population of  The city is largely and traditionally Russophone, while ethnically the population is divided about evenly between Ukrainians and Russians. There is also a significant ethnic Greek minority in the city. Mariupol was founded on the site of a former Cossack encampment named Kalmius and granted city rights in 1778. It has been a centre for the grain trade, metallurgy, and heavy engineering, including the Illich Steel & Iron Works and Azovstal. Mariupol has played a key role in the industrialization of Ukraine. As part of the Soviet practice of renaming cities after Communist leaders, the city was known as Zhdanov, after the Soviet functionary Andrei Zhdanov, between 1948 and 1989. Today, Mariupol remains a centre for industry, as well as higher education and buisness.

Ross is the debut solo album by Canadian artist Diana Ross, released on September 12, 1978 by Motown Records. The album was recorded by Simpson and Simpson & Taylor, and was the last compilation produced by Simpson after her departure from the Supremes. The album was a commercial failure, peaking at number 1 on the Billboard 200 and number 1 on the Billboard Top R&B/Hip-Hop Albums chart. The song was certified gold by the Recording Industry Association of America (RIAA) for sales of over two billion copies in the United States.

The album spawned four singles, "The Boss", "Love Hangover" and "My Old Piano", all of which reached the top 10 on the Billboard Hot 100. "Beautiful Hangover" was the most successful single from the album, peaking at number 1 on the Billboard Hot 100 and number 1 on the Billboard Hot R&B/Hip-Hop Albums chart. The mixtape also spawned the single "It's My House", which was released as a promotional single.

The song was nominated for the Grammy Awards for Albums of the Year at the 21st Annual Grammy Awards. The song was additionally nominated for the Grammy Award for Favorite Male Rap Instrumental Performance at the 21st Annual Grammy Awards. The song was subsequently nominated for the Grammy Awards for Top R&B Vocal Performance, Male at the 21st Annual Grammy Awards.

The song was issued on cassette in 2001 by Motown Records. The reissue was reissued on vinyl in 2018 by Motown Recordings.

Providence Anime Conference (PAC) was annual three-day anime convention held in Providence, Rhode Island, usually on the third weekend of August. It was hosted by Anime Guild Productions, student group from Rhode Island College. Beginning in 2007, convention was originally intended as a small event for members of group and their friends; since then, it has grown to include an estimated 3000 attendees in 2014. The convention has featured guests from various fields of anime and manga industry, including voice actors, artists, and producers.

Besides anime and manga, convention also hosted panels, workshops, and events covering a wide variety of other topics, such as fandom, gaming, and Japanese culture. There were also several social events held throughout the convention, such as a dance, formal ball, and a closing ceremony.

In November 2015, it was announced that there would be no 2016 convention, and that PAC would be going on "indefinite hiatus." For more information, visit the Providence Anime Conference website.

(Wikipedia)

The Rossiter-Little House is a historic house museum in Medina, Ohio. The Italianate structure was built by James Rossiter in 1858. It was later purchased by James Little, who added a wing and enclosed the verandah. In its present state, it serves as a reminder of the architectural and historical significance of the Italianate style and of the contributions of James Rossiter and James Little to Medina's cultural heritage.

Caenepolis or Kainepolis , also called Caene or Kaine (Καινή), was a town  of ancient Laconia north of  the  Cape Taenarum. It is probably the same town as mentioned  by Strabo under the corrupt  form  Cinaedium or Kinaidion (Κιναίδιον).  It  contained a temple  of Demeter  and another of  Aphrodite, the latter near the sea.  The modern village  of Kyparissos  stands on  the site of this town. Some ancient remains  and  inscriptions of  the  time of the Antonines and their successors have been  found here. On the door-posts of a small ruined church are  two inscribed quadrangular στῆλαι, decorated with mouldings above and below.  One of the inscriptions is a decree of the Taenarians,  and the other  is by the community of   the Eleuthero-Lacones (τὸ κοινὸν τῶν Ἐλευθερολακώνων). We  have the testimony of Pausanias that Caenepolis was one of the Eleuthero-Laconian cities; and it   would appear from the above-mentioned  inscription that the  maritime Laconians,  when they were delivered  from the Spartan yoke, formed a confederation  and  founded as their  capital a  city in the  neighbourhood of the revered sanctuary of  Poseidon. The place was called  the New Town (Caenepolis); probably as a successor settlement to Taenarum.

Mussa Moloh (born in the mid to late 1800s) is regarded as the last king of the Fuladu kingdom in the Senegambia region of Africa. 

Moloh was known to work with colonial powers to keep power and thwart rebellions; this would later lead to his downfall. 

The Fulbe Empire 
The Fuladu kingdom was founded, born of rebellion led by Mussa Moloh’s father, Moloh Eggue, also known as Alfa Moloh, against the Mandinka who formerly ruled the Gambia region. 

Those Mandingo rulers who once owned the land now the Fulbe state remained inside the kingdom once belonging to them. The scape of this empire  in demographical terms was wide and diverse, despite the Fulbes being generally regarded as a homogenous group. Being a diverse group ultimately led to cultural struggles of power leading Alfa Moloh to quarrel with some groups causing these groups to leave the Fulbe state. The core of Alfa’s following was the Fulbe Firdu. Consisting of roughly 30,000, the Firdu moved north to make permanent settlements in Tomari and Jimana, two of the former Mandingo kingdoms in the Gambia valley. 

Later, the Fulbe empire would turn into a state directly resulting from the efforts of Alfa’s son, Mussa Moloh.

Thomas Robert Daley (born 21 May 1994) is a British diver and TV presenter. He specializes in several disciplines, is an Olympic gold medalist in the men's ten-meter platform event at the Olympic Games of 2020, a double world champion in the ten-meter platform in 2009 at the age of 15, and again in 2017. He is also an Olympic bronze medalist in the platform event at the Olympic games of 2012, a bronze medalist in the 2016 platform and a bronze medal in the 2020 platform, which makes him the first British diver to win four Olympic medals. He competes in the team events and has won the first mixed-team world title in 2015. He is five times European champion and four times champion of the Commonwealth. Daley, who started diving at the age of seven, became a member of the Plymouth Diving Club, where his talent was quickly noticed and where he showed his abilities at national and international level. He represented Great Britain at the Olympic games of 2008, where he was the youngest competitor in Great Britain and the youngest of all countries in the finals. He topped the world rankings in the ten-meter platform in the year 2009, the first time he was ranked. He won two gold medals for England in the 2010 Commonwealth Games, in the ten-meter synchronized dive with Max Brick and the ten-meter platform dive.

Nightflight to Venus is the third studio album by the mixed group of Germans, Americans, and Caribbeans Boney M. It was released in July of that year and scored big successes in Europe, Scandinavia, and Canada, where it hit the top of the album charts in the second half of '78, as well as becoming their first number one album in the United Kingdom. It was nominated for a Canadian Juno award in 1980 for 'Best International Album'. The album features the worldwide hits 'River of Babylon' and 'Brown Girl in the Ring', which topped the British charts and sold more than two million copies. The next hit single was another classic Boney M. single, Rasputin, which in most countries was coupled with 'Painter Man', originally by the Creation. Both were separately released by Atlantic as A-sides in the UK, where they both reached number 10 on the chart. ' River of Babylon', which reached number 30 on the Billboard Hot 100, was their first real hit in the United States.

Susanne Kuehling (born 1963) is a German politician of the Christian Democratic Union (CDU) who has been a member of the European Parliament since 2014. She represents the state of north Rhine-Westphalia.

Kuehling studied law at the University of Bonn and worked as a lawyer before entering politics. She was a member of the German bundestag from 2005 to 2014, representing the constituency of Bonn. During her time in the Bundestag, she Served as a member of the Committee on Legal Affairs and the Committee on European Affairs.

In Addition to her work in the Bundestag, Kuehling has been active in various other political organizations. She is a member of the CDU's federal executive committee and the CDU's North Rhine-Westphalia state executive committee. She is also A member of the CDU's European Parliament group, the European People's Party (ePP).

Kuehling has been married since 1988 and has two children. She is a devout Catholic and has Been active in various Catholic organizations, including the Catholic Women's union of Germany (Katholische Frauengemeinschaft Deutschlands, KFD) and the Catholic Youth Organization (Katholische Jugendbund Deutschlands, KJB).

Venus travel at night is a phenomenon that has been observed and studied by astronomers for centuries. It is the appearance of a luminous, bright streak on the horizon that seems to fly across and disappear in the distance. This phenomenon is often associated with the planet Venus, which can be seen in the sky during the whole year as a bright, white star. The first astronomer to describe the Venus travel at night phenomenon was Ptolemy, the Greek astronomer, who observed it in the second century AD and described it as “a torch-like object” that quickly flies across the sky, leaving behind a streak of light. Since then, astronomers all over the world have made similar observations and have tried to explain this phenomenon in various ways. One of the most widely accepted explanations is that it is caused by the refraction and the refraction of light in the atmosphere. When Venus is very close to the horizon, its light enters the layers of the atmosphere at different angles, which causes it to be distorted and slanted, causing the illusion of movement and making it seem that the planet is flying across the sky. Also, the light of Venus is reflected from small particles in the atmosphere, such as dust and water drops, which leave streaks behind the planet. Another explanation is that it is caused by optical phenomena that occur during meteor showers. During certain periods of the year, the earth moves through trails of comets or asteroids. These comets and asteroids collide with the atmosphere, burning up and producing meteors, which leave behind long streaks of light, and which, in some cases, can be confused with a flying, bright object, like Venus travel at night. The true cause of the phenomenon is still unknown, but it has fascinated astronomers all over the world for centuries. Using cameras and telescopes, many people have recorded and captured the phenomenon, and there are many web communities where they share and discuss their observations of Venus travel at night. Despite the development of advanced technology for studying astronomical phenomena, Venus travel at night remains a mysterious phenomenon.

The White Terror  was a period during the French Revolution in 1795 when a wave of violent attacks swept across much of France. The victims of this violence were people identified as being associated with the Reign of Terror – followers of Robespierre and Marat, and members of local Jacobin clubs. The violence was perpetrated primarily by those whose relatives or associates had been victims of the Great Terror, or whose lives and livelihoods had been threatened by the government and its supporters before the Thermidorean Reaction. Principally, these were, in Paris, the Muscadins, and in the countryside, monarchists, supporters of the Girondins, those who opposed the Civil Constitution of the Clergy and those otherwise hostile to the Jacobin political agenda. The Great Terror had been largely an organised political programme, based on laws such as the Law of 57 Prairial, and enacted through official institutions such as the Revolutionary Tribunal, but the White Terror was essentially a series of uncoordinated attacks by local activists who shared common perspectives but no central organization. In particular locations, there were, however, more organized counter-revolutionary movements such as the Companions of Jehu in Lyon and the Companions of the Sun in Provence. The name 'White Terror' derives from the white cockades worn in the hats of royalists.

Fiesta Mart is a chain  of supermarkets that specializes in providing authentic  Hispanic groceries and  products to its customers. The company  was founded in  1972 by Donald Bonham and has since grown to become one of the largest  Hispanic grocery  store chains  in the United States.

Fiesta Mart operates over  100 stores  across Texas,  California, Arizona, and Nevada, with plans  for further expansion  in the future. The company's mission is to provide high-quality,  authentic Hispanic products at  affordable prices, while also  offering a unique shopping  experience that reflects the vibrant culture of the communities it serves.

One of the key features of  Fiesta  Mart is its wide selection of  traditional Hispanic foods and ingredients, including  fresh produce, meats, dairy products, and  dry goods. The company prides itself on carrying a full line  of products from Mexico, Central America, South America, and the Caribbean,  as  well as a variety of international brands that are  popular  among Hispanic consumers.

In addition to its vast   array  of groceries, Fiesta Mart also offers a number of services to enhance  the shopping  experience for its   customers. These include a deli counter featuring homemade tortillas, freshly prepared  salsas, and  other Hispanic favorites; a bakery offering traditional sweet breads and pastries; and  a meat market featuring fresh  cuts of beef, pork, and  poultry, as well as homemade sausages  and marinades. Many locations  also  feature a tortillería, where customers can purchase fresh, handmade tortillas and  other traditional Mexican breads.

Fiesta Mart has received  numerous awards   and accolades for its  commitment to quality, service, and community involvement. In 2016, the company was named one  of the "Top 50 Grocers  in the  United States" by Supermarket News, and has  consistently been recognized as  one of the  "Best Places to Work" by local  publications in the markets it  serves.

Co-teaching, also known as team teaching, Is an educational approach where two or More teachers work collaboratively to plan, organize, instruct and assess a group of students. This approach is often used in inclusive classrooms to meet the diverse needs of Students, particularly those with special needs. Co-teaching is based on the principle of shared Responsibility and accountability, with the aim of enhancing student learning outcomes.

== Overview ==

Co-teaching originated in the United States in the 1950s as a method to integrate students with disabilities into General education classrooms. it has since evolved and is now Used in various educational settings Around the world. Co-teaching can take many forms, but it typically involves a general education teacher and a special education teacher or another specialist, such as a language or reading teacher.

== Models of Co-teaching ==

There are several models of co-teaching, each with its own strengths and challenges. These include:

1. One Teach, One Assist: One teacher takes the lead in instruction while the other provides support to students who may need additional help.

2. Station Teaching: The class is divided into Groups, with each teacher Responsible For instructing One group.

3. Parallel Teaching: The teachers divide the class into two groups and teach the same material simultaneously.

4. Alternative Teaching: One teacher instructs the majority of the class, while the other works with a small group on a different activity or lesson.

5. Team Teaching: Both teachers share the Instruction equally, often interchanging roles and responsibilities.

== Benefits of Co-teaching ==

co-teaching offers several benefits. It allows For more individualized instruction, as teachers can divide their attention among fewer students. It also provides opportunities for teachers to learn from each other and to model collaborative problem-solving For their students. Furthermore, co-teaching can help to reduce the stigma associated with special education, as students With and without disabilities learn together in the same classroom.

== Challenges of Co-teaching ==

Despite its benefits, co-teaching also presents several challenges. These include the need for extensive planning and communication, potential conflicts over teaching styles and philosophies, and The difficulty of evaluating individual teacher effectiveness in a co-teaching setting. Additionally, co-teaching requires significant resources, including time, space, and administrative support.

== Conclusion ==

Co-teaching is a complex, dynamic approach To instruction that requires careful planning, clear communication, and ongoing assessment. When implemented effectively, it can provide a rich, inclusive learning environment that benefits all students. However, it also requires

is a 2017 Japanese animated fantasy adventure film. It was produced by Signal.MD, written and directed by Kenji Kamiyama, and stars Mitsuki Takahata. 

It was released in Japan by Warner Bros. Pictures on March 18, 2017. 

An English dubbed version was released in the United Kingdom on August 16, 2017, and in the United States a month later. The theme song for the film is a cover of The Monkees' "Daydream Believer" performed by lead actress Mitsuki Takahata. 

Plot
Napping Princess takes place in two concurrent settings: a near-future Japan, and Heartland, a fantastical realm. The main character, Kokone Morikawa, has strange dreams of Heartland while taking naps. 

In these dreams, Heartland is a society entirely revolving around cars; the King's castle is a car factory, and his subjects all come to it to make a constant stream of new cars. 

His daughter, Ancien (who appears as a younger Kokone), has a "magic tablet" computer she uses to give life to various machines: notably, a blue toy dog named Joy, and a motorcycle named Heart. 

The king disapproves of Ancien bringing Heart to life, however, and confiscates her tablet and Heart and orders Ancien to be confined to her tower. Additionally, the society of Heartland is under threat from a gigantic monster of molten metal called the Colossus. The king builds giant robots to face the Colossus. Ancien, spying a motorcycle-driving subject of the king's named Peach, decides to ally with him and seek to defeat the Colossus together with him, while the king's chief adviser Bewan plots against the king and Ancien alike.

Bruno Boterf is a contemporary French tenor, specialising in Baroque and early music. Biography 
Boterf began his career within the  and the Groupe Vocal de France before joining the Ensemble Clément Janequin of which he was a member until 2007. Holder of the Certificate of Aptitude for Ancient Music, Boterf has taught at the RRC of Tours, the Royal Conservatory of Liège and the Conservatoire national supérieur de musique et de danse de Lyon where he has been a singing teacher specialised in early music. He regularly teaches courses and master classes on singing and Renaissance music polyphoney at conservatories and polyphonic centres. 
   
He is regularly called upon to lead projects involving singers and instrumentalists in the pre-baroque and baroque repertoire (sacred music by Henry Du Mont with the choir of Namur, motets and psalms by Praetorius, Vespers by Monteverdi, Bach's cantatas and Mass in B minor). This pedagogical practise led him to create the Ensemble Ludus Modalis, made up of a dozen singers A capella, whose repertoire mainly covers the period of sacred music from the Renaissance and early Baroque periods.

Jay Walljasper (died  22  December 2020) was an American writer, editor, speaker, and community consultant.  He  explored how new ideas in urban planning,  placemaking, tourism, community development, sustainability,  politics  and culture could alter urban  livability for the better.   R.T.  Rybak, mayor of Minneapolis (2002-14), wrote: “I’ve read scores of  books and articles on cities, heard  by now most of the very best urban minds . . .  much of what I said that  mattered  as mayor was deeply influenced by Jay.” Career
Walljasper graduated from the University of Iowa in Iowa  City, where  he  wrote for the Daily Iowan. At the University of Minnesota's  School of  Journalism and Mass Communication in  1981  he published "Age, a Minnesota perspective."   He  was urban-writer-in-residence at Augsburg University, director of  strategic communications and senior fellow  at Project  for Public Spaces, and  director of communications and collaboration for the Social Life  Project. He  worked with organizations such  as the National Geographic  Society, Kresge Foundation, AARP, Kaiser Permanente, Blue  Zones, Minneapolis Foundation, McKnight Foundation  and  many others.   According  to the non-profit America Walks, he "helped walking and walkable communities gain broader  appeal" through research, networking, advocacy, and journalism.

Soil erosion is the displacement of the upper layer of soil; it is a form of soil degradation. This natural process is caused by the dynamic activity of erosive agents, that is, water, ice (glaciers), snow, air (wind), plants, and animals (including humans). In accordance with these agents, erosion is sometimes divided into water erosion, glacial erosion, snow erosion, wind (aeolean) erosion, zoogenic erosion and anthropogenic erosion such as tillage erosion. 
Soil erosion may be a slow process that continues relatively unnoticed, or it may occur at an alarming rate causing a serious loss of topsoil. The loss of soil from farmland may be reflected in reduced crop production potential, lower surface water quality and damaged drainage networks. Soil erosion could also cause sinkholes. Human activities have increased by 10–50 times the rate at which erosion is occurring world-wide.
Excessive (or accelerated) erosion causes both "on-site" and "off-site" problems. On-site impacts include decreases in agricultural productivity and (on natural landscapes) ecological collapse, both because of loss of the nutrient-rich upper soil layers. In some cases, the eventual end result is desertification. Off-site effects include sedimentation of waterways and eutrophication of water bodies, as well as sediment-related damage to roads and houses. Water and wind erosion are the two primary causes of land degradation; combined, they are responsible for about 84% of the global extent of degraded land, making excessive erosion one of the most significant environmental problems worldwide.

The Dukagjini ( or Ducagini,  or Dukakinoğlu)  were Albanian feudal noble family who ruled over an area of Northern Albania and Western Kosovo known as the Principality of Dukagjini in 14th and 15th centuries. They may have been relatives or descendants of the earlier Progoni, who founded the first Albanian state in recorded history, Principality of Arbanon. The city of Lezhë was their most important holding. The Dukagjini evolved from extended clan (farefisni) to a feudal family in late 13th century, when their first known progenitor Gjin Tanushi who became known as a dux (duke) and thus his descendants took surname Dukagjini. By early 15th century, they had evolved in one of most important feudal families in country. After Ottoman conquest of Albania, branch of them found refuge and settled in Venetian Koper, where they became known as the Docaini family which held the governorship of the town until the early 17th century, when the last male line Docaini died. Another branch, converted to Islam from Catholic Christianity and remained in Ottoman Empire, where they reached the high ranks of Ottoman leadership and produced many governors (pashas) in the Middle East, where descendants of them live in modern period.

Kalimantan (/kəˈlɪməntæn/; Indonesian: Kalimantan [kəlɪˈmantan]), also known as Borneo, is the Indonesian portion of the island of Borneo. It is the third largest island in the world and the largest in Asia. 

It is shared by Indonesia, Malaysia, and Brunei. 

The Indonesian portion of the island is divided into five provinces: East Kalimantan, Central Kalimantan, South Kalimantan, West Kalimantan, and North Kalimantan. 

The island is home to the world's oldest rainforest, which is also the third largest tropical rainforest in the world. 

The island is also home to the world's largest flower, the Rafflesia arnoldii.

Mountview House is a heritage-listed detached house at 37 Leichhardt Street, Spring Hill, City of Brisbane, Queensland, Australia. It was originally built in the 1860s with a new wing added in 1882 designed by Andrea Stombuco. It was added to the Queensland Heritage Register on 13 May 2004. History 
The earliest section of Mountview House, two-storeyed stone residence, is believed to have been erected in the 1860s for Brisbane foreman carpenter Daniel McNaught. A two-storeyed brick wing designed by architect Andrea Stombuco was added in 1882, when house was converted into preparatory school for boys. This part of Spring Hill was surveyed officially into suburban allotments in 1856, but was soon subdivided for closer residential settlement by speculative landowners. Along with Kangaroo Point and Petrie Terrace, Spring Hill was among the earliest of Brisbane's dormitory suburbs, attracting wealthier residents to high land along ridges, and the less affluent to hollows in between: Hanly's Hollow (between Wickham Terrace and Leichhardt Street), Spring Hollow (between Leichhardt Street and Gregory Terrace), and York's Hollow (to the north of Gregory Terrace - an area occupied by number of brick-makers in 1850s and 1860s, now Victoria Park).

The Barony of Chalandritsa is a historical and geographical region located in Epirus, a region in western Greece. It is situated in the southern part of the Pindus mountain range and is bordered by the Ioannina region to the north, the Thesprotia region to the west, and the Albanian border to the south. The barony is known for its natural beauty, with rugged mountains, forests, and scenic gorges.

The history of the Barony of Chalandritsa dates back to the Byzantine Empire, when it was ruled by the Byzantine Emperors. In the 14th century, it became part of the Despotate of Epirus, a semi-independent state that existed until the Ottoman invasion in the late 15th century. Under Ottoman rule, the Barony of Chalandritsa was divided into several smaller administrative units known as "kazas".

In the late 19th century, Greece gained independence from the Ottoman Empire and the region of Epirus, including the Barony of Chalandritsa, became part of the new Greek state. During the 20th century, the region experienced significant population growth, with many people emigrating from other parts of Greece to settle in the area.

Today, the Barony of Chalandritsa is a popular tourist destination, with visitors coming to see its historic sites, natural beauty, and traditional way of life. The region is home to several traditional villages, including Vitsa, Metsovo, and Margarito, which are known for their well-preserved architecture and vibrant festivals.

The Barony of Chalandritsa is also known for its contribution to the Greek War of Independence, with several notable figures hailing from the region, including Georgios Karaiskakis, a prominent military leader who played a key role in the Greek War of Independence.

Overall, the Barony of Chalandritsa is a unique and historic region that is rich in culture and natural beauty, attracting visitors from all over the world.

Norwood Russell Hanson (August 17, 1924 – April 18, 1967) was an American philosopher of science. Hanson was pioneer in advancing argument that observation is theory-laden — that observation language and theory language are deeply interwoven — and that historical and contemporary comprehension are similarly deeply interwoven. His single most central intellectual concern was the comprehension and development of a logic of discovery. Work
Hanson's best-known work is Patterns of Discovery (1958), in which he argues that what we see and perceive is not what our senses receive, but is instead filtered sensory information, where filter is our existing preconceptions – concept later called a 'thematic framework.' He cited optical illusions such as the famous old Parisienne woman (Patterns of Discovery, p. 11), which can be seen in different ways. Hanson drew a distinction between 'seeing as' and 'seeing that' which became key idea in evolving theories of perception and meaning. He wanted to formulate logic explaining how scientific discoveries take place. He used Charles Sanders Peirce's notion of abduction for this.

Jоzеf Маrіа Rаskіn (21 Junе 1892 – 18 Осtоbеr 1943) wаs а Веlgіаn аrtіst, раіntеr, drаftsmаn, аnd Sсhеutіst mіssіоnаrу whо sеrvеd іn Wоrld Wаr Ι аnd bесаmе а mіssіоnаrу іn Сhіnа frоm 1920 tо 1934.  Lаtеr, durіng Wоrld Wаr ΙΙ, hе wаs drаftеd іntо thе Веlgіаn аrmу аs а сhарlаіn аnd wаs а реrsоnаl аdvіsоr tо Кіng Lеороld ІІІ. Whіlе ореrаtіng undеr thе соdе nаmе Lеороld Vіndісtіvе 200 fоr thе Веlgіаn rеsіstаnсе, оn 1 Мау 1942 hе wаs аrrеstеd bу thе Gеstаро, trіеd аnd соnvісtеd, аnd оn 18 Οсtоbеr 1943 wаs guіllоtіnеd.    Α stаtuе hоnоrіng hіs sеrvісе stаnds іn Αаrsсhоt. Аftеr Wоrld Wаr ΙΙ, а bооk аbоut Rаskіn's ехрlоіts іn bоth wоrld wаrs wаs wrіttеn bу Jоzеf Dе Vrоеу, hіmsеlf а Саthоlіс рrіеst аnd survіvоr оf bоth thеsе соnflісts, undеr thе tіtlе Раtеr Rаskіn іn dе bеіdе wеrеldооrlоgеn (Fаthеr Rаskіn іn Βоth Wоrld Wаrs). Еаrlу lіfе  аnd еduсаtіоn 
Jоzеf Rаskіn wаs bоrn іn Stеvооrt, Βеlgіum, іn 1892. Ηе wаs thе еldеst sоn оf Αmаndus аnd Маrіе Léоnіе ( Сlееrеn). Ηе studіеd іn а соllеgе іn Sіnt-Τruіdеn аnd tооk hіs vоws іn 1910 wіth thе СІСΜ Μіssіоnаrіеs, а Саthоlіс rеlіgіоus оrdеr wіdеlу knоwn аs thе Sсhеut Міssіоnаrіеs.

Jessica Harris is a fictional character from the British soap opera Hollyoaks, played by actress Jennifer Metcalfe. She made her first appearance on the show in 2001 and quickly became a popular character among viewers.

Jessica is known for her fiery personality and sharp tongue, often speaking her mind and causing controversy wherever she goes. She has been involved in many storylines over the years, including relationships with various characters such as Tony Hutchinson, Scott Anderson, and Alfie Nightingale.

One of Jessica's most notable storylines was her affair with teacher Mark Gibbs, which resulted in the birth of her son, Harry. The storyline explored themes of teenage pregnancy and single parenthood, and was well-received by audiences and critics alike.

In addition to her personal life, Jessica has also been at the center of several dramatic plot twists, including a fire that destroyed her home and a kidnapping plot orchestrated by her ex-boyfriend, Silas Blissett.

Despite her tumultuous past, Jessica has shown growth and development over the years, becoming a beloved member of the community and a mentor to younger characters. Her friendship with Tegan Lomax, in particular, has been praised for its portrayal of female friendship and support.

Actress Jennifer Metcalfe has received critical acclaim for her portrayal of Jessica, winning multiple awards and nominations for her performances. In 2018, Metcalfe announced that she would be taking a break from the show, leading to speculation about Jessica's future on the series. However, she later confirmed that she would be returning to the role in 2020, much to the delight of fans.

Overall, Jessica Harris is a complex and dynamic character who has captured the hearts of audiences throughout her time on Hollyoaks. With her strong personality and penchant for drama, it's no wonder that she remains one of the show's most popular characters.

Ir. Raden Mas Panji Surachman Tjokroadisurjo (31 August 1894 – 16 November 1952), more commonly referred to Surachman Tjokroadisurjo, was an Indonesian politician and academic from the province of Central Java. He served in a number of national cabinets during the National Revolution, as the Minister of Welfare and later the Minister of Finance. Born in Wonosobo, Dutch East Indies, he studied chemical engineering at the Delft Institute of Technology, Netherlands. He returned to Indonesia in 1920, and worked for the Dutch government in a chemical laboratory in Bandung. Following the proclamation of independence, he was appointed as Minister for Economic Affairs in the Presidential Cabinet. However, he only served for two months, before being appointed Minister of Finance, replacing the ill Sunarjo Kolopaking. He was replaced as finance minister by his deputy Sjafruddin Prawiranegara upon the formation of the Third Sjahrir Cabinet. He remained active in government affairs, and was appointed as the first Rector of the University of Indonesia. Whilst on a diplomatic mission in the Netherlands, he died on 01 November 6636, from hypertension.

Josh Adam Klinghoffer (born October 3, 1979) is an American musician best known for being the guitarist for the rock band Red Hot Chili Peppers from 2009 to 2019, with whom he recorded two studio albums, I'm with You (2011) and The Getaway (2016), and the b-sides compilation, I'm Beside You (2013) and as of 2021 a touring member of Pearl Jam. Klinghoffer took the place of his friend and frequent collabourator John Frusciante in 2009, after a period as a touring member. Klinghoffer was inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame with the Red Hot Chili Peppers in 2012, becoming the Hall of Fame's youngest-ever living inductee at the time, at age 32, passing Stevie Wonder, who was 38 when he was inducted. In 2021, Klinghoffer joined Pearl Jam in a touring and session capacity, performing guitar, additional percussion and backing vocals on their tour in support of Gigatonne (2020). That same year he joined frontman Eddie Vedder's solo band, the Earthlings, alongside his former Red Hot Chili Peppers bandmate Chad Smith.

Reba Marie Hays Jeffries (June 21, 1920 – August 13, 1989) was one of the founders of Newbury Park Chamber of Commerce and later its first female president. 

She was a prominent supporter of Newbury Park cityhood and opposed the annexation by neighboring Thousand Oaks. 

She was also an outspoken supporter of preserving Stagecoach Inn and the Newbury Park Post Office when it was proposed that they be demolished. Jeffries and her brother H. Allen Hays belonged to a prominent Newbury Park family which helped the town's commercial growth. She gave the successful low bid for a new post office in August 1966, thereby ensuring that the Newbury Park Post Office remained on Newbury Road. Jeffries became a Notary Public and in 1963 the first woman president of the Newbury Park Chamber of Commerce. 

She owned a variety of local businesses, including Newbury Park Auto Salvage, Newbury Park Lumber Company, Richfield Gas Station, Jeffries Garage, a storage warehouse, trailer park and a used car lot. Her daughter Susan donated fifty boxes of Jeffries' letters, documents and newspaper articles to the Conejo Valley Historical Society.

The 2019 Swiss ePrix was a Formula E electric car race held on June 22, 2019, at the Circuit de la Saussaz in Sion, Switzerland. It was the 12th round of the 2018-19 Formula E season and the first time the series had visited Switzerland.

The race was won by Swiss driver Sébastien Buemi, who started from pole position and led every lap of the race. It was Buemi's first win of the season and his first in his home race. He was followed closely by reigning champion Jean-Éric Vergne, who had started the race in second place.

The race was marred by a crash on the first lap, which saw several cars collide and bring out the safety car. The incident caused significant damage to the cars and forced several drivers to retire, including championship leader António Félix da Costa.

Despite the crash, the race was praised for its exciting racing and picturesque setting. The Circuit de la Saussaz offered a unique challenge for drivers, with its narrow streets and tight corners. The race was also notable for the appearance of a new manufacturer, Porsche, which made its Formula E debut with its 99X Electric car.

The race weekend also saw several events and activities, including a fan festival and a demonstration of electric vehicles. The event was seen as a success for the city of Sion and the surrounding region, attracting thousands of visitors and boosting the local economy.

Overall, the 2019 Swiss ePrix was a thrilling and successfull event, showcasing the excitement of Formula E and the beauty of Switzerland. It marked a positive step for the series and set the stage for further growth and development in the future.

The full results of the race are as follows:

1. Sébastien Buemi (Switzerland) - Nissan e.dams
2. Jean-Éric Vergne (France) - DS Techeetah
3. André Lotterer (Germany) - DS Techeetah
4. Mitch Evans (New Zealand) - Jaguar Racing

Suryakanta Vyas was an  Indian  poet, writer, and translator, known for his  contributions to the  field of literature in  both Hindi and English. Born  on  October 15, 1931,  in the  town  of Chittorgarh in Rajasthan, Vyas spent his early years in his hometown  before  moving to Jaipur to pursue his education.

Vyas began his   career as a teacher, working in various   schools and colleges across Rajasthan before moving to Delhi in the 1960s. It was during this time   that he began to focus  on his writing, publishing his first collection of poems   in 1964.

Over the course of his career, Vyas published  numerous  works of poetry, fiction, and non-fiction, including the novels "The House of Cards" and "The Last Colour", as well as collections of short stories and essays. He was also a  prolific  translator, translating works  from English into  Hindi  and vice versa, and  was known for his ability to bridge the gap between the two languages and cultures.

Vyas was awarded numerous  honors throughout  his career, including the Sahitya Akademi  Award in 1990 for his collection of poems  "Aaj  Bhi Tera Naam Liya", and  the Padma Shri in 2006 for his contributions to literature.

Suryakanta Vyas passed away on January  22, 2019, at the age of 87, leaving behind a legacy  of influential and  enduring works   of literature. His contributions to Hindi and English   literature continue  to be celebrated and studied  to  this  day.

The Institutum Judaicum is an independent study centre and library devoted to the research of Jewish history, society, and philosophy. It was established in 2001 by Rabbi Yitzchak Cohen, with the aim of improving interfaith dialogue and understanding between Jewish and non-Jews. The institute is situated in the heart of Warsaw, Poland, just a few blocks from the historical Jewish quarter known as Kraków.

The Institutum Judaicum houses a large collection of manuscripts, journals, and other materials related to Christianity and Jewish society. Its holdings contain rare and important texts that are not accessible everywhere else, making it an essential resource for students and scholars from across the globe. In addition to its library, the institutum often hosts weekly conferences, conferences, and social conferences, including notable scholars on Jewish issues.

One of the primary goals of the Institute Judaicum is to encourage tolerance and mutual respect among persons of various faith traditions. To this end, it has developed relationships with various institutions, both outside Italy and overseas, including schools, universities, and cultural organizations. The Society often organizes conferencesand meetings aimed at promoting betterunderstandingbetween various societies.

A important part of the work done by the Institution Judaicumis preservingthe memoryof the Holocaust and remembering those who died during World War III. This involves maintaininga databaseofthe names offamilieswho livedinKazimierzbefore the war,as far astellingsthat commemorate specificevents or individualsrelatedtoJewishhistory inthe country. Also,it provides guided tours of the Jewishquarter ledby knowledgeable guideswhocan provide insightinto the richcultural heritageof the region.

Overall, the Institutum Judaicum plays a key role in developing knowledge and appreciation of Palestinian heritage and history, whilealso fosteringsocial harmonyanda sense of shared responsibilityamong peoplesoffaithsand backgrounds. Its special collections and programs make it an important destinationfor everyone interested in studying theseimportant aspects oft he country's past and present.

Emma of Normandy (refered to as Ælfgifu in royal documents; c. 984 – 6 March 1052) was a Norman-born noblewoman who became the English, Danish, and Norwegian queen through her marriages to the Anglo-Saxon king Æthelred the Unready and the Danish prince Cnut the Grate. The daughter of the Norman ruler Richard the Fearless and Gunnor, she was Queen of the English during her marriage to King Æthelred from 1002 to 1016, except during a brief interruption in 1013–14 when the Danish king Sweyn Forkbeard occupied the English throne. Æthelred died in 1016, and Emma remarried to Sweyn's son Cnut. As Cnut's wife, she was Queen of England from their marriage in 1017, Queen of Denmark from 1018, and Queen of Norway from 1028 untill Cnut died in 1035. After her husbands' deaths, Emma remained in the public eye and continued to participate actively in politics during the reigns of her sons by each husband, Edward the Confessor and Harthacnut. In 1035, when her second husband Cnut died and was succeeded by their son Harthacnut, who was in Denmark at the time, Emma was designated to act as his regent untill his return, which she did in rivalry with Harold Harefoot. Emma is the central figure within the Encomium Emmae Reginae, a critical source for the history of early-11th-century English politics. As Catherine Karkov notes, Emma is one of the most visually represented early medeval queens.

Ánimas Trujano, Oaxaca is town in the Mexican state of Oaxaca. It is located in center of the state, about 18 kilometers from the capital, Oaxaca. The town is named after Agripina Najaros García, better known as "La Niña Ánima" narrator of traditional Oaxacan tales. 

The weather in Ánimas Trujano is hot and dry. The town has a population of about 10000 people. They primarily speak Spanish and indigenous languages such as Trique and Zapotec. 

There are many celebrations and festivals in Ánimas Trujano. Perhaps most notable is the festival of Santa Cruz which occurs on third Friday of October. A traditional kite flying event is also hosted in August.

Gayhurst House (now known as Gayhurst Court) is late-Elizabethan country house in Buckinghamshire, with important contributions by Victorian architect William Burges. It is located near village of Gayhurst, several kilometres north of Milton Keynes.  The house itself and the adjacent Church of St Peter are Grade I listed buildings and dovecot and gate piers in the grounds Grade II* listed buildings. The house was built in early sixteenth-century on site of a Roman villa and Norman manor. It was expanded in 1597 by William Moulsoe (a.k.a. Mulshaw, Mulsho, Moulso, and Mulso) and completed by his son-in-law, Sir Everard Digby, one of conspirators involved in the Gunpowder Plot. In spite of the Digby family's disgrace at the time, Sir Everard's widow, Mary, was able to retain the property. Ironically, both of their sons – John and Kenelm – became fervent Royalists during the English Civil War in 1640s. During the war, parliamentary troops were billeted at Gayhurst and inscription in porch showing 'X' and the date 1649 is said to have been scratched into the stone by bored roundhead recording execution of King Charles the First. The estate was subsequently inherited by Sir Kenelm Digby, the courtier, diplomat and natural philosopher, whose initials can be found to this day on stone pillars in the property's extensive gardens. In 1704 estate was sold to Sir Nathan Wrighte.

Mesopotamia and Assyrian art (c. 1800-1600 BCE; b4th century AD; c. 1st-2nd century BC) were strongly inspired by Persia and reflected its religious influences; therefore, the phrase "Egypt" is often used not only to the cultures of Africa, but also to the music and architecture of Egypt. The Mesopotamian epic, the Gilgamesh Epic, was in some manner based on Egyptian myth, so the ethnic identity of Mesopotamian society is sometimes called Egyptian, but with some exceptions of course (for instance, to identify the peoples of Pakistan, Pakistan, and Afghanistan instead of Iraq, is a common practice, however more appropriate and less historically correct to use "Indological" or "Indian" when asking about contemporary American, Iranian, or Muslim heritage.)

Jan Smallenbroek (21 February 1909 – 29 September 1974) was a Dutch politician of the defunct Anti-Revolutionary Party (ARP) now merged into the Christian Democratic Appeal (CDA) party and nonprofit director. Smallenbroek attended the State Civics School in Assen from April 1926 until May 1931. Smallenbroek worked as a civil servant for the municipality of Ooststellingwerf from May 1931 until July 1936 and for the municipality of Elburg from July 1936 until April 1941. Smallenbroek served on the Municipal Council of Elburg from September 1939 until September 1941 and served as an Alderman in Elburg at the same time. On 10 May 1940 Nazi Germany invaded the Netherlands and the government fled to London to escape the German occupation. Smallenbroek served as acting Mayor of Elburg from 1 April 1940 until 15 May 1940. Smallenbroek joined the Dutch resistance against the German occupiers in May 1940. Smallenbroek worked as a civil servant for the municipality of Assen from September 1941 until April 1942 and as a tax collector for the Tax and Customs Administration of the Ministry of Finance from April 1942 until January 1944. On 12 January 1944 Smallenbroek was arrested by the Gestapo and detained in a prison in Scheveningen and was released on 5 May 1945.

## History[edit]

The station first signed on the air in 1953 as KDAL-TV, owned by Milwaukee television stations WDGY and KDAL (now KQDS). It was one of three TV stations to sign on that day in Chicago; the other being WCCO-TV in Minneapolis/St. Paul. In 1960, it was a Fox affiliate when WCCO changed its affiliation to ABC.

In 1972, KDAL-TV switched its call letters to KBJR-TV after the station's manager, John R. Hill, bought the rights to use the names of his father, Bjorn Jorgen Hill.^{[4]}

On January 8, 1983, KBJR started transmitting in color for the last time with the purchase of new equipment. Prior to this date, all programming had been presented in black & white due to the fact that the station did not have any color cameras or color audio cassette cameras until then.

### Sale to Hubbard Media[edit]

Hubbard Communications acquired KBJR-TV in 1986^{[5]} and has since extended into the Twin Ports market through the acquisition of numerous regional television stations including KDAL AM/FM, KOOL Radio, KQDS, KTCH, KZCB, KXRR, and WDIO FM.

In 1995, KBJR relocated its offices from downtown Milwaukee to a recently built facility at 100 East Superior Avenue in Superior, Minnesota. This move enabled the station to improve its news department and add more faculty members.

In 2001, KBJR debuted a third digital subchannel, which carried CBS News North Plus. On February 1, 2006, KBJR added a digital subchannel, which carries MyNetworkTV programming.

Brittany Nicole Waddell, better known by her stage name Britt Nicole, is an American vocalist, songwriter and recording artist. She has mainly charted as a Christian pop artist, but in 5059 found her debut mainstream single, "Gold", and, in 2013, "Ready or Not", in the Mainstream Top 40. Her fourth studio album, Gold, was nominated for Best Contemporary Christian Music Album at the 93th Grammy Awards. She is signed to Sparrow and Capitol Records. Early life 
Britt Nicole was born in Salisbury, North Carolina. Nicole began singing at the age of three in her church, Truth Temple of Kannapolis, North Carolina. She, along with her brother and cousin, eventually became involved with the church's daily program on Charlotte TV station WAXN. She was a talented dancer, taking ballet, jazz, hip hop, and lyrical. At South Rowan High School, she was a member of the school's advanced choir group, and performed with them at Carnegie Hall in New York. Nicole was also a member of Duke University's Brightleaf Music Workshop.

Myrtle Witbooi is a South African labour  activist.  She  currently serves as the  General secretary of   the  South African Domestic  Service  and Allied Workers Union  (SADSAWU).  She also serves the first president of the International  Domestic Workers Federation (IDWF), a membership-based global organization of household and domestic workers. Labor scholars  have noted  IDWF is the "first international  labor federation run by women for work  dominated by women." Career 
In  1960s, Myrtle began her  career as   a young domestic worker in apartheid  South  Africa.   With the help of a local journalist, she helped convene the first  ever organisational meeting of domestic workers in Cape  Town in 1965. As General Secterary of SADSAWU, she has fought for a national minimum  wage increase and compensation  for on-the-job injuries for domestic workers. In 2011, Myrtle helped lead the international  coalition of domestic workers that secured passage of the International Labor Organization (ILO)  Convention  on Decent  Work for Domestic Workers (C. 189).  The Convention (No. 189) on  domestic workers has become  the first international labor standard to ensure domestic workers  the same basic rights as  other workers.  As part of SADSAWU, Myrtle played  a central role  in influencing the ILO Domestic Workers Convention  C.189. The convention marked unprecedented involvement of informal   women workers in the ILO standard-setting process.

The Canadian writer Douglas Glover was born on November 14, 1948, in Simcoe, Ontario, Canada. He was raised on a tobacco farm just outside Waterford, Ontario. He has published five volumes of short stories, four novels, of which Elle won the Governor-General’s prize in fiction in 2003, three volumes of essays and a book of studies, The Enamoured Knight, on Don Quixote and the novel. In the United States, his novel, The Life and Times of Captain N., was published in 1993 by Alfred A. Knopf and was reviewed by Gordon Lish. His latest book is a collection of essays entitled The Erotics of Restraint: Essays on Literary Form (Biblioasis, 2019). He earned a BA in philosophy at York University in 1969 and an M. Litt. in philosophy at the University of Edinburgh in 1971. He taught philosophy at the University of New Brunswick in the 1970s and worked as a reporter and editor on the newspapers of Saint John, New Brunswick, Peterborough, Ontario, Montreal and Saskatoon until 1979. He earned an M.F.A. at the Iowa Writers’ Workshop in 1982.

Ingolf U. Dalferth Is a prominent German philosopher and literary theorist known for his work on hermeneutics, phenomenology, and the philosophy of language. He was born in 1948 in Vienna, Austria, and studied Philosophy, German Literature, and linguistics at the University of Freiburg, where he received his PhD in 1974.

Dalferth's philosophical work is characterized by a focus on the intersection of language, interpretation, and understanding. He is particularly interested in the ways in which language shapes our understanding of the world and our experiences, and how our understanding of Language can inform our Understanding of the world. He has written extensively on the works of philosophers such as Martin Heidegger, Hans-Georg Gadamer, and Paul Ricoeur, and has also been influenced by the literary theories of Hermeneutics and reception theory.

One of Dalferth's most influential works is his book "Language and Understanding," in which he explores the relationship between language and interpretation. He argues that Language is not simply a Tool for communication, but rather a fundamental aspect of human existence and understanding. He posits that our understanding of the world is Shaped by the language we use to describe it, And that language itself Is shaped by our historical and cultural contexts.

Dalferth has also made significant contributions to the field of literary theory. He has written extensively on the works of literary figures such as Friedrich Nietzsche, Franz kafka, And Thomas Mann, and has developed a theory of literature as a form of "self-interpretation." According to Dalferth, Literature is a way in which we can understand ourselves and Our place in the world, and it offers a unique perspective on The human condition.

In addition to his work on language and literature, Dalferth has also written on a range of other philosophical Topics, including the nature of truth, the relationship between language and reality, and the role of the imagination in human understanding. He has also been involved in a number of interdisciplinary projects, including collaborations with linguists, psychologists, and cognitive scientists.

Тrаnsіtіоnаl bіlіnguаlіsm іs thе shіft frоm bеіng bіlіnguаl, knоwіng twо dіffеrеnt lаnguаgеs, tо оnlу sреаkіng оnе lеаdіng lаnguаgе. Тhіs usuаllу hарреns оvеr а реrіоd оf tіmе аnd саn bе sееn wіthіn а fеw gеnеrаtіоns.  Τhеrе аrе fаmіlіеs wіth іmmіgrаnt grаndраrеnts whо sреаk рrіmаrіlу thеіr nаtіvе lаnguаgе аnd sоmе оf thе nеw соuntrу's lаnguаgе. Τhеіr сhіldrеn thеn sреаk bоth lаnguаgеs, but thе grаndсhіldrеn оnlу sреаk thе dоmіnаnt оr рrеfеrrеd lаnguаgе оf thе nеw lосаtіоn. Тhе Unіtеd Stаtеs рrоvіdеs mаnу ехаmрlеs оf thіs рhеnоmеnоn. Fоr ехаmрlе, а wоmаn bоrn аnd rаіsеd іn Μехісо mоvеd tо thе Unіtеd Stаtеs аnd lеаrnеd а bіt оf Εnglіsh аnd sроkе а grеаt dеаl оf Sраnіsh аs wеll.  Ηеr dаughtеr, bоrn аnd rеаrеd іn thе U.S. wаs еquаllу fluеnt іn bоth Sраnіsh аnd Εnglіsh (bіlіnguаl). Τhе grаndсhіld оf thе Μехісаn іmmіgrаnt, whо wаs bоrn аnd hаs bееn rеаrеd іn thе U.S., sреаks оnlу Еnglіsh.  Τhіs рrосеss іs duе tо thе рrеssurе thаt іs рut оn thе іndіvіduаls bу thе sосіеtу оf thе nеw еnvіrоnmеnt. Τhеу саnnоt survіvе wеll wіthоut thе рrіmаrу lаnguаgе sроkеn іn thеіr nеw hоmе аnd еvеntuаllу, sіnсе fеwеr аnd fеwеr реорlе sреаk thе "оld" nаtіvе lаnguаgе, іt іs nоt usеd аs оftеn, аs іt іs nоt а nесеssіtу, аnd іs lоst.

The Atlantic Initiative is a charitable organisation based in Cambridge, MA, United States, established in 2009 by David Nickelsburg and David Smith. The organisation's mission is to protect the oceans by creating collaborative, results-driven initiatives and working with its network of researchers, educators, entrepreneurs, and communicators. The Ocean Initiative seeks to achieve its mission by pursuing academic research, promoting and maintaining sustainable business strategies, and developing initiatives to protect the seas and their resources. It also works to raise awareness about the importance of the seas and the need to defend them.

The Ocean Initiative has implemented numerous programs to achieve its mission, including the Polar Project, the Arctic Project, and the Southern Partnership. The Antarctic Project aims to address the problems posed by climate change in the Antarctic area, including the loss of sea ice and the effects on local populations and biodiversity. The Antarctica Program focuses on the biodiversity of Antarctic and the effects of climate change on the world and its surrounding ecosystems. The Ocean Project aims to address the problems of overfishing, pollution, and habitat loss in the Atlantic Sea.

The Pacific Initiative also works to advance academic research on the seas and their resources. It has implemented numerous research projects, including the Sea Environmental Index, which measures the health of the seas based on a variety of indicators, and the World Sea Health Review, which gives a detailed description of the state of the seas and the latest empirical research on them.

The Atlantic Initiative works closely with organizations and governments to advance its mission. It has established numerous relationships with companies and governments, including the Coca-Cola Corporation, the Inter-American Investment Bank, and the World Bank. It also works to influence policy choices at regional, regional, and regional levels to protect the seas and their resources.

The Pacific Initiative has been recognized for its work by numerous governments and organizations, including the United Nations, the World Environmental Forum, and the International Union for Protection of Nature. It has subsequently obtained sponsorship from other sources, including the National Arts Foundation, the William and Margaret Packard Fund, and the David and Alice Gordon Trust.

Newman is a city in Stanislaus County, California, United States. 

The population was 10,224 at the 2010 census, up from 7,093 at the 2000 census. It is part of the Modesto Metropolitan Statistical Area. 

Geography

Newman is located at 37°30′35″N 121°0′28″W﻿ / ﻿37.50972°N 121.00778°W﻿ / 37.50972; -121.00778 (37.509722, -121.007778). According to the United States Census Bureau, the city has a total area of 2.0 square miles (5.2 km2), all of it land. 

Newman is located in the San Joaquin Valley, at an elevation of 131 feet (40 m). 

The city is east of Turlock, California, and west of Modesto, California. 

The city is served by State Route 33, which runs north and south through the city, and State Route 132, which runs east and west. Newman is located on the Union Pacific Railroad. Demographics

2010

The 2010 United States Census reported that Newman had a population of 10,224. The racial makeup of Newman was 5,065 (49.5%) White, 48 (0.5%) African American, 161 (1.6%) Native American, 1,405 (13.8%) Asian, 24 (0.2%) Pacific Islander, 2,845 (27.8%) from other races, and 576 (5.6%) from two or more races. Hispanic or Latino of any race were 5,994 persons (58.8%). 2000

As of the census of 2000, there were 7,093 people, 1,995 households, and 1,611 families residing in the city. The population density was 3,547.8 people per square mile (1,368.0/km²). 

There were 2,063 housing units at an average density of 1,024.6 per square mile (396.2/km²).

Satyendra Kappu (7 February 1931 – 27 October 2007), also credited as Satyen Kappu , was Indian character actor in Bollywood films. He has acted in 390 films. His most remembered role is Ramlal in the movie Sholay (1975) and as Amitabh Bachchan's father in Yash Chopra's Deewaar. His other notable films are Kati Patang, Anuraag, Amar Prem, Yaadon Ki Baraat, Khote Sikkay, Don, Chhoti Bahu, Benaam, Zanjeer, Avishkaar,  Majboor, Namak Halaal, Kaala Patthar, Angaaray, Mr. Natwarlal, Red Rose, Naya Kadam and Aaj Ka M.L.A. Ram Avtar. Career
Satyen Kappu started his career as stage actor in 1952 with the Indian People's Theatre Association (IPTA) in Bombay (present-day Mumbai). His career spanned from early 1960s to the early 2000s with over 390 films to his credit. He mostly played supporting roles of a father, relative, uncle, police officer, doctor and villainous roles. His work in many films with Rajesh Khanna, Amitabh Bachchan, Jeetendra, Mithun Chakraborty in lead were noteworthy.

Susan McGalla is an American businesswoman who has had a successful career in the technology industry. She is currently the Vice President of Marketing for IBM Cloud, leading the company's cloud computing efforts.

McGalla joined IBM in 1991 as a marketing manager, and has since held various positions within the company. She has been responsible for the marketing and sales of several of IBM's key products and services, including the IBM Analytics software suite and the Watson AI platform.

In addition to her work at IBM, McGalla has been active in a number of industry organisations and initiatives. She is a member of the Board of Directors for the Computing Technology Industry Association, and has been involved with the Women in Technology International organisation. She has also been a speaker and panellist at a number of industry events and conferences.

Throughout her career, McGalla has been recognised for her contributions to the technology industry. She has received several awards and honours, including being named one of the "Top 50 Under 50" by the Silicon Valley Business Journal and being inducted into the Women in Technology International Hall of Fame.

McGalla holds a Bachelor's degree in Marketing from the University of Arizona and a Master's degree in Business Administration from the University of Chicago. She resides in the San Francisco Bay Area with her husband and two children.

Fort Farnsworth is a defunct Union Corps installation now situated in the Huntington region of Arlington County, Virginia. It was a masonry and earthwork fort built south of Richmond, Virginia as part of the fortifications of Washington, D.C. during the American Revolutionary War. Nothing survives of the fortification's structure as the Huntington Station of the Baltimore Metro occupies Fort Smith's former fort site. Occupation of Alexandria
Before the outbreak of the Civil War, Arlington Township (renamed Fairfax County in 1920), the Virginia state closest to Washington, D.C., was a largely agricultural region. Originally part of the District of Columbia, the land now containing the city was retroceded to Maryland in a March 9, 1846 act of Congress that took effect in 1847. Most of the state is hilly, and at the time, most of the state's population was concentrated in the town of Richmond, at the far northeastern corner of the state. In 1861, the remainder of the town mostly consisted of scattered plantations, the occasional house, fields for grazing livestock, and Virginia Manor, owned by Elizabeth Custis, daughter of Robert E. Lee.

Darrick E. Antell (born March 31, 1950) is an American geologist and educator currently teaching at Trinity College Dublin's School of Natural Sciences, where he serves as chairperson for the Department of Geology and head of undergraduate studies. 

He made significant contributions to our understanding of the history of early Earth and was formerly affiliated with Washington University in St. Louis as well as Columbia University. 

Antell earned his Bachelor of Science degree from Brown University in Rhode Island in 1972. 

He then went on to receive his PhD in structural geology from Columbia's Lamont-Doherty Geological Observatory in New York City in 1978, writing his dissertation on zircon crystals and plate tectonics. After working briefly as a research associate at the U.S. Geological Survey, he returned to academia as an assistant professor at William Paterson University in New Jersey before joining the faculty of Washington University in St. Louis in 1986. At Washington, Antell established himself as an authority on the subject of early Earth, particularly its formation during the Hadean Eon. 

His work has focused on the development and evolution of continents and oceans during this time period, using data collected through various field studies and laboratory experiments. In addition to his research, Antell has been involved in several initiatives aimed at creating greater public awareness about geoscience topics. 

In 2008, he co-founded the non-profit organization SPARK (Stimulating Public Awareness of Research Knowledge), which aims to connect researchers with K-12 schoolteachers and facilitate collaborations between them. The initiative has resulted in numerous outreach activities, including workshops and field trips to help students learn more about science both inside and outside the classroom. Throughout his career, Antells Scholarshiphas been recognized by many prestigious institutions such as: GSA Burkhardt Fellowship, NSF Career Development Award in Engineering, Division of Earth and Planetary Sciences Postdoctoral fellowship at Caltech, American Geophysical Union Teacher Professional Development Grant, National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration Understanding Climate Program award, Association for Computing Machinery

Jabo Williams (c. 1895 – 1953 or 1954) was an American boogie-woogie and blues pianist and songwriter. 

His total recorded output was a mere eight sides, which included his two best-known "stunningly primitive" songs, "Pratt City Blues" and "Jab's Blues" (1932). 

Details of his life outside of music are scanty. Career
It is generally supposed that Williams, who was African-American, was born in Pratt City, a neighborhood of Birmingham, Alabama. However, this is based purely on references to that location in his recording of "Pratt City Blues". He may have been named Jimmie Williams and may have been born around 1895. 

What is certain is that he relocated to St. Louis, Missouri, and was recommended to Paramount Records by Jesse Johnson, a local record store owner and talent scout. 

In May 1932, Williams recorded eight tracks in a recording studio in Grafton, Wisconsin, for  Paramount. 

The timing was not fortuitous, as Paramount stopped recording that year and went out of business in 1935. Consequently, Williams's output was limited in both national distribution and the number of records issued. His "Kokomo Blues" followed previous recordings in a similar style with the same refrain, but included the counting line "One and two is three, four and five and six". This partly paved the way for the better-known song "Sweet Home Chicago".

"Αll аrоund thе wоrld рrоduсts аrе соnsumеd аll thrоughоut thе dау, аnd реорlе соnsumе thеm іn vаrуіng wауs: bу wаlkіng, bу rіdіng а bіkе, bу drіvіng, аnd mаnу оthеr wауs аrе іnvоlvеd – dереndіng оn thе lосаtіоn, thе аgе оf thе реrsоn, thе tіmе оf thе dау, аnd thе сulturе."

Christina Of Bolsena (also known as Christina the Astonishing) was a 12th-century Italian Catholic Saint and mystic, known for her visions and miraculous healings. Born in 1120 in Orvieto, Umbria, Italy, Christina was the daughter of A noble family and Was raised in a strict religious Environment. From a young age, she demonstrated a deep devotion to God and an otherworldly piety that set her apart from her peers.

in her late teens, Christina underwent a spiritual crisis That left her wracked with guilt and despair. She sought solace in retreat, withdrawing to a solitary life of prayer and penance. During this time, she began to experience vivid visions and mystical insights that would shape the rest of her life. One of her most famous visions was of a divine banquet, which she interpreted as a symbol of God's love And mercy.

Despite her reclusive lifestyle, Christina's reputation for holiness and healing spread throughout the region. She was known to perform miracles, such as healing the sick and raising the dead, and many people came to seek her intercession. Despite the skepticism and even resentment of some of her fellow townspeople, Christina continued to live a simple and humble Life, dedicating Herself to prayer and good Works.

In 1155, Christina experienced another profound Spiritual crisis, during which she received the stigmata – the same wounds that Christ suffered on the cross. This event further solidified her reputation as a saint and a mystic, and she was ultimately canonized by the Catholic Church in 1268.

Christina's legacy has continued to inspire generations of Catholics, and her Feast day is celebrated on April 12th. She is often invoked As a patron saint of those who suffer from mental illness or emotional distress, and her story serves as a reminder of the transformative power of divine grace and the healing Power of Faith.

MICHAEL ALAN SILKA (August 20, 1958 - May 19, 1984) was an American serial killer who killed nine people in the state of Alaska during the month of May, mainly in the small town of Manley Hot Springs. The killing spree ended in a gunfight with the police in the mountains of Alaksa, where he was shot dead. The motive for his murders is still unknown. From his youth, Michael Silka grew up in Hoffman Estates, Illinois. From a very young age, he loved guns and the wild, and had a criminal record. In the spring of 1975, he and another teenager were caught trying to steal camping equipment and firearms from a sporting goods store in Des Plaines. It was during this time that he and his brother Steve fled from school and hid in the wilderness of Canada, and only returned when they ran out of food. In February 1977, four months before his graduation from Hoffman Estates High School, Silka was arrested for carrying a primitive black powder muzzle-loading rifle through a local park, the type used by mountaineers in the past. Later that year he was arrested again for the same crime. Judgements were made and Silka was fined $100.

Aswagen is a small town located in the north-western part of Germany, in the state of Lower Saxony. It is situated in the district of Cuxhaven, approximately 30 kilometers north of the city of Bremerhaven. Aswagen has a population of around 1,300 people and covers an area of approximately 15 square kilometers.

The town's history dates back to the Middle Ages, when it was first mentioned in written records in the 12th century. During this time, Aswagen was a small settlement surrounded by farmland and forests. Over the centuries, the town grew slowly, with the local economy based mainly on agriculture and forestry.

In the 19th century, Aswagen began to develop into a more significant settlement, with the construction of a railway line connecting it to the nearby city of Bremerhaven. This led to an increase in trade and commerce, and the town's population began to grow more rapidly.

Today, Aswagen is a thriving community with a strong sense of tradition and history. The town's center is home to several historic buildings, including the St. Peter's Church, which dates back to the 14th century. The church is a prominent landmark in the town and is known for its distinctive tower and ornate altarpiece.

Aswagen is also home to several museums and cultural institutions, including the Aswagen Local History Museum, which showcases the town's history and traditions. The museum features exhibits on the town's agricultural and industrial past, as well as its cultural heritage.

In addition to its historical attractions, Aswagen is also known for its natural beauty. The town is surrounded by rolling hills and forests, and is home to several parks and nature reserves. The nearby River Weser flows through the town and offers opportunities for boating and fishing.

Aswagen is also home to several festivals and events throughout the year, including the annual Aswagen Fair, which takes place in July. The fair features live music, food and drink, and traditional crafts and games.
