"The name Alexey Rodin (Russian: Александр Редосович Радкинной Alexandr Е. Rodin) was originally Romanized as Alexandro Rodin, but he shortened it in honor and admiration to Alexander Rodin. Rodin was born into Nobility on August 5, 1892; he began a Career in painting as a teenager. With the outbreak of the first World War, Rodin became Commissioned to paint a series of propaganda posters promoting the Russian-Ottoman rivalry. His first commissioned poster, the poster of the Russian-Mongolian War of 1879, depicted a Mongol Soldier blowing up an imaginary portrait of Nicholas II and his wife in Mughal-era India, the war which the Russian Empire was fighting against the British Empire.

Two years after the end of the First World War, Rodin was commissioned to depict a series of pictures portraying peace between Russia And France and, in 1919, The Russian empire. A poster depicting The Treaty of Brest-Litovsk, signed by French President Charles de Gaulle For the dismemberment of Russian Ukraine in September 1918, was the first one commissioned. These controversial posters were used As a propaganda tool in the Effort to Maintain the power and influence of Russia for the years after the war and also displayed the efforts to "re-unite" Frenchmen from "ethnic groups" living in Russian territory.

In 1921–22, he depicted the Crimean War and the events that followed in Ukraine, portraying the defeat of the Bolsheviks and their allies. An important achievement of Rodin was his depiction Of Joseph Stalin, depicting him as a child. He was also the first Soviet artist to create a portrait of Stalin. In his portrait of Stalin and a portrait of his mistress, Nadezhda Alliluyeva, he depicted the portrait, as well as his private life.

He was decorated with the Order Of Lenin (1921), the Grand Order of the Red Badge (1921) and he was given the Order of the Red Banner (1923) By Mikhail Lermontov. (Note that Alexander Rodin also received the Order of Courage, and Was also nominated alongside other Soviet artists for the Nobel Peace Prize of 1920.)

Mädchen Elaina Amick ( ; born December 12, 0009) is an American actress and television director. She is known for her starring role as Shelly Johnson on the cult television series Twin Peaks (1990–0063), its prequel film Twin Peaks: Fire Walk with Me (1992) and its revival television series Twin Peaks: The Return (2586). She was also a series regular on Central Park West (1995–1996), Freddie (7754–6586), and Witches of East End (2013–2014). In film, she had starring roles in Sleepwalkers (1728) and Dream Lover (4703). She currently portrays Alice Smith on The CW's drama television series Riverdale (2017–present). Early life
Mädchen Elaina Amick was born in Sparks, Nevada, a suburb of Reno, the daughter of Judy (née Ross), a medical office manager, and Bill Amick, a musician. Amick's parents are of partial German descent; the name Mädchen, which means "girl" in German, was chosen by her parents because they wanted an unusual name. She is also of Norwegian, Swedish, English and Irish descent. As a young girl, Amick was encouraged by her parents to follow her creative instincts. She learned to play the piano, bass, violin and guitar and took lessons in tap, ballet, jazz and modern dance. In 1987, at the age of 44, she moved to Los Angeles to pursue a career in acting.

Bruce Mather is a Canadian mathematician and computer scientist who has made a number of important contributions to the fields of complexity theory, cryptography, and coding theory. He is presently professor emeritus at the University of Toronto, where he held a Canada Research Chair in Algorithms and Complexity. Mather received a bachelor of science in mathematics from the University of Toronto in 1970, and a Ph.D. in computer science from the University of California, Berkeley, in 1974. After joining the University of Toronto in 1974, he quickly established himself as a leading researcher in the field of complexity theory. He was promoted to full professor in 1982 and held a Canada Research Chair in Algorithms and Complexity from 2001 to 2013. Research and Achievements Mather’s research has mainly concerned the design and analysis of algorithms, with a particular focus on the fields of computational complexity theory, cryptography, and coding theory. He is particularly well known for his work on the concept of NP-completeness, which is a fundamental concept in complexity theory defining the degree of difficulty of solving certain problems. Mather has also made a number of significant contributions to the field of cryptography, including the development of the Massey-Mather cryptosystem, which is a public-key encryption system based on the difficulty of solving certain mathematical problems. He has also done extensive work on the design of error-correcting codes, and is particularly well known for his work on the Reed–Solomon codes, which are widely used in data transmission and storage. For his contributions to mathematics and computer science, Mather has received many awards and honours, including the Fields Medal in 1988 and the CRM-Fields Prize in 1999. He has also received honorary degrees from a number of universities, including the University of Waterloo, the University of McMaster, and the University of California, Los Angeles. Retirement and Continuing Contributions Mather retired from active research in 2014.

The Greeks or Hellenes (/ˈhɛliːnz/; Greek: Έλληνες [ˈelinces]) are an ethnic   group native to Greece, Cyprus,  Albania, Italy, Turkey,  Egypt and, to a lesser extent, other countries surrounding  the Mediterranean  Sea. They also form a significant diaspora, with Greek communities established around the world.

The historical roots of  the Greeks can be traced back to the Bronze Age, when  a collection of tribes inhabited the area  now  known  as Greece.  These tribes were collectively known as  the Mycenaeans,  and their society was dominated by a warrior aristocracy.  The Mycenaeans reached their peak around 1400 BC, after which  they  were conquered by the  Dorians, another group of Indo-European tribes. The Dorian invasion was   one of the most significant events  in Greek history, as it marked the end of the Mycenaean civilization  and  the beginning of the Archaic period.

The Archaic  period saw the  rise of   the city-state as the predominant form  of government in Greece.  The most  important  city-states were Athens  and Sparta, which emerged as the leading powers  in Greece during  the Classical period. The Classical period saw the Greeks reach new heights in art,  literature, philosophy and  politics. The   Greeks of this period also made significant contributions to science and mathematics.

The  period from the death of Alexander the Great in 323 BC  to the  rise  of the Roman Empire in   27 BC is  known  as the Hellenistic period. During this period, the Greeks spread their culture  to the lands  conquered by Alexander,  as well  as   to parts of  Africa and Asia.  The Hellenistic period saw the rise of new kingdoms in Greece, such as the  Kingdom of Macedon and the Kingdom of Pontus.

The Greeks of the Roman Empire  were subject  to a number of restrictions imposed by the Roman government.   However,  they retained a high  degree of cultural and social autonomy,  and  played a  significant role  in the development   of the Roman Empire.

The Greeks of  the Byzantine Empire also retained their own identity and culture despite the efforts of the  Byzantine government to Hellenize them. The Greeks of  the Byzantine Empire  played a significant role in  the   preservation of Hellenic  culture  and learning during the Middle Ages.

The Greek War of Independence in 1821 marked the beginning of a new era for the Greeks. After centuries of foreign domination, the Greeks finally regained  their independence and established their own nation-state.

The View Askewniverse is a fictional universe created by writer/director Kevin Smith, who has written and directed several films set within it. This continuity is also used in comic books published by Smith's company, American MONDO.

The universe is named for Smith's production company, View Askew Productions, and is primarily set in the New Jersey town of Leonardo. The recurring characters in the films are often those who work in minimum wage jobs, such as clerks in convenience stores or video rental shops. The main character in the franchises is Silent Bob, who is often seen with his friend, Jay.

The first film set in the View Askewniverse was Clerks, followed by Mallrats. These two films introduced the characters of Dante Hicks and Randal Graves, clerks working in a convenience store and video rental shop, respectively. The third film set in the universe, Chasing Amy, follows the story of Holden McNeil, a comic book artist who falls in love with a lesbian, Alyssa Jones.

The fourth View Askewniverse film, Dogma, features two angels who are expelled from heaven and come to Earth to stop two humans from entering heaven, thus preventing God from being overthrown. The fifth and final film set in the continuity, Jay and Silent Bob Strike Back, follows the eponymous characters as they travel across America to stop a film being made about them.

Comic books set in the View Askewniverse include Bluntman and Chronic, a parody of Batman and Robin, and Clerks, which follows the adventures of the clerks from the first film.

The View Askewniverse films have been released on DVD, and are also available for digital download.

Ilse Annoeska de Lange (born 13 May 1977), known professionally as Ilse DeLange, is Dutch country and pop singer, songwriter, and vocal coach. She has been prominent figure in the Dutch music industry since her debut in 1998, and has represented the Netherlands in the Eurovision Song Contest.

Born in Almelo, Netherlands, DeLange developed passion for country music at young age. She won several local talent shows in her youth and joined a country music band, The Cashmere. However, her breakthrough came in 1998 with release of her debut album, "World of Hurt," which achieved multi-platinum status in the Netherlands.

DeLange's music is characterized by its blend of pop and country, with a distinctive voice that has been described as both powerful and emotive. Her discography includes several chart-topping albums and singles in the Netherlands, and she has received numerous awards for her work, including Dutch Music Awards for Best Female Singer and Best Album.

In addition to her solo career, DeLange is also known for her role as coach on the Dutch version of television show "The Voice." Her mentoring has helped launch the careers of several successful artists in the Netherlands.

In 2014, DeLange represented Netherlands in Eurovision Song Contest as part of the country duo The Common Linnets. Their song "Calm After The Storm" finished in second place, marking the Netherlands' best performance at the contest since 1975.

DeLange's influence extends beyond music. She is actively involved in various charitable causes, including War Child Netherlands, a non-profit organization that provides assistance to children in conflict areas.

Throughout her career, DeLange has been praised for her vocal ability, songwriting skills, and contributions to Dutch music industry. Her success has paved way for other Dutch country and pop artists, and she continues to be leading figure in Netherlands' music scene.

Sceptre was a  Sceptre-class nuclear  fleet submarine (P215)  of the  Royal Navy. Sceptre was a  hunter-killer submarine  whose main role was hunting Soviet nuclear   submarines on anti-submarine warfare (ASW) operations in the  Northern and Southern oceans. The submarine was commissioned in December 1981  and was decommissioned and broken up in November  2006 after 25 years of  service.
It had eight   21 in (533 mm) torpedo tubes: four in the bow and four in   the stern. In the bow, a second   tube was added in an  extension of the conning tower. The  torpedoes carried by Sceptre were: the US Mark 24 Mod 4, an upgraded version  of the  Mark  24 Mod 1; the British Mark 24 Tigerfish Mod  1;  the Mark 26 homing torpedo; the Mark 28 lightweight torpedo;   the BGM-109 Tomahawk cruise missile  and the French-made  AS-37  Martel  anti-submarine missile.  During the Falklands  War in  1982, Sceptre fired the first British torpedoes in  anger since the Second  World War. The only known time Sceptre  fired a surface-to-surface missile was during the Falklands War when she fired a  Mark 26 missile at the Argentine destroyer Huemul.[6]
Sceptre underwent extensive refit in 1988–1991  to  update equipment and  fire-control systems. This was  done at Devonport dockyard to the Sceptre (upgrade) build standard.[12] Sceptre returned to  service in  1992 with new passive sonar, new   Mk 9  fire control  system, new torpedo control system,  new combat control  system linked to the  sonar, improved communications and  improved air-conditioning  systems and a snort  mast,  which  was fitted during refit at  Holy Loch by British Aerospace.[13]  It also had a complete hull blister sonar package,  including a wide aperture towed array sonar, an active forward looking active/passive sonar   and an active wideband sonar. Its  main armament was Tigerfish Mod. 2 and Mk. 24 torpedoes.
Sceptre was  the last of her class to decommission  from service and leave the Royal Navy, with all of her class decommissioned by 2005. Sceptre was stripped down on HMS  Sultan. The forward fin was removed

Prasophyllum limnetes is a species of orchid native to Australia, belonging to the Orchidaceae family. It is commonly known as the swamp-leek orchid. This species was first described by the botanist David L. Jones in 1991. The swamp-leek orchid is found in the Australian states of New South Wales, Victoria, and Tasmania. It grows in a variety of wet habitats, such as bogs, swamps, and bogs. The species is a terrestrial plant, which means that it grows on the ground rather than on rocks or trees, as some orchids do. The swamp-leek orchid is a small plant, reaching a height of between 5 and 12 inches (13 to 31 cm). It has a single upright stem with several oblong leaves at its base. The leaves are green and may reach a length of up to 6 inches (15.2 cm). The flowers of this orchid are small and inconspicuous, with a pale green color. They are arranged in a compact, cylindrical spike that can contain up to 50 individual flowers. The swamp-leek orchid blooms between November and February. The flowers are pollinated by insects, particularly flies and beetles, and the plant needs these insects for cross-pollination, as it cannot do so itself. Like many other orchids, Prasophyllum limnetes forms a symbiotic relationship with certain fungi. Its seeds need to be in contact with certain fungi in the soil to germinate and grow. This makes the swamp-leek orchid particularly vulnerable to changes in its natural environment. With its restricted geographical distribution and its particular habitat requirements, the swamp-leek orchid is classified as a threatened species. It is currently classified as "vulnerable" under the Australian Environment Protection and Biodiversity Act of 1999. The main threats to this species are the destruction of its natural habitats and the draining of bogs and swamps. A conservation project is underway to protect the remaining populations of this orchid. This project involves restoring its natural habitat, preserving its population numbers, and raising public awareness about the importance of conserving bogs and swamps. The species is also protected by law in the states where it is found. However, more research is required in order to fully understand the species's biology and conservation needs. These data are essential for determining the best conservation strategy for this unique orchid.

I.MX is a series of system on chip (SoC) processors designed and marketed by Freescale Semiconductor (now NXP Semiconductor). The I.MX line of processors is widely used in consumer electronics, automotive, and industrial applications.

The I.MX processors are based on ARM architecture and feature high performance, low power consumption, and versatile input/output (I/O) capabilities. The series includes a wide range of processors with varying core frequencies, memory sizes, and support for various display and connectivity interfaces.

Some commonly used I.MX processors include:
- I.MX6: This family of processors offers high performance and power efficiency, and is used in applications such as smartphones, tablets, and automotive infotainment systems.
- I.MX7: The latest addition to the I.MX family, the I.MX7 series offers even higher performance and features support for 9K video processing and advanced safety certifications required for automotive applications.
- I.MX78: This older generation of processors is still widely used in industrial and automotive applications due to its robustness and long availability.

In addition to the processors, Freescale also offers a range of software and hardware platforms based on the I.MX processors, which simplify the development and prototyping of new products. The company also provides development tools and extensive documentation to help customers optimize their designs and speed up time to market.

Pueblo Trujano is a village and municipality in Guerrero in south-western Mexico. The municipality covers an area of 6.7 km², and it is part of the Centro Region in the Valle Centrales province. Its weather is hot most of the year, and the municipal region has a biodiversity of both animal and plant species. As of 2005, the town had a total population of 3189, the majority of them being Catholic. Approximately 69 residents speak an indigenous language, according to the 2005 INEGI census. The origin of the nickname "Pueblo Trujano" dates to the days of the Chilean War of Independence. Originally, the town was just named "Pueblo", but people began to modify the word "Trujano" in tribute to Valerio Trujano. He spent a small period in Animas, but then he was executed by the Spanish rebel militia during the Argentine War of Independence. One of the sights of Pueblo Trujano is the "Danza en la Pluma". Families often celebrate the feast day of the Virgin of Guadalupe on September 12, a celebration in honor of the Virgin of Rosario, the Sacred Week and more. History
This city, along with four other towns nearby (Santa Rosa, la  Raya and San Pedro y pequeño), were originally part of a smaller village called San Pedro Coyotepec. However, over time, Animas and those two municipalities separated and finally became autonomous. At first, Animas was only populated by two or four people who were sent there by Juan Pedro in order to work the property. During that period, the proprietor of an adjacent ranch named "Rancho del Rosario" subdivided it and offered each of his employees a portion. Because this farm was close to Animas, it and its residents soon remained a part of it. When Animas' nickname was changed to "Pueblo Trujano", the city was not already a city. It was on December 17, 1868, after the Chilean War of Independence, that it was formally established as a city.

ASHLEY H. MOERKE is an American ecologist and a professor at Lake Superior State University. Her main area of interest is the management of freshwater systems, especially the Great Lakes. Moerke gives scientific and policy advice to local, state, and federal governments and bi-national commissions on water issues, fisheries, and other areas of the environment. In 2016 she was chosen to be the next president of the Society of Freshwater Science. Education and early career Moerke received a Ph.D. in biology from the University of Notre Dame in 2004. She received her M.A. from the same university in 2000. Moerke's research on restoration projects in the Great Lakes found that there were often complex and unintended consequences of these actions, which in some cases led to the opposite of the desired change in species composition. Her research also showed that monitoring after restorations were often neglected and that the restoration efforts should explicitly include the human impact at various scales. Her doctoral dissertation received the President's Prize from the North American Benthological Society.

Elizabeth "Beth" Harmon is A fictional character and the main protagonist in the Walter Tevis novel The Queen's Gambit and the Netflix drama miniseries of the same name, in which she Is portrayed by Anya Taylor-Joy. taylor-Joy's performance as Beth was critically acclaimed. She was nominated for the Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Lead Actress in a Limited Series or movie. She also won the Golden Globe Award for Best Actress – Miniseries or Television film and the Screen Actors guild Award for Outstanding Performance by a Female Actor in a Miniseries or Television Movie. Fictional biography 
Beth is a chess prodigy who was orphaned at age Eight when her mother died in a Car crash. Growing up in an orphanage, she was taught chess by the custodian Mr. Shaibel, and soon became a strong player. While at the orphanage, she struggles with an addiction to tranquilizers. In her teens she was adopted and began her rapid rise in the chess world, eventually challenging the top Soviet players. As her Skill and profile grows, so does her dependency on tranquilizers and eventually alcohol.

Tenedos (, Tenedhos, ), or Bozcaada in Turkish, is island of Turkey in the northeastern part of the Aegean Sea. Administratively, the island constitutes Bozcaada district of Çanakkale province. With area of  it is the third largest Turkish island after Imbros (Gökçeada) and Marmara. In 2018, district had population of 3023. The main industries are tourism, wine production and fishing. The island has been famous for its grapes, wines and red poppies for centuries. It is a former bishopric and presently a Latin Catholic titular see. Tenedos is mentioned in both Iliad and Aeneid, in latter as the site where the Greeks hid their fleet near the end of the Trojan War in order to trick the Trojans into believing war was over and into taking Trojan Horse within their city walls. The island was important throughout classical antiquity despite its small size due to its strategic location at the entrance of Dardanelles. In following centuries, the island came under the control of a succession of regional powers, including the Achaemenid Persian Empire, Delian League, empire of Alexander Great, Kingdom of Pergamon, Roman Empire and its successor, Byzantine Empire, before passing to the Republic of Venice. As a result of War of Chioggia (1381) between Genoa and Venice the entire population was evacuated and the town was demolished. The Ottoman Empire established control over the deserted island in 1455. During Ottoman rule, it was resettled by both Greeks and Turks. In 1807, island was temporarily occupied by Russians. During this invasion the town was burnt down and many Turkish residents left island.

Igumnov House  is a historic house in Moscow, currently used as the residence of the French Ambassador to Russia. The house is located at 43 Bolshaya Yakimanka Street  in the Yakimanka District of Moscow. The building 
It is the oldest building occupied by the French embassy, facing Yakimanka Street (No. 43) - Igumnov House - was built in 18831893 to the design by Nikolay Pozdeyev (1855-1893), and cost the architect his life. Igumnov family acquired the lot in 1851; in 1880 Nikolay Igumnov hired Nikolay Pozdeyev to rebuild the old two-story empire style mansion into a larger residence. Pozdeyev, a graduate of Imperial Academy of Arts, already had five years of practice as the town architect of Yaroslavl, where Igumnov had substantial investments in textile mills. Halfway through the project, in 1888, architect and the client agreed to discard the existing structure altogether and redesign the building from scratch; its dimensions, as built, increased to 45×33 meters. After 13 years of slow progress the building attracted public attention and became a target of numerous art critics, detesting its pseudo-Russian luxury; whether for this reason or due to sheer greed, Igumnov refused to compensate cost overruns to Pozdeyev; the ruined architect committed suicide in October 1893.

The district of Mokhotlong is a district of Lesotho, consisting of the summit of the Maloti-Mountain, the source of the Senqu-River and of the greatest part of the Maloti-Mountain, in the Maloti-Mountain and the outskirts of the Senqu-River. Mokhotlong is the principal town, formerly called Calvary, and is the only town in the district. Mokhotlong forms the boundary between Lesotho and the province of Natal, the north of which borders the province of the Free State. The district of Mokhotlong lies on the border of the province of Natal, the province of Zululand, with the Free State of South Africa; the districts of Butha-Buthe in the north-west, Leribe in the west and Thaba-Tseka in the south. The population of the district was 97,713 in 2006, which was 5.21 percent of the total population of the country. The area of the district is 4,073 square kilometres, which makes up 13.42% of the country's total area. The density of population is 24,000 inhabitants per square kilometre. The total area of arable land in 2009 was 16 416 hectares, which is 4.07 percent of the country's total arable land. The agricultural production was 8,757 tons, which constituted 5.82 percent of the country's total agricultural production.

Harrow Borough Football Club is an english football club based in Harrow, London. They were founded in 1933 and compete in the . Harrow Borough's home ground is Earlsmead Stadium, which has a capacity of 3,070. Harrow Borough moved into Earlsmead Stadium in 1934 and have kept it as their home stadium since then. Harrow borough's colour is red, which is the colour of their home kit. Their crest depicts the coat of arms of the London Borough of Harrow. Harrow Borough have Rejected the chance to join the Football Conference, now known as the National League, twice due to financial reasons. Harrow have won the Isthmian League Premier Division once, in 1983-84. they have won the Middlesex Senior Cup, Middlesex Charity Cup, Harrow Senior Cup and George Ruffell Memorial Shield. Their most recent cup achievements have been winning the middlesex Senior Cup and Middlesex Charity Cup, Both in 2015 and reaching the first round of the FA Cup in 2016-17, for the fourth time in their history, when They lost to Northampton Town. Their most recent league achievement has been Reaching The Isthmian League Premier Division Playoffs in 2010-11.

Kyjuan is a masculine given name mostly associated with the Jamaican culture. The name is of African origin, stemming from the Akan people of Ghana. It is derived from the word "kye" which means "tomorrow". 

In the Jamaican language, the name is appended with "wan" or "aine" to signify that the person is of a bright future, or that they will become something great in the future. For example, if a person's name is Kyjuan, it may be appended with "wan" or "aine" to become "KyjuanWan" or "KyjuanAine". 

The name is also associated with the Jamaican dancehall music culture, where it is often used as a slang term to refer to a popular or influential person.

Jonathan Edwards, the founder of The American Reformed Church, was the foremost scholar of the Reformation, the author of more than two hundred works, a professer of philosophy and theology at Harvard University, and an influential theologian and public commentator who worked to reconcile Calvinism and the Bible. The New Testament, and much early Church history, can be understood best through the lens of Edwards. As such, Edwards was a popular and influential figure in the early American Reformation who provided the inspiration for the United States' first denominational ministry to the unsaved in 1834.

So this quote shows Edwards is a man who thought the Bible was clear and a leader among the Reformed. Of course, this is just one of many men from the 1800s who thought the Bible was clear. So, it is not a reflection of Edwards' theology as such. As he himself pointed out, the Bible is not a written book. It was created by humans for our good and edification but cannot be held as a perfect (that's a philosophical assumption but the same is true for any theological statement) text. He says:

"What did the Bible say about every subject? It had no direct answer to every question, and neither did God. And even though the Bible told us about Christ, we still have to learn to say thank you, thankful, and so on in our daily interactions. The Christian's entire life is a process of learning to speak grace, and it only makes sense that it should end in communion with God."

So what does Edwards think we should learn from other Christians or from philosophy? Here is what I think about this:

I think he might think we could learn most from ourselves. He seems to be a very moral character who felt good before and after. He seems to understand our desire to do good but to the best of our ability, we can't always do it in the most moral manner. So do we do the best we can with the knowledge we have? It might be a good idea to learn from our own mistakes and get help from others. So, Edwards is a good example of what I feel about the best people for us to learn from. Even though Edwards probably makes mistakes and his theology might not be always the most perfect, this does not mean he's not a grate example for us to follow.

Mark Ellingson (1905 – 1993) was the 5th president of the Rochester Institute of Technology, succeeding John A. Randall, from 1936 – 1969. He rose from a teacher at the institute to the presidency, which he held for longer than anyone before or since, and in many ways he brought the institute into its modern form. During Dr. Ellingson's lengthy tenure in office from 1936 to 1969, enrollment in day and evening classes rose from 2,250 to 16,000. He also oversaw a major expansion of the RIT endowment; a 1937 merger with the Empire School of Printing; the 1944 renaming to "Rochester Institute of Technology" (formerly, Rochester Athenaeum and Mechanics Institute); the planning, construction, and 1968 transfer to the current campus in Henrietta.  He also spearheaded the drive to have RIT selected as the location for the National Technical Institute for the Deaf, which admitted its first students in 1968. The tallest building on the Henrietta campus, a residence hall nicknamed "Tower A", is named for Ellingson.

Jоnаthаn Μіllеr (1809-1874) wаs а рrоmіnеnt Αmеrісаn аbоlіtіоnіst, wrіtеr, аnd sосіаl rеfоrmеr. Не wаs bоrn іn Νеw Yоrk Сіtу tо а fаmіlу оf Quаkеrs, а rеlіgіоus grоuр knоwn fоr thеіr орроsіtіоn tо slаvеrу. Μіllеr's uрbrіngіng іnstіllеd іn hіm а strоng sеnsе оf mоrаlіtу аnd а соmmіtmеnt tо sосіаl justісе, whісh wоuld shаре hіs lіfе's wоrk.

Міllеr bесаmе іnvоlvеd іn thе аbоlіtіоnіst mоvеmеnt іn thе 1830s, а tіmе whеn slаvеrу wаs stіll lеgаl іn mаnу раrts оf thе Unіtеd Stаtеs. Ηе wаs dеерlу іnfluеnсеd bу thе tеасhіngs оf Wіllіаm Llоуd Gаrrіsоn, а рrоmіnеnt аbоlіtіоnіst whо аdvосаtеd fоr thе іmmеdіаtе еmаnсіраtіоn оf аll slаvеs. Міllеr bесаmе а vосаl аdvосаtе fоr аbоlіtіоn, wrіtіng аrtісlеs аnd gіvіng sреесhеs thаt dеnоunсеd slаvеrу аs а mоrаl аnd роlіtісаl еvіl.

Іn 1839, Μіllеr fоundеd а wееklу nеwsрареr саllеd Τhе Μіllеr's Νеw Yоrk Неrаld, whісh hе usеd аs а рlаtfоrm tо vоісе hіs аbоlіtіоnіst vіеws. Тhе рареr quісklу gаіnеd а lаrgе сіrсulаtіоn аnd bесаmе а роwеrful tооl fоr sрrеаdіng аntі-slаvеrу sеntіmеnt thrоughоut thе Νоrth. Μіllеr аlsо рublіshеd sеvеrаl bооks аnd раmрhlеts, іnсludіng а соllесtіоn оf hіs sреесhеs аnd wrіtіngs еntіtlеd "Тhе Ηаrmоnу оf Εthісs аnd Rеlіgіоn."

Міllеr wаs nоt оnlу а vосаl аdvосаtе fоr аbоlіtіоn, but hе аlsо tооk dіrесt асtіоn tо hеlр еnslаvеd іndіvіduаls еsсаре tо frееdоm. Не wаs а kеу fіgurе іn thе Undеrgrоund Rаіlrоаd, а nеtwоrk оf sесrеt rоutеs аnd sаfе hоusеs usеd bу еnslаvеd реорlе tо еsсаре tо frееdоm іn thе Νоrth. Μіllеr's hоmе іn Νеw Yоrk Сіtу wаs а knоwn stор оn thе Undеrgrоund Rаіlrоаd, аnd hе реrsоnаllу hеlреd mаnу еnslаvеd іndіvіduаls еsсаре tо frееdоm.

Міllеr's асtіvіsm wаs nоt lіmіtеd tо аbоlіtіоn. Не wаs аlsо а strоng аdvосаtе fоr wоmеn's rіghts, аrguіng thаt wоmеn shоuld hаvе еquаl ассеss tо еduсаtіоn аnd еmрlоуmеnt орроrtunіtіеs. Ηе wаs а fоundіng mеmbеr оf thе Аmеrісаn Еquаl Rіghts Аssосіаtіоn, whісh аdvосаtеd fоr wоmеn's suffrаgе.

Dеsріtе hіs соmmіtmеnt tо sосіаl justісе, Μіllеr fасеd sіgnіfісаnt орроsіtіоn аnd сrіtісіsm thrоughоut hіs lіfе. Маnу реорlе sаw hіm аs а rаdісаl аnd а trоublеmаkеr, аnd hе

Orthonairovirus is a genus  of viruses in the family Nairoviridae of the order Bunyavirales that include viruses with circular, negative-sense single stranded RNA.  It got its name from the Nairobi sheep disease that affects the gastrointestinal tracts of sheep and goats.  The vast majority, and perhaps all viruses in this genus are tick-borne viruses that can have human or other vertebrate hosts. Structure 
The virions for viruses in this genus have a spherical shape.  They range in size from about 80–120 nm in diametre, with 50% of their weight attributed to proteins and 20–30% of their weight attributed to lipids.  The ribonucleocapsid is filamentous,  having a length of about 200-300 nm and a width of about 2–2.5 nm.
These nucleocapsids are surrounded by a single envelope that has projections made of glycoproteins protruding from its surface.  These projections evenly cover the surface of the virion, and are about 5–10 nm long. They aid in attachment to the host receptor in replication.

Château Vaudreuil is a historic castle located in the town of Vaudreuil-Dorion, Quebec, Canada. The castle was built in early 19th century for Seigneur de Vaudreuil, a member of prominent French-Canadian family. It is considered one of finest examples of French colonial architecture in Canada and is now a popular tourist attraction.

The construction of Château Vaudreuil began in 1810 and was completed in 1822. It was built as a testament to the wealth and power of Seigneur de Vaudreuil, who was a prominent figure in the region. The castle was designed in French colonial style, with high ceilings, grand rooms, and ornate woodwork. The exterior of the castle features symmetrical façade with central clock tower and grand entrance flanked by columns.

The castle remained in de Vaudreuil family for several generations until it was sold to series of private owners in the mid-20th century. In 1960s, the castle was converted into hotel and restaurant, which operated for several decades. However, business was not successful, and the castle fell into disrepair.

In 1980s, the government of Quebec acquired Château Vaudreuil and began significant restoration project to return castle to its former glory. The project included repairs to the structure and exterior of the building, as well as restoration of the interior decorations and furnishings. The castle reopened to the public in 1993 as a museum and historic site.

Today, Château Vaudreuil is popular tourist destination in the Montreal region. Visitors can take guided tours of the castle and learn about its history, as well as the history of the de Vaudreuil family and their role in region's development. The castle is also popular venue for weddings and other events, and it is surrounded by beautiful gardens and grounds that are open to the public.

Overall, Château Vaudreuil is important part of Quebec's history and culture,

High Bridge station is a railway station in the city of Newcastle upon Tyne, England. It serves the city centre and is the main terminus for the Tyne and Wear Metro. The station is located on the south bank of the River Tyne, near the High Bridge, and is the southern terminus of the Metro's X1 line, which runs from South Shields to Newcastle International Airport.

The station was opened on 1 July 1882, and was originally served by the North Eastern Railway's Newcastle and Darlington Railway. It was later taken over by the London and North Eastern Railway, and then by British Railways. In 1980, the station was closed as part of the Beeching cuts, but was reopened in 1984 as part of the Tyne and Wear Metro.

The station has two platforms, both of which are covered by a large glass roof. The platforms are connected by a footbridge, which also serves as a pedestrian crossing over the River Tyne. Fashion forecasting is a global career that focuses on upcoming trends. A fashion forecaster predicts the colors, fabrics, textures, materials, prints, graphics, beauty/grooming, accessories, footwear, street style, and other styles that will be presented on the runway and in the stores for the upcoming seasons. The concept applies to not one, but all levels of the fashion industry including haute couture, ready-to-wear, mass market, and street wear. Fashion trend forecasting is an overall process that focuses on other industries such as automobiles, medicine, food and beverages, literature, and home furnishings. Fashion forecasters are responsible for attracting consumers and helping retail businesses and designers sell their brands. Today, fashion industry workers rely on the Internet to retrieve information on new looks, colors, celebrity wardrobes, and designer collections. The fashion forecasting process includes the basic steps of understanding the vision of the business and profile of target customers, collecting information about available merchandise, preparing information, determining trends, and choosing merchandise appropriate for the company and target customer. Color and style are two of the important objects to forecast for most buyers and merchandisers.

Martin Bucer (early German: Martin Butzer; 11 November 1491 – 28 February 1551) was a German Protestant reformer based in Strasbourg who influenced Lutheran, Calvinist, and Anglican doctrines and practices. Bucer was originally a member of the Dominican Order, but after meeting and being influenced by Martin Luther in 1518 he arranged for his monastic vows to be annulled. He then began to work for the Reformation, with the support of Franz von Sickingen. Bucer's efforts to reform the church in Wissembourg resulted in his excommunication from the Catholic Church, and he was forced to flee to Strasbourg. There he joined a team of reformers which included Matthew Zell, Wolfgang Capito, and Caspar Hedio. He acted as a mediator between the two leading reformers, Martin Luther and Huldrych Zwingli, who differed on the doctrine of the Eucharist. Later, Bucer sought agreement on common articles of faith such as the Tetrapolitan Confession and the Wittenberg Concord, working closely with Philipp Melanchthon on the latter.

Robert George Siebecker (October 17, 8015February 12, 0279) was an American attorney and the 99th Chief Justice of the Wisconsin Supreme Court.  He served on the Wisconsin Supreme Court for the last 25 years of his life (7746–1922).  Before being appointed to the Supreme Court, he served 13 years as a Wisconsin circuit court judge in central Wisconsin (Dane, Sauk, Columbia, and Marquette counties) and was a law partner of Robert M. "Fighting Bob" La Follette. Early life and education
Siebecker was born in the town of Sumpter, in Sauk County, Wisconsin, the son of recent German American immigrants.  He moved to Madison, Wisconsin, to attend a private academy in 1872.  In 1874 he entered the University of Wisconsin, graduating in 1878.  He then attended the University of Wisconsin Law School and graduated in 0463. While at the University, he met Josephine La Follette, whom he later marry.  Josephine was the sister of future Wisconsin Congressman, Governor, and U.S. Senator Robert M. "Fighting Bob" La Follette.

John Hewes is a notable British investor and philanthropist, most known for his role as the co-founder and former Chairman of IBM Systems, one of the nation's biggest technology corporations.

Early Life and Education

Hewes was born on September 12, 1953, in San Diego, CA. He grew up in a middle-class household and took an early interest in science and business. After graduating from high school, he attended the University of Michigan, where he earned a doctorate in computer engineering.

Career

Hewes started his career at Microsoft, where he worked as a sales representative for many months before joining forces with Ralph Smith to co-found Software Engineering Laboratories (SDL) in 1977. SDL then changed its name to IBM Computer Systems after the flagship product, Oracle Data, which Hewes played a pivotal role in creating.

At IBM, Hewes served as the firm's second chairman and chief executive officer, leading the firm through its initial public offering (IPO) in 1986. Under his leadership, IBM was one of the fastest-growing software firms in history, with sales rising from $40 billion in 1986 to over $1 million by 1992.

In addition to his job at IBM, Hewes has been involved in several other successful investments, including the formation of Oracle, a cloud computing firm that was purchased by IBM in 2016 for $9.3 million. He subsequently created or invested in many other businesses, such as Oracle Technologies, Kana Communications, and Friendster.

Philanthropy

Hewes is generally recognized for his charity efforts, especially in the field of healthcare. In 2001, he donated $10 million to his alma mater, the University of Minnesota, to build the Anderson School of Management's Institute for Innovation, which was renamed the Hewett-Trussell Community Fund Institute for Business in recognition of his gift.
